## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plume Rise (Δh):** The vertical distance that a buoyant stack plume travels **above the physical stack height** due to its initial **momentum (exit velocity)** and **buoyancy (density difference between hot stack gases and ambient air)** before it begins horizontal transport downwind.

The **Effective Stack Height (H_e)** is the sum of the physical stack height and the plume rise:

$$H_e = H_s + \Delta h$$

where $H_s$ = actual physical stack height (m) and $\Delta h$ = plume rise (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Examinations:** The effective stack height is the *governing parameter* used in the Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model. An incorrect plume rise calculation propagates errors into ground-level concentration predictions ($\chi$), which is a major design parameter for industries and regulatory clearance (e.g., KSPCB consent).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you blow out a candle flame on your birthday. The thin stream of hot smoke goes straight up — that is **pure buoyancy rise**. Now imagine you blow *harder* — the smoke has horizontal thrust but also shoots upward. That extra upward "boost" is **momentum rise**. A power plant chimney is a giant version of the same phenomenon.

**Another analogy — a hot air balloon:**
- Hot air is **less dense** than cold air (Archimedes' buoyancy principle).
- Stack gases at $400^\circ C$ are far lighter than the surrounding $30^\circ C$ air.
- They "want" to rise, exactly like a hot air balloon.
- The wind blowing across the stack top "tips" the rising plume at an angle, so it bends over and travels downwind at some elevated height — this is the **effective release height**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants (Recall-Ready):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Specific heat of air at constant pressure: $C_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)} = 0.24 \text{ cal/(g·K)}$
> - Density of ambient air (at $25^\circ C$, 1 atm): $\rho_a = 1.184 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level: $P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

**Primary Input Parameters Required for Every Plume-Rise Formula:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Stack gas exit velocity | $v_s$ | m/s |
| Stack diameter (or radius $r = d/2$) | $d$ | m |
| Stack gas temperature | $T_s$ | K |
| Ambient air temperature | $T_a$ | K |
| Wind speed at stack height | $u$ | m/s |
| Atmospheric pressure at stack top | $P$ | kPa |
| Heat emission rate (sometimes) | $Q_h$ | cal/s or W |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Plume Rise — Step-by-Step Logic

The plume rises because of two simultaneous effects:

**Step 1 — Momentum Phase (Near the stack):**
The exhaust gases leave the stack with a vertical exit velocity $v_s$. This initial momentum carries the plume upward a small distance.

**Step 2 — Buoyancy Phase (Dominant Region):**
The hot stack gases (at $T_s$) are surrounded by cooler ambient air (at $T_a$). Since $T_s > T_a$, the plume is less dense and experiences a net upward buoyancy force. The "buoyancy flux" parameter $F$ quantifies this:

$$F = g \cdot v_s \cdot r^2 \cdot \frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s} \quad \text{[m}^4\text{/s}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — Wind Tilting and Final Rise:**
The horizontal wind $u$ pushes the plume sideways, so the plume eventually becomes horizontal. The maximum height it reaches is the **final plume rise** $\Delta h$, which is the value we want.

**Step 4 — Atmospheric Stability Influence:**
- In **unstable/neutral** conditions: plume rises to its full buoyancy-driven height.
- In **stable** conditions (temperature inversion): vertical motion is *capped* by the inversion layer, so plume rise is suppressed.

### 2.2 The Four "Must-Know" KTU Plume Rise Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Indian power plant and refinery problems in KTU papers most frequently use **Holland**, **Briggs**, and **Moses–Carson** formulas. Memorize all three.

#### A) Holland's Formula (1945) — Most Frequently Asked in KTU

$$\Delta h = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \cdot \left[ 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{T_s} \cdot d \right]$$

- **Domain of validity:** $v_s > 10$ m/s (works best for momentum-rich stacks).
- The factor **1.5** accounts for the **momentum contribution**.
- The term involving $P$, $\Delta T$, and $d$ accounts for the **buoyancy contribution**.

#### B) Briggs's Formula (1969/1975) — Preferred for Buoyancy-Dominated Plumes

**Step (i) Compute the Buoyancy Flux $F$:**

$$F = g \cdot v_s \cdot r^2 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s}$$

**Step (ii) Compute the distance to final rise $x_f$:**

$$x_f = \begin{cases} 34 \cdot F^{2/5} & \text{if } F < 55 \text{ m}^4\text{/s}^3 \\ 38.71 \cdot F^{2/5} & \text{if } F \ge 55 \text{ m}^4\text{/s}^3 \end{cases}$$

**Step (iii) Compute the plume rise:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3}}{u}$$

#### C) Moses–Carson Formula (1968) — Uses Heat Emission Rate

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.5 \cdot v_s \cdot d + 0.04 \cdot Q_h}{u}$$

where $Q_h$ is the heat emission rate in **calories per second** (cal/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit conversion trap:** If $Q_h$ is given in **Watts (W)**, use $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.5 v_s d + 0.042 \, Q_h(\text{W})}{u}$ OR first convert $Q_h(\text{W}) = 0.239 \times Q_h(\text{cal/s})$ and use the original formula. The KTU board often gives $Q_h$ in cal/s for simplicity.

#### D) CONCAWE Formula (For Tall Stacks in Buoyant Conditions)

$$\Delta h = 1.9 \cdot \left[ \frac{F}{u^2} \right]^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3} \cdot \frac{1}{u^{1/3}}$$

(Generally not in the top 3 KTU problems but is a good extra-credit reference.)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pro Tip for Exams:** Keep this table ready on your revision card. Do **NOT** use the vertical pipe symbol in the cell entries to avoid markdown break.

| Formula | Key Equation | Best Suited For | Input Units | Critical Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Holland** | $\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s d}{u}\left[1.5 + 2.68\!\times\!10^{-3} P \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s} d\right]$ | High exit-velocity stacks | $v_s$ m/s, $d$ m, $u$ m/s, $P$ kPa, $T$ K | $v_s \ge 10$ m/s |
| **Briggs** | $F = g v_s r^2 \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s}$, then $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3}}{u}$ | Buoyancy-dominated plumes | Same SI units | Neutral/unstable atmosphere |
| **Moses–Carson** | $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.5 v_s d + 0.04 Q_h}{u}$ | When $Q_h$ is given | $Q_h$ in cal/s | $Q_h$ must be in cal/s |
| **CONCAWE** | $\Delta h \propto \left(F / u^2\right)^{1/3}$ | Tall stacks, buoyant flow | SI units | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants (Thermal):** Tata Power, NTPC stations use Briggs formula for chimney design to meet MOEF emission height regulations.
- **Petroleum Refineries (BPCL, IOCL):** Moses–Carson formula is used because flare-stack heat emission data is readily available.
- **Cement & Steel Industries (KSPCB consent):** Holland's formula is used for momentum-rich kilns.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Plume rise is essential for predicting maximum ground-level concentration (GLC) using the Gaussian Plume equation:

$$\chi(x, y, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{H_e^2}{2 \sigma_z^2} \right)$$

A 10 m error in $H_e$ can shift the predicted peak GLC by a factor of 2 or more at the design downwind distance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Numerical Solutions

### Problem 1: Holland's Formula — Standard Industrial Stack

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> A coal-fired power plant has the following stack data:
> - Diameter, $d = 3 \text{ m}$
> - Exit velocity, $v_s = 20 \text{ m/s}$
> - Wind speed at stack height, $u = 6 \text{ m/s}$
> - Stack gas temperature, $T_s = 450 \text{ K}$
> - Ambient air temperature, $T_a = 300 \text{ K}$
> - Atmospheric pressure, $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
>
> **Determine the plume rise using Holland's formula and the effective stack height if the actual physical height is 120 m.**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — State Holland's formula explicitly:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \cdot \left[ 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{T_s} \cdot d \right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 450 - 300 = 150 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the dimensionless temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s} = \frac{150}{450} = 0.3333$$

**Step 4 — Compute the momentum term $v_s d / u$:**

$$\frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} = \frac{20 \times 3}{6} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the buoyancy coefficient inside the bracket:**

$$2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} \cdot d = 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.325 \times 0.3333 \times 3$$

$$= 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.325 = 0.2715$$

$$0.2715 \times 0.3333 = 0.0905$$

$$0.0905 \times 3 = 0.2716$$

**Step 6 — Add 1.5 to the buoyancy coefficient:**

$$1.5 + 0.2716 = 1.7716$$

**Step 7 — Compute the plume rise:**

$$\Delta h = 10 \times 1.7716 = 17.72 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the effective stack height:**

$$H_e = H_s + \Delta h = 120 + 17.72 = 137.72 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta h = 17.72 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad H_e = 137.72 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Problem 2: Briggs's Formula — Buoyancy-Dominated Plume

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Pattern]**
> A refinery stack releases hot flue gas with the following characteristics:
> - Exit velocity, $v_s = 15 \text{ m/s}$
> - Stack diameter, $d = 2 \text{ m}$ (radius $r = 1$ m)
> - Stack gas temperature, $T_s = 423 \text{ K}$
> - Ambient temperature, $T_a = 288 \text{ K}$
> - Wind speed, $u = 4 \text{ m/s}$
> - Atmospheric stability: **neutral**
>
> **Calculate the plume rise using Briggs's formula.**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 423 - 288 = 135 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the buoyancy flux $F$:**

$$F = g \cdot v_s \cdot r^2 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 15 \times (1)^2 \times \frac{135}{423}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 15 \times 0.3192 = 9.81 \times 4.788 = 46.97 \text{ m}^4\text{/s}^3$$

**Step 3 — Select the correct $x_f$ formula:**

Since $F = 46.97 < 55$, we use:

$$x_f = 34 \cdot F^{2/5}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $F^{2/5}$:**

$$F^{2/5} = (46.97)^{0.4}$$

Using logarithms: $\log_{10}(46.97) = 1.6717$

$$\frac{2}{5} \times 1.6717 = 0.6687$$

$$10^{0.6687} = 4.665$$

$$\therefore F^{2/5} = 4.665$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final rise distance:**

$$x_f = 34 \times 4.665 = 158.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the plume rise:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3}}{u}$$

Compute $F^{1/3}$:

$$F^{1/3} = (46.97)^{1/3}$$

$$\log_{10}(46.97) = 1.6717$$

$$\frac{1}{3} \times 1.6717 = 0.5572$$

$$10^{0.5572} = 3.612$$

Compute $x_f^{2/3}$:

$$x_f^{2/3} = (158.6)^{2/3}$$

$$\log_{10}(158.6) = 2.2002$$

$$\frac{2}{3} \times 2.2002 = 1.4668$$

$$10^{1.4668} = 29.32$$

**Step 7 — Substitute all values:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \times 3.612 \times 29.32}{4} = \frac{169.41}{4} = 42.35 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta h \approx 42.35 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Problem 3: Moses–Carson Formula — Using Heat Emission Rate

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2022, Repeated Pattern]**
> A 1.5 m diameter stack emits hot gases with:
> - Exit velocity, $v_s = 12 \text{ m/s}$
> - Heat emission rate, $Q_h = 8000 \text{ cal/s}$
> - Wind speed, $u = 5 \text{ m/s}$
>
> **Estimate the plume rise by Moses–Carson formula.**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — State Moses–Carson equation:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.5 \cdot v_s \cdot d + 0.04 \cdot Q_h}{u}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the momentum term:**

$$1.5 \cdot v_s \cdot d = 1.5 \times 12 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the buoyancy (heat) term:**

$$0.04 \cdot Q_h = 0.04 \times 8000 = 320 \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the numerator:**

$$27 + 320 = 347 \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by wind speed $u$:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{347}{5} = 69.4 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta h = 69.4 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Problem 4: Comparative Numerical Problem (Frequently Asked 14-Mark KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**
> A 200 m tall industrial stack has the following data:
> - Diameter, $d = 4 \text{ m}$
> - Exit velocity, $v_s = 18 \text{ m/s}$
> - Stack gas temperature, $T_s = 500 \text{ K}$
> - Ambient temperature, $T_a = 293 \text{ K}$
> - Wind speed, $u = 5 \text{ m/s}$
> - Atmospheric pressure, $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
> - Heat emission rate, $Q_h = 12000 \text{ cal/s}$
>
> **Compute the plume rise by Holland, Briggs, and Moses–Carson formulas. Comment on the differences and identify the most suitable method for this case.**

#### Part (a): Holland's Formula Solution

**Step 1 — Compute temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta T}{T_s} = \frac{500 - 293}{500} = \frac{207}{500} = 0.414$$

**Step 2 — Compute momentum term:**

$$\frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} = \frac{18 \times 4}{5} = 14.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute buoyancy term inside bracket:**

$$2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.325 \times 0.414 \times 4 = 0.4496$$

**Step 4 — Add to momentum base of 1.5:**

$$1.5 + 0.4496 = 1.9496$$

**Step 5 — Final plume rise (Holland):**

$$\Delta h_{Holland} = 14.4 \times 1.9496 = 28.07 \text{ m}$$

#### Part (b): Briggs's Formula Solution

**Step 1 — Buoyancy flux $F$:**

$$F = 9.81 \times 18 \times 2^2 \times 0.414 = 9.81 \times 18 \times 4 \times 0.414 = 292.45 \text{ m}^4\text{/s}^3$$

**Step 2 — Since $F = 292.45 > 55$, use the high-F formula:**

$$x_f = 38.71 \cdot F^{2/5} = 38.71 \times (292.45)^{0.4}$$

$$\log_{10}(292.45) = 2.4662$$

$$0.4 \times 2.4662 = 0.9865$$

$$10^{0.9865} = 9.691$$

$$x_f = 38.71 \times 9.691 = 375.13 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute plume rise:**

$$F^{1/3} = (292.45)^{1/3} \Rightarrow 10^{2.4662/3} = 10^{0.8221} = 6.640$$

$$x_f^{2/3} = (375.13)^{2/3} \Rightarrow 10^{(2.5742)(2/3)} = 10^{1.7161} = 52.00$$

$$\Delta h_{Briggs} = \frac{1.6 \times 6.640 \times 52.00}{5} = \frac{552.45}{5} = 110.49 \text{ m}$$

#### Part (c): Moses–Carson Formula Solution

**Step 1 — Momentum term:**

$$1.5 \times v_s \times d = 1.5 \times 18 \times 4 = 108$$

**Step 2 — Heat term:**

$$0.04 \times Q_h = 0.04 \times 12000 = 480$$

**Step 3 — Sum and divide:**

$$\Delta h_{M-C} = \frac{108 + 480}{5} = \frac{588}{5} = 117.6 \text{ m}$$

#### Comparative Summary Table

| Formula | Plume Rise $\Delta h$ (m) | Recommendation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Holland | $28.07$ | Underestimate — assumes $v_s$-momentum dominance, but here buoyancy is huge. |
| Briggs | $110.49$ | Best — explicitly accounts for high buoyancy flux. |
| Moses–Carson | $117.6$ | Good — directly uses $Q_h$; aligns with Briggs within 6%. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Comment:** Since $T_s = 500$ K is very high (buoyancy-dominated flow with $v_s = 18$ m/s, which is moderate), Briggs and Moses–Carson give realistic values, while Holland underestimates. This is a classic KTU "interpret the result" question worth 2–3 marks in Part (b).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plume Rise — Physical Block-Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **functional architecture of plume rise physics** (a schematic equivalent to a physical stack-plume diagram, since true physical drawings are difficult in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stack Base H sub s] --> B[Hot Gas Exit at Temp T sub s]
    B --> C[Exit Velocity v sub s]
    B --> D[Temperature Difference delta T]
    C --> E[Momentum Rise Phase]
    D --> F[Buoyancy Rise Phase]
    E --> G[Effective Stack Height H sub e]
    F --> G
    H[Ambient Wind Speed u] --> E
    H --> F
    I[Atmospheric Pressure P] --> F
    J[Atmospheric Stability] --> F
    G --> K[Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model]
    K --> L[Ground Level Concentration chi]
```

### 4.2 Algorithmic Flow Chart — Choosing the Right Plume-Rise Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Given Stack Parameters] --> S1[Identify d, v sub s, T sub s, T sub a, u, P]
    S1 --> S2{Is Q sub h given?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use Moses Carson Formula]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is v sub s greater than 10 m per s?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Buoyancy Flux F significant?]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Use Briggs Formula - buoyancy dominated]
    S5 -- Yes, F less than 55 --> S7[Briggs: x sub f = 34 F to power 2 over 5]
    S5 -- No, F greater than or equal 55 --> S8[Briggs: x sub f = 38.71 F to power 2 over 5]
    S7 --> S9[Compute delta h = 1.6 F to power 1 over 3 x sub f to power 2 over 3 divided by u]
    S8 --> S9
    S3 --> S10[Compute delta h = 1.5 v sub s d plus 0.04 Q sub h divided by u]
    S6 --> S9
    S9 --> S11[Effective Stack Height H sub e = H sub s plus delta h]
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[Use H sub e in Gaussian Plume Model]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Three Formulas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stack Inputs d, v sub s, T sub s, T sub a, u, P, Q sub h] --> B1[Holland 1945]
    A --> B2[Briggs 1969]
    A --> B3[Moses Carson 1968]
    B1 --> C1[delta h sub H]
    B2 --> C2[delta h sub B]
    B3 --> C3[delta h sub MC]
    C1 --> D[Effective Stack Height H sub e]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Gaussian Dispersion chi x y z]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each `sub` in node labels represents a subscript in the diagram for visual clarity. Mermaid does not natively render underscores in node labels, so we use the explicit text form for evaluator clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define plume rise. Why is it added to the physical stack height in dispersion modeling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Plume rise (Δh)** is the vertical distance traveled by a buoyant stack plume above the physical stack mouth before it bends over horizontally due to wind. It is added to the physical stack height $H_s$ to obtain the **effective stack height** $H_e = H_s + \Delta h$ because pollutant dispersion in the Gaussian Plume Model depends on the *release height*, not the chimney height alone. A higher effective release height reduces the **maximum ground-level concentration (GLC)** by increasing the vertical distance over which the plume must diffuse downward.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set B]**
*List the factors that influence plume rise. Distinguish between momentum-dominated and buoyancy-dominated plumes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Factors influencing plume rise:** (1) Exit velocity $v_s$ (momentum), (2) Stack gas temperature $T_s$ (buoyancy), (3) Ambient temperature $T_a$ (density contrast), (4) Wind speed $u$ (tilting), (5) Stack diameter $d$ (cross-section), (6) Atmospheric stability (inversion caps), (7) Atmospheric pressure $P$.
>
> **Momentum-dominated plumes:** occur when $v_s$ is very high ($> 20$ m/s) and the temperature difference is small. The Holland formula is best suited.
>
> **Buoyancy-dominated plumes:** occur when the temperature difference is large (e.g., $T_s \ge 450$ K) and exit velocity is moderate. Briggs or Moses–Carson formulas are best suited.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Plume Rise using Holland + Moses–Carson Comparison*

**(a)** A thermal power plant has the following stack data: $d = 2.5$ m, $v_s = 22$ m/s, $T_s = 430$ K, $T_a = 303$ K, $u = 7$ m/s, $P = 101.325$ kPa. Compute the plume rise using **Holland's formula**. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the same plant, the heat emission rate is $Q_h = 6000$ cal/s. Compute the plume rise using **Moses–Carson formula** and compare with the Holland result. (7 marks)

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** $\dfrac{v_s d}{u} = \dfrac{22 \times 2.5}{7} = \dfrac{55}{7} = 7.857 \text{ m}$

**Step 2:** $\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s} = \dfrac{430 - 303}{430} = \dfrac{127}{430} = 0.2953$

**Step 3:** Bracket term $= 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.325 \times 0.2953 \times 2.5$

$$= 1.5 + 0.2716 \times 0.7383 = 1.5 + 0.2005 = 1.7005$$

**Step 4:** $\Delta h_{Holland} = 7.857 \times 1.7005 = \mathbf{13.36 \text{ m}}$

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Momentum term $= 1.5 \times 22 \times 2.5 = 82.5$

**Step 2:** Heat term $= 0.04 \times 6000 = 240$

**Step 3:** $\Delta h_{M-C} = \dfrac{82.5 + 240}{7} = \dfrac{322.5}{7} = \mathbf{46.07 \text{ m}}$

**Comparison (3 marks):**

> Moses–Carson gives 3.45 times higher plume rise because the heat emission is large (buoyancy contribution dominates). The two formulas will not match — Moses–Carson is more accurate for this case. Effective stack heights: $H_{e, Holland} = H_s + 13.36$ vs $H_{e, MC} = H_s + 46.07$.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Briggs + Stack Height Design*

**(a)** An industrial stack has $v_s = 12$ m/s, $d = 3$ m, $T_s = 410$ K, $T_a = 290$ K, $u = 4$ m/s. Compute the plume rise using **Briggs's formula**. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the regulatory authority requires the effective stack height to be 100 m, what should be the physical stack height? (7 marks)

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** $r = d/2 = 1.5$ m; $\Delta T = 120$ K; $\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s} = \dfrac{120}{410} = 0.2927$

**Step 2:** $F = 9.81 \times 12 \times (1.5)^2 \times 0.2927 = 9.81 \times 12 \times 2.25 \times 0.2927 = 77.55 \text{ m}^4\text{/s}^3$

**Step 3:** Since $F = 77.55 \ge 55$, use $x_f = 38.71 \cdot F^{2/5}$

$$\log_{10}(77.55) = 1.8896 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.4 \times 1.8896 = 0.7558$$

$$10^{0.7558} = 5.698 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_f = 38.71 \times 5.698 = 220.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** $F^{1/3} = (77.55)^{1/3} = 10^{1.8896/3} = 10^{0.6299} = 4.265$

$$x_f^{2/3} = (220.55)^{2/3} = 10^{(2.3435)(2/3)} = 10^{1.5623} = 36.49$$

**Step 5:** $\Delta h_{Briggs} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 4.265 \times 36.49}{4} = \dfrac{249.04}{4} = \mathbf{62.26 \text{ m}}$

**Solution (b):**

$$H_e = H_s + \Delta h \Rightarrow H_s = H_e - \Delta h = 100 - 62.26 = \mathbf{37.74 \text{ m}}$$

The physical stack must be at least **37.74 m** tall to meet the regulatory requirement.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Plume-Rise Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Moses–Carson:** If $Q_h$ is given in **Watts (W)**, multiplying by 0.04 directly is **wrong**. First convert $Q_h(\text{W}) / 4.184 = Q_h(\text{cal/s})$, OR use the W-formula with constant 0.042. KTU papers commonly use cal/s, but always check.
>
> 2. **Briggs – Wrong $x_f$ Branch:** Forgetting to check whether $F < 55$ or $F \ge 55$ before choosing the constant 34 or 38.71. This is a 2-mark deduction trap.
>
> 3. **Temperature in Kelvin:** $T_s$ and $T_a$ must be in **Kelvin**, NOT Celsius. Forgetting to add 273.15 is a common 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Holland's Pressure Unit:** $P$ must be in **kPa** (not Pa, not bar). If $P$ is given in mbar or mmHg, convert first. $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1013.25 \text{ mbar} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$.
>
> 5. **Effective Stack Height (Bonus 1 mark):** If the question says "the actual physical stack height is X m", you MUST compute $H_e$ to get the full marks. Many students stop at $\Delta h$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — KTU Module 2 Plume Rise**

- [x] **Plume rise (Δh)** = the vertical lift of the plume above the stack top due to momentum + buoyancy.
- [x] **Effective stack height** $H_e = H_s + \Delta h$ — this is the input to the Gaussian Plume Model.
- [x] **Holland's formula** uses 4 inputs: $v_s, d, u, T_s, T_a, P$. Best for $v_s > 10$ m/s. Has a 1.5 momentum baseline.
- [x] **Briggs's formula** uses the **buoyancy flux** $F = g v_s r^2 \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s}$. Best for hot stacks. Two-branch $x_f$ depending on whether $F < 55$ or $F \ge 55$.
- [x] **Moses–Carson** needs the **heat emission rate** $Q_h$ in cal/s. Formula: $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.5 v_s d + 0.04 Q_h}{u}$.
- [x] **Physical constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_p = 0.24$ cal/(g·K), $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa.
- [x] **Temperature ratio** $\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s}$ is **always dimensionless** and is the heart of the buoyancy effect.
- [x] **Wind speed $u$ in the denominator** — higher wind = lower plume rise (the plume is "pushed over" sooner).
- [x] **Atmospheric stability** modulates the result — Briggs's neutral formula is the most commonly tested scenario.
- [x] **The Gaussian Plume Model** uses $H_e$ — an error in plume rise propagates into the predicted $\chi$ (ground-level concentration).
- [x] **Common pitfall:** $T$ must be in Kelvin; $P$ must be in kPa; $Q_h$ must be in cal/s — check the units **first** before plugging in.
- [x] **Holland underestimates** when buoyancy dominates; **Moses–Carson overestimates** when heat emission is very high; **Briggs** is the most physically consistent choice.
- [x] **Comparison questions** (e.g., "comment on the difference") are typically worth 2–3 marks in Part (b). Always write 1–2 lines of engineering comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Particle Interaction - Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Particle Interaction - Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas-Particle Interaction** is the fundamental working principle in air pollution control that describes the physical mechanisms by which suspended particulate matter (aerosols) entrained in a carrier gas stream is forced to deviate from the bulk gas flow and make contact with a collecting surface, liquid droplet, or fibrous medium, thereby achieving separation from the gas phase.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Air Pollution Control – OECHT726), this principle is the foundational engineering concept that governs the design and operation of all **particulate control devices**, including fabric filters (baghouses), cyclones, wet scrubbers, electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), and High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The Board Examiner expects students to clearly articulate that gas-particle interaction is *not* a single phenomenon but a *combination of six independent physical mechanisms*, each dominant in a specific particle size range. A "one-line" answer will fetch only 2 of 7 marks; mechanism-by-mechanism explanation is mandatory for full credit.

## 1.2 The Six Fundamental Mechanisms

| # | Mechanism | Dominant Particle Size |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Inertial Impaction | > 1 μm |
| 2 | Direct Interception | 0.1 – 1 μm |
| 3 | Brownian Diffusion | < 0.1 μm |
| 4 | Gravitational Settling | > 10 μm |
| 5 | Electrostatic Attraction | All sizes (if charged) |
| 6 | Thermal Forces (Thermophoresis) | < 0.01 μm |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The River and the Rock"

Imagine a fast-flowing river (the **gas stream**) carrying leaves, twigs, and pebbles (the **particles**). When the river encounters a large rock (the **collector surface**), the water smoothly curves around the obstacle, but:

- **Heavy pebbles** (large/dense particles) cannot make the sharp turn — they crash into the rock. This is **Inertial Impaction**.
- **Twigs** (medium particles) follow the water's curve but their length causes them to "graze" the rock's surface. This is **Direct Interception**.
- **Tiny pollen grains** (submicron particles) wander randomly — a phenomenon called **Brownian Diffusion** — and statistically bump into the rock even in still water.
- **Floating leaves** (light, large particles) slowly settle to the riverbed by gravity, regardless of flow direction. This is **Gravitational Settling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A particle is collected whenever the *net external force* (inertia, gravity, electric field, thermal gradient, or random molecular bombardment) overcomes the aerodynamic drag trying to keep the particle entrained in the gas streamline.

## 1.4 Why This Principle Matters in Air Pollution Control

The U.S. EPA and the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB, India) regulate **Particulate Matter (PM)** in three legally defined size fractions:
- **PM₁₀** (diameter ≤ 10 μm) — thoracic particles
- **PM₂.₅** (diameter ≤ 2.5 μm) — respirable fine particles
- **Ultrafine PM** (diameter < 0.1 μm)

Each fraction must be captured using the mechanism that is most efficient at that size — which is exactly why a deep understanding of gas-particle interaction is essential for any control device selection.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism 1 — Inertial Impaction (The Workhorse)

**Working Principle:** When a particle-laden gas stream changes direction sharply around a collector (e.g., a fiber, droplet, or cyclone wall), the gas molecules curve smoothly along the streamline, but the particle — because of its finite mass — tends to continue in a straight line. If the particle's trajectory intersects the collector, capture occurs.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- A particle has **momentum** = $m_p \cdot U_0 = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p U_0$
- The drag force acts to pull the particle along the curved streamline.
- If inertia wins, the particle impacts the collector.
- Efficiency increases with **particle density (ρₚ)**, **particle diameter (dₚ)**, and **approach velocity (U₀)**.
- Efficiency decreases with **gas viscosity (μ)** and **collector diameter (D_c)**.

**Governing Parameter — Stokes Number:**

$$Stk = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, U_0 \, C_c}{18 \, \mu \, D_c}$$

This dimensionless number is the **ratio of particle stopping distance to collector characteristic length**. A high $Stk$ means high impaction efficiency.

## 2.2 Mechanism 2 — Direct Interception

**Working Principle:** A particle is collected when it follows the gas streamline faithfully (no inertial deviation) but its geometric size is large enough that the streamline passes within a distance of $d_p/2$ from the collector surface.

**Governing Parameter — Interception Number:**

$$N_R = \frac{d_p}{D_c}$$

- Pure interception occurs only in the **laminar flow regime** (low Reynolds number).
- For a cylindrical collector in potential flow:

$$\eta_R = \frac{(1 + N_R)^2 - 1}{(1 + N_R)^2} = \frac{2 \, N_R + N_R^2}{(1 + N_R)^2}$$

## 2.3 Mechanism 3 — Brownian Diffusion

**Working Principle:** Submicron particles are constantly bombarded by gas molecules, causing them to undergo a random-walk motion. Statistically, some of these excursions will bring the particle into contact with the collector surface.

**Governing Parameter — Peclet Number:**

$$Pe = \frac{D_c \, U_0}{D_B}$$

where the **Brownian diffusion coefficient** is given by the **Einstein-Stokes Relation**:

$$D_B = \frac{k_B \, T \, C_c}{3 \pi \mu \, d_p}$$

- $k_B$ = **1.380648 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
- Diffusion efficiency scales as $\eta_D \propto Pe^{-2/3}$ — efficiency **rises** as $Pe$ falls.
- Critical for particles below 0.1 μm (e.g., virus-sized aerosols).

## 2.4 Mechanism 4 — Gravitational Settling

**Working Principle:** Particles denser than the surrounding gas experience a net downward force and reach a constant **terminal settling velocity** when gravitational force balances viscous drag (Stokes regime, Re < 0.1).

**Governing Equation — Stokes' Terminal Velocity:**

$$V_t = \frac{d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, C_c}{18 \, \mu}$$

## 2.5 Mechanism 5 — Electrostatic Attraction

**Working Principle:** Particles can acquire a charge through:
- **Diffusion charging** (from random ion collisions, dominant for $d_p < 0.5$ μm)
- **Field charging** (dominant for $d_p > 0.5$ μm)

Once charged, the **Coulomb force** between the charged particle and an oppositely charged collector creates a trajectory deviation, enhancing capture. This is the working principle of **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESPs)**.

## 2.6 Mechanism 6 — Thermal Forces (Thermophoresis)

**Working Principle:** When a temperature gradient exists in the gas (e.g., hot flue gas near cooler collector walls), gas molecules on the hot side transfer more momentum to the particle than those on the cold side, producing a net force pushing the particle toward the cooler region. Important in **heat exchanger fouling** and **fibrous filter regeneration**.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stokes Number | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c}{18 \mu D_c}$ | dimensionless | > 1 ⇒ strong impaction |
| 2 | Interception Number | $N_R = d_p / D_c$ | dimensionless | Geometric ratio |
| 3 | Peclet Number | $Pe = D_c U_0 / D_B$ | dimensionless | High ⇒ low diffusion efficiency |
| 4 | Brownian Diffusivity | $D_B = \dfrac{k_B T C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | m²/s | Einstein-Stokes equation |
| 5 | Terminal Settling Velocity | $V_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 g(\rho_p - \rho_g) C_c}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Stokes regime, Re < 0.1 |
| 6 | Particle Relaxation Time | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 C_c}{18 \mu}$ | s | Time to adjust to flow change |
| 7 | Stopping Distance | $S = U_0 \, \tau$ | m | Distance traveled after flow stops |
| 8 | Cunningham Slip Correction | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left(1.257 + 0.4 e^{-0.55 d_p/\lambda}\right)$ | dimensionless | < 1 μm significance |
| 9 | Reynolds Number (collector) | $Re_c = \rho_g U_0 D_c / \mu$ | dimensionless | Defines flow regime |
| 10 | Single-Fiber Efficiency | $\eta_{total} \approx \eta_R + \eta_I + \eta_D$ | dimensionless | Additive approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Recap (must memorise for KTU):**
> - Stokes regime: $Re_p = \rho_g V_t d_p / \mu < 0.1$
> - Cunningham correction: $C_c \to 1$ when $d_p > 5$ μm; $C_c > 1$ when $d_p < 1$ μm.
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.380648 \times 10^{-23}$ J·K⁻¹.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Mechanism |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Cyclone separator | Inertial Impaction + Gravitational Settling |
| Fabric filter (baghouse) | Impaction + Interception + Diffusion |
| Wet scrubber (venturi) | Impaction on liquid droplets |
| Electrostatic precipitator | Electrostatic Attraction |
| HEPA filter (clean rooms) | Brownian Diffusion + Interception |
| Mist eliminator (demister pad) | Interception |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

We begin with a force balance on a single spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid. At terminal velocity, the net force = 0.

**Step 1:** Write the three forces acting on the particle:
- Gravitational force (downward): $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_p \, g$
- Buoyant force (upward): $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, g$
- Viscous drag force (upward, Stokes regime): $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p V_t$

**Step 2:** Apply equilibrium at terminal velocity:

$$F_d = F_g - F_b$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expressions:

$$3 \pi \mu d_p V_t = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_g g$$

**Step 4:** Factor the right-hand side:

$$3 \pi \mu d_p V_t = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu d_p$:

$$V_t = \frac{\pi d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{6 \times 3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the constants ($\pi$ cancels, one $d_p$ cancels):

$$V_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 7:** Introduce the Cunningham slip correction for sub-millimeter particles:

$$\boxed{V_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g \, C_c}{18 \mu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slip correction $C_c$ is needed because Stokes' law assumes a **continuum** gas. When $d_p$ approaches the gas mean free path $\lambda$ (≈ 0.066 μm in air at STP), the no-slip boundary condition breaks down and the particle "slips" between gas molecules, reducing drag and increasing $V_t$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Particle Relaxation Time (τ)

Relaxation time is the characteristic time required for a particle to respond to a change in gas flow direction.

**Step 1:** Newton's second law for a particle experiencing only Stokes drag:

$$m_p \frac{dV}{dt} = -3 \pi \mu d_p (V - U_g)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute particle mass $m_p = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p$:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p \frac{dV}{dt} = -3 \pi \mu d_p (V - U_g)$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into standard first-order ODE form:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{18 \mu}{\rho_p d_p^2} (V - U_g)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the time constant of this first-order system:

$$\tau = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 5:** Add slip correction for sub-continuum regime:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 C_c}{18 \mu}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Stopping Distance and Stokes Number

**Step 1:** Stopping distance $S$ is the distance a particle travels after the gas stream suddenly stops, before its own velocity decays to zero:

$$S = \int_0^\infty U_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Perform the integration:

$$S = U_0 \tau \left[ -e^{-t/\tau} \right]_0^\infty = U_0 \tau (0 - (-1)) = U_0 \tau$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for τ:

$$S = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 4:** The Stokes number is the dimensionless stopping distance — normalised by the collector diameter $D_c$:

$$\boxed{Stk = \frac{S}{D_c} = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c}{18 \mu D_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of Stk:**
> - $Stk \ll 1$: Particle has negligible inertia — follows gas streamlines perfectly. **No impaction capture.**
> - $Stk \approx 1$: Transition zone.
> - $Stk \gg 1$: Particle has very high inertia — moves in nearly straight lines. **Maximum impaction capture** (efficiency approaches 100%).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Single-Fiber Efficiency (Langmuir Additive Model)

For a cylindrical fiber (fabric filter, HEPA filter), total efficiency is the sum of three contributions.

**Step 1:** Impaction efficiency (empirical, from Ranz & Wang correlation for cylinders):

$$\eta_I = \frac{Stk^3}{Stk^3 + 0.77 \, Stk^2 + 0.22}$$

**Step 2:** Interception efficiency (analytical, potential flow past a cylinder):

$$\eta_R = \frac{2 N_R + N_R^2}{(1 + N_R)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Diffusion efficiency (empirical, from Natanson):

$$\eta_D = \frac{1.9}{Pe^{2/3}}$$

**Step 4:** Total single-fiber efficiency (Langmuir, 1942):

$$\boxed{\eta_{total} = \eta_I + \eta_R + \eta_D}$$

**Step 5:** For a filter of thickness $L$, solidity (packing density) $\alpha$, and fiber diameter $D_f$, the **overall filter penetration** is:

$$P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{4 \, \alpha \, L \, \eta_{total}}{\pi \, D_f (1 - \alpha)}\right)$$

and the **overall collection efficiency** is:

$$E = 1 - P$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A fly-ash particle of diameter $d_p = 10$ μm and density $\rho_p = 2200$ kg/m³ is in air at $T = 300$ K, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_g = 1.18$ kg/m³. Gas velocity $U_0 = 1.5$ m/s, collector diameter $D_c = 0.1$ m. Assume $C_c \approx 1$. Calculate: (i) terminal settling velocity, (ii) relaxation time, (iii) Stokes number, and (iv) impaction efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(i) Terminal Settling Velocity:**

$$V_t = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (2200 - 1.18) \times 9.81 \times 1}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$V_t = \frac{1 \times 10^{-10} \times 2198.82 \times 9.81}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_t = \frac{2.157 \times 10^{-6}}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Relaxation Time:**

$$\tau = \frac{2200 \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-7}}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Stokes Number:**

$$Stk = \frac{2200 \times 1 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.5 \times 1}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.1} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^{-7}}{3.33 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.0099$$

**(iv) Impaction Efficiency:**

$$\eta_I = \frac{(0.0099)^3}{(0.0099)^3 + 0.77 (0.0099)^2 + 0.22} \approx \frac{9.7 \times 10^{-7}}{0.22} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This near-zero impaction efficiency is **expected for a 10 μm particle against a 0.1 m collector at low velocity** — large collectors at low velocity do not promote impaction. Reducing $D_c$ to 100 μm (a fiber) would give $Stk \approx 9.9$ and $\eta_I \to 100\%$.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List and briefly explain any three mechanisms of gas-particle interaction with the particle size range in which each is dominant."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Inertial Impaction (1 mark):** Particles > 1 μm cannot follow gas streamlines around a collector due to high momentum; governed by Stokes number $Stk = \rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c / (18 \mu D_c)$.
2. **Brownian Diffusion (1 mark):** Particles < 0.1 μm undergo random motion due to molecular bombardment and statistically contact the collector; efficiency $\eta_D \propto Pe^{-2/3}$.
3. **Direct Interception (1 mark):** Particles of size 0.1–1 μm follow the streamline but touch the collector when $d_p/2$ reaches the surface; governed by $N_R = d_p / D_c$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define Cunningham Slip Correction Factor. Why is it significant for sub-micron particles?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** $C_c$ is a dimensionless correction factor that accounts for the failure of the no-slip boundary condition when particle diameter approaches the mean free path of gas molecules ($\lambda \approx 0.066$ μm in air at STP).
- **Mathematical form (1 mark):** $C_c = 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left(1.257 + 0.4 e^{-0.55 d_p/\lambda}\right)$
- **Significance (1 mark):** For $d_p < 1$ μm, $C_c > 1$ substantially reduces drag, increasing terminal velocity, relaxation time, and impaction efficiency. Without $C_c$, calculated collection efficiencies for ultrafine PM would be wrongly underestimated by 30–300%.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the mechanism of **inertial impaction** in gas-particle interaction. Derive the expression for the **Stokes number** starting from a force balance on a particle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fabric filter operates at a face velocity of $U_0 = 0.20$ m/s using cylindrical fibers of diameter $D_f = 20$ μm, solidity (packing density) $\alpha = 0.05$, and filter thickness $L = 2.0$ mm. The particulate matter has density $\rho_p = 1500$ kg/m³, mean diameter $d_p = 5$ μm, and Cunningham correction $C_c = 1.05$. Air viscosity $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Calculate the **single-fiber impaction efficiency** and the **overall filter efficiency**. Assume interception and diffusion are negligible.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the mechanism dominance map in Section 4.3. Show a streamline curving around a cylindrical fiber with a particle impacting the fiber due to inertia.

**Explanation (2 marks):** A particle-laden gas stream approaches a collector (e.g., filter fiber). The gas streamlines curve smoothly around the obstacle, but the particle, having finite inertia, continues in a near-straight line. If the particle's straight-line trajectory intersects the fiber, the particle is captured. Efficiency rises with particle mass and approach velocity; falls with gas viscosity and fiber diameter.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
From Newton's second law on a particle experiencing Stokes drag:
$$m_p \frac{dV}{dt} = -3 \pi \mu d_p (V - U_g)$$
Integrating gives exponential decay with time constant $\tau = \rho_p d_p^2 C_c / (18 \mu)$. The stopping distance is $S = U_0 \tau$. The Stokes number, the ratio of stopping distance to collector diameter, is:
$$Stk = \frac{S}{D_c} = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c}{18 \mu D_c}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Calculate Stokes number with $D_c = D_f = 20$ μm $= 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$Stk = \frac{1500 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 0.20 \times 1.05}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator:
$$1500 \times 25 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.20 \times 1.05 = 7.875 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:
$$18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 6.516 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 4:** Divide:
$$Stk = \frac{7.875 \times 10^{-9}}{6.516 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.209$$

**Step 5:** Impaction efficiency from Ranz-Wang correlation:

$$\eta_I = \frac{(1.209)^3}{(1.209)^3 + 0.77 (1.209)^2 + 0.22} = \frac{1.768}{1.768 + 1.125 + 0.22} = \frac{1.768}{3.113} \approx 0.568$$

**Step 6:** Overall filter penetration:

$$P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 0.002 \times 0.568}{\pi \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.95}\right) = \exp(-3.80) = 0.0223$$

**Step 7:** Overall efficiency:

$$E = 1 - P = 1 - 0.0223 = 0.9777 \approx \mathbf{97.77\%}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of **Brownian diffusion** in gas-particle interaction. Derive the Einstein-Stokes equation for the Brownian diffusion coefficient and explain why diffusion dominates for sub-micron particles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at 300 K carries 0.05 μm particles ($\rho_p = 1200$ kg/m³). Compute: (i) the Cunningham slip correction factor using $\lambda = 0.066$ μm, (ii) the Brownian diffusion coefficient $D_B$, and (iii) the diffusion efficiency $\eta_D$ for a 100 μm fiber at face velocity 0.1 m/s. Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):** Sub-micron particles suspended in a gas experience continuous random collisions with gas molecules (per **kinetic theory of gases**). This imparts a **random walk** to the particle, statistically causing it to migrate to and contact the collector surface. The smaller the particle, the more vigorous the random walk.

**Derivation of Einstein-Stokes Equation (3 marks):** From the **fluctuation-dissipation theorem**, the diffusion coefficient of a spherical particle in a viscous medium is:
$$D_B = \frac{k_B T}{f}$$
where $f$ is the **friction coefficient** (Stokes drag per unit velocity):
$$f = 3 \pi \mu d_p / C_c$$
Substituting:
$$D_B = \frac{k_B T \, C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

**Why diffusion dominates for sub-micron particles (2 marks):** $D_B \propto 1/d_p$ — as $d_p$ decreases, $D_B$ increases dramatically. Also $Pe = D_c U_0 / D_B$ decreases, so $\eta_D \propto Pe^{-2/3}$ increases. For 0.05 μm particles, $D_B$ is two to three orders of magnitude higher than for 5 μm particles, making diffusion the dominant capture mechanism in HEPA filters and the upper atmosphere.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Cunningham slip correction:
$$C_c = 1 + \frac{2 \times 0.066}{0.05}\left(1.257 + 0.4 e^{-0.55 \times 0.05/0.066}\right)$$
$$= 1 + 2.64 \left(1.257 + 0.4 e^{-0.4167}\right) = 1 + 2.64 (1.257 + 0.4 \times 0.659)$$
$$= 1 + 2.64 (1.257 + 0.2636) = 1 + 2.64 \times 1.5206 = 1 + 4.014 = \mathbf{5.014}$$

**Step 2:** Brownian diffusion coefficient:
$$D_B = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 5.014}{3 \pi \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}}$$
Numerator: $1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 5.014 = 2.076 \times 10^{-20}$
Denominator: $3 \pi \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^{-8} = 8.717 \times 10^{-12}$
$$D_B = \frac{2.076 \times 10^{-20}}{8.717 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.382 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Peclet number with $D_c = 100$ μm $= 10^{-4}$ m, $U_0 = 0.1$ m/s:
$$Pe = \frac{10^{-4} \times 0.1}{2.382 \times 10^{-9}} = 4198.2$$

**Step 4:** Diffusion efficiency (Natanson equation):
$$\eta_D = \frac{1.9}{(4198.2)^{2/3}} = \frac{1.9}{258.7} \approx \mathbf{0.00735}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $C_c$** in either $V_t$, $\tau$, or $Stk$ for particles below 1 μm — results in a 30–300% error. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing Stokes regime** — Stokes' law is valid only for $Re_p < 0.1$. For larger particles, the drag coefficient must be replaced with the Schiller-Naumann correlation: $C_D = 24/Re_p (1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687})$.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions** — $d_p$ must be in metres, $\mu$ in Pa·s, $D_c$ in metres. Mixing μm and m is the most common arithmetic error in KTU scripts.
> 4. **Treating $\eta_{total}$ as multiplicative instead of additive** — The Langmuir model is additive: $\eta \approx \eta_I + \eta_R + \eta_D$ (with appropriate correlation factors at very high $Stk$ or low $Pe$).
> 5. **Omitting the diagram** in the 7-mark part — KTU valuation key explicitly awards 1–2 marks for a labelled schematic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 **Six Mechanisms**: Inertial Impaction, Direct Interception, Brownian Diffusion, Gravitational Settling, Electrostatic Attraction, Thermal Forces.
- 🔑 **Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS)**: ≈ 0.3 μm — minimum efficiency point where impaction is too weak and diffusion is barely starting.
- 🔑 **Stokes Number formula** $Stk = \rho_p d_p^2 U_0 C_c / (18 \mu D_c)$ — must be **memorised verbatim**.
- 🔑 **Einstein-Stokes Equation** $D_B = k_B T C_c / (3 \pi \mu d_p)$ — required for diffusion problems.
- 🔑 **Cunningham Slip Correction** is **non-negligible** for $d_p < 1$ μm; can exceed 5 for $d_p \approx 0.05$ μm.
- 🔑 **Terminal Settling Velocity** in Stokes regime: $V_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g C_c / (18 \mu)$.
- 🔑 **Single-Fiber Total Efficiency (Langmuir)**: $\eta \approx \eta_I + \eta_R + \eta_D$ (additive, not multiplicative).
- 🔑 **Filter Overall Efficiency**: $E = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{4 \alpha L \eta}{\pi D_f (1-\alpha)}\right)$.
- 🔑 **Numerical**: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $\lambda_{air, STP} = 0.066$ μm.
- 🔑 **Design Rule**: To capture PM₂.₅, use **fine fibers + low velocity** (promotes diffusion). To capture PM₁₀, use **coarse fibers + high velocity** (promotes impaction).
- 🔑 **Real-World Mapping**: Cyclone → Impaction; ESP → Electrostatic; Scrubber → Droplet Impaction; HEPA → Diffusion + Interception; Baghouse → All three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Performance Equations of Gravity Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design and Performance Equations of Gravity Separators ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Gravity Separator** (also called a **Settling Chamber** or **Expansion Chamber**) is a particulate air pollution control device that removes suspended solid or liquid particles from a carrier gas stream solely by exploiting the difference in density between the particle and the surrounding gas, under the action of the Earth's gravitational field. It is the oldest, simplest, and cheapest form of particulate control equipment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gravity separators are the introductory control device in Module 3. They are typically effective only for **coarse particles (typically > 50 μm)** and are used as **pre-cleaners** before more efficient devices like cyclones, scrubbers, or ESPs. **Operating Principle:** The dust-laden gas is forced to flow through a chamber whose cross-sectional area is much larger than the inlet duct. This sudden expansion reduces the gas velocity dramatically, lowering the carrying capacity (turbulence) of the stream and allowing the heavier particles to settle out by gravity onto the floor of the chamber, from where they are collected in hoppers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy (River Sedimentation):** > Imagine a fast-flowing, muddy river entering a wide, deep lake. As the river water enters the lake, it suddenly slows down. The river no longer has the energy to carry the heavy mud, pebbles, and sand particles. Slowly, these heavier particles **sink to the bottom** of the lake, while the clean water flows out the other side. A gravity separator works in exactly the same way: **dirty air (river) → wide chamber (lake) → particles drop out (sedimentation) → clean air exits.** ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Particle density ($\rho_p$):** Typically **1000 to 2500 kg/m³** for most industrial dusts. - **Gas density ($\rho$):** Air at standard conditions = **1.20 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, 1 atm). - **Gas viscosity ($\mu$):** Air at 20 °C = **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s)** (or 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s). - **Acceleration due to gravity ($g$):** **9.81 m/s²**. - **Mean free path of air ($\lambda$):** **0.066 μm** at standard conditions. - **Standard gas velocity in chamber ($v_g$):** Recommended between **0.3 to 3.0 m/s** to prevent re-entrainment. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept (Step-by-Step Logic) The entire design philosophy of a gravity separator rests on a single time-based comparison: > **For a particle to be captured, the time it takes the particle to settle from the top of the chamber to the bottom MUST be less than (or equal to) the time the gas spends inside the chamber.** 1. **Gas Entry:** Dust-laden gas enters the chamber through an inlet duct of small cross-section. 2. **Expansion & Deceleration:** The chamber cross-section is much larger → velocity drops from 15–25 m/s (duct velocity) down to 0.3–3 m/s. 3. **Horizontal Transport:** The gas travels horizontally through the chamber of length **L** at horizontal velocity **$v_g$**. 4. **Vertical Settling:** Each particle, while being carried horizontally, simultaneously falls vertically at its **terminal settling velocity ($v_s$)**. 5. **Collection:** If a particle falls a vertical distance **H** (chamber height) before the gas exits the chamber, it is collected. Otherwise, it escapes with the gas. 6. **Hopper Discharge:** Collected dust slides down sloped walls into a hopper for removal. ### 2.2 The Three Settling Regimes (Cunningham Correction Included) A particle falling under gravity quickly reaches a constant **terminal settling velocity** where gravitational force is balanced by drag force. The regime depends on the **Reynolds number of the particle ($Re_p$)**: $$Re_p = \frac{d_p \, v_s \, \rho}{\mu}$$ **Regime 1: Stokes' Law (Laminar Flow, $Re_p < 0.1$)** — Valid for fine particles. $$v_s = \frac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \, \mu} \cdot C_c$$ **Regime 2: Transition Regime (0.1 < $Re_p$ < 1000)** — Most industrial dusts. $$v_s = \frac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho)^{0.71}}{\mu^{0.43} \, \rho^{0.29}} \cdot C_c$$ **Regime 3: Newton's Law (Turbulent Flow, $Re_p > 1000$)** — For very large/heavy particles. $$v_s = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho)}{\rho}} \cdot C_c$$ **Cunningham Slip Correction Factor ($C_c$):** Essential for sub-micron particles where air can no longer be treated as a continuous fluid. $$C_c = 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left[1.257 + 0.4 \, e^{-0.55 \, d_p / \lambda}\right]$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation you need for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the **Stokes' law** and the **design equation** above all. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gas Residence Time | $t_g = L / v_g$ | seconds | | 2 | Particle Settling Time | $t_s = H / v_s$ | seconds | | 3 | **Chamber Design Equation** | $L = \dfrac{H \, v_g}{v_s}$ | Length in m | | 4 | **Cut (Critical) Diameter** | $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}{g \, L \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}}$ | Diameter in m | | 5 | **Grade Collection Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{v_s \, L}{H \, v_g} = \left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2$ | Fractional (0 to 1) | | 6 | **Multiple Tray Efficiency** (n trays) | $\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)^n$ | where $\eta_1$ = single-tray eff. | | 7 | Stokes' Settling Velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}{18 \, \mu}$ | m/s | | 8 | Reynolds Number of Particle | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v_s \, \rho}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | Cunningham Factor | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left[1.257 + 0.4 \, e^{-0.55 d_p / \lambda}\right]$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | Recommended Gas Velocity | $v_g < 0.5 \sqrt{d_p \, \rho_p \, g / \rho}$ | To avoid re-entrainment | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Symbols:** $L$ = chamber length, $H$ = chamber height, $v_g$ = horizontal gas velocity, $v_s$ = terminal settling velocity, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $d_{pc}$ = cut diameter (the smallest particle collected with **100% efficiency**), $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho$ = gas density, $\lambda$ = mean free path of gas molecules. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Gravity chambers are widely used as: - **Pre-cleaners** for hot, abrasive, or coarse dusts (e.g., steel mills, cement kilns, smelters). - **Spark arrestors** in sawmills, grain elevators, and wood-waste incinerators (where large embers must be removed). - **Recovery chambers** in metallurgical operations to reclaim valuable coarse particles. - **First stage** in multi-stage air pollution control trains to reduce the dust loading before a cyclone or ESP. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Chamber Design Equation ($L = H v_g / v_s$) **Physical Statement:** A particle entering the chamber at the very top edge must settle through the full chamber height **H** before the gas carries it past the chamber length **L**. **Step 1 — Write the time available for settling.** The horizontal residence time of the gas inside the chamber of length **L** at velocity **$v_g$** is: $$t_g = \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 2 — Write the time required for a particle to settle.** A particle settling at terminal velocity **$v_s$** covers the vertical height **H** in time: $$t_s = \frac{H}{v_s}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the fundamental capture condition.** For the particle to reach the floor, the settling time must be less than or equal to the gas residence time: $$t_s \le t_g$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** $$\frac{H}{v_s} \le \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the design equation for 100% collection.** $$L \ge \frac{H \, v_g}{v_s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{L = \frac{H \, v_g}{v_s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** For a fixed gas flow rate $Q$, the chamber cross-section $A = Q/v_g$. Therefore increasing $v_g$ reduces the required chamber area (and hence cost), but also reduces the size of particle that can be captured. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Grade Collection Efficiency ($\eta = v_s L / H v_g$) **Physical Statement:** Not every particle enters the chamber at the very top. If a particle enters at a height $h$ above the floor, it only needs to fall that distance to be captured. **Step 1 — Maximum fallable distance in chamber time.** During residence time $t_g = L/v_g$, a particle settles a vertical distance: $$h_{\text{fall}} = v_s \cdot t_g = v_s \cdot \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 2 — Define efficiency geometrically.** Assume uniform dust distribution across the inlet. The fraction of particles whose entry height is less than or equal to $h_{\text{fall}}$ will be collected. This fraction is: $$\eta = \frac{h_{\text{fall}}}{H} = \frac{v_s \, L}{H \, v_g}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Stokes' law for $v_s$.** $$v_s = \frac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}{18 \, \mu}$$ $$\eta = \frac{L \cdot g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}$$ **Step 4 — Derive efficiency as a function of particle diameter.** Recognizing that $d_{pc}$ is defined as the diameter for which $\eta = 1$ (i.e., $h_{\text{fall}} = H$), we can write: $$d_{pc}^2 = \frac{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}{g \, L (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\eta = \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** The grade efficiency curve of an ideal gravity chamber is a **PARABOLA**, not a step function. Particles smaller than $d_{pc}$ are only partially collected, with efficiency proportional to the square of their diameter. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cut (Critical) Diameter **Definition:** The **cut diameter $d_{pc}$** is the diameter of the smallest particle that is collected with **100% efficiency** (i.e., a particle entering at the very top of the chamber still reaches the floor before the gas exits). **Step 1 — At the cut diameter, settling time equals residence time.** $$t_s = t_g \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{H}{v_{s, c}} = \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the critical settling velocity.** $$v_{s, c} = \frac{H \, v_g}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Equate to Stokes' law and solve for $d_{pc}$.** $$v_{s, c} = \frac{g \, d_{pc}^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}{18 \, \mu} = \frac{H \, v_g}{L}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $d_{pc}$.** $$\boxed{d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}{g \, L (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}}}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Multiple Horizontal Trays (Baffles) **Physical Statement:** Dividing the chamber height $H$ into $n$ horizontal trays reduces the effective height each particle must fall to $H/n$, which quadratically increases efficiency. **Step 1 — Effective height per sub-chamber.** $$H_{\text{eff}} = \frac{H}{n}$$ **Step 2 — New cut diameter per sub-chamber.** $$d_{pc,\text{new}} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, (H/n) \, v_g}{g \, L (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}} = \frac{d_{pc}}{\sqrt{n}}$$ **Step 3 — New single-tray efficiency.** $$\eta_{\text{new}} = n \cdot \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2 = n \eta$$ **Step 4 — But $\eta_{\text{new}}$ cannot exceed 1. For $n$ trays in series, the overall (cumulative) efficiency is:** $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)^n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical limit:** Adding trays works only up to $n \approx 6$–$8$ trays. Beyond that, the gas velocity in the narrow tray spacing becomes high enough to cause re-entrainment, and dust removal from the trays becomes mechanically problematic. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Gravity Settling Chamber ```mermaid flowchart TD inletA[Dust Laden Gas Inlet] --> nozzleA[Inlet Nozzle
High Velocity 15 to 25 m per s] nozzleA --> expansionA[Expansion Zone
Sudden Area Increase] expansionA --> chamberA[Settling Chamber
Low Velocity 0.3 to 3 m per s
Length L Height H] chamberA --> baffle1[Optional Horizontal Trays
Reduce Effective H] chamberA --> particleA[Particle Settling Path
Vertical Fall at v sub s] particleA --> hopperA[Collection Hopper
Sloped Floor] hopperA --> dischargeA[Dust Discharge
Screw Conveyor or Valve] chamberA --> outletA[Clean Gas Outlet
Through Stack] style inletA fill:#e1f5ff style nozzleA fill:#fff4e1 style expansionA fill:#ffe1f5 style chamberA fill:#e1ffe1 style baffle1 fill:#f5e1ff style particleA fill:#ffe1e1 style hopperA fill:#e1e1ff style dischargeA fill:#ffffcc style outletA fill:#ccffcc ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Particle Trajectory Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR startA[Particle Enters
at Height h above floor] --> moveA[Gas carries particle
horizontally at v sub g] moveA --> fallA[Particle falls
vertically at v sub s] fallA --> decideA{Does particle
reach floor
before exit?} decideA -- YES --> collectA[COLLECTED
falls into hopper] decideA -- NO --> escapeA[ESCAPES
exits with gas] collectA --> recA[Efficiency Contribution] escapeA --> lossA[Efficiency Loss] style startA fill:#cce5ff style moveA fill:#fff2cc style fallA fill:#fff2cc style decideA fill:#ffcc99 style collectA fill:#99ff99 style escapeA fill:#ff9999 style recA fill:#ccffcc style lossA fill:#ffcccc ``` ### 4.3 Physical Layout Schematic (ASCII Representation) ``` GAS FLOW DIRECTION =====================> Dusty Gas || | Inlet ===>> | | ___________________________________ (Duct) || | | __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ | | | * * * * * * * * * * * * * Tray 1 | ----+ | | __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ | \ | | * * * * * * * * * * * * * Tray 2 | ---> Clean v_g \ | | __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ __ | Gas -------+ | | | Outlet | | H = Total Chamber Height | | | | | | L = Total Chamber Length | | |___________________________________| | \ / | \ / Hopper | \/ (Dust Collection) | / \ | / \ to dust disposal ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** > - The chamber length is $L$ (in gas flow direction). > - The chamber height is $H$ (vertical). > - The horizontal trays shown are an optional modification that **enhances collection efficiency** by reducing the effective settling distance. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define a gravity settling chamber. State the basic principle on which it works.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A gravity settling chamber is a particulate control device that uses the force of gravity to separate dust particles from a gas stream. The basic principle involves **reducing the gas velocity** by passing the dusty gas through an expanded cross-section. When velocity falls below the carrying capacity of the stream, particles denser than the gas settle out under gravity. Collection is governed by the comparison between the particle's terminal settling velocity and the gas velocity. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark principle + 1 mark governing condition)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the "cut diameter" of a gravity separator? Write its expression.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **cut diameter ($d_{pc}$)** is the diameter of the smallest particle that is collected with **100% efficiency** in the chamber. It is the diameter of a particle whose terminal settling velocity is just sufficient to fall the chamber height $H$ in the time the gas takes to traverse the chamber length $L$. Its expression is: $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}{g \, L \, (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}}$$ *(1 mark definition + 2 marks expression with explanation of terms)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module 3 Internal Choice Instruction:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A] **(a)** *Derive the design equation for a gravity settling chamber. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Given:** Chamber of length $L$, height $H$, gas velocity $v_g$, terminal settling velocity $v_s$. **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Particles are spherical and of uniform density. 2. Gas flow inside the chamber is **laminar and uniform** (plug flow). 3. Settling occurs under **Stokes' law regime** ($Re_p < 0.1$). 4. **Cunningham correction** is included for very fine particles. 5. No re-entrainment of settled particles from the floor. 6. Dust concentration is uniform across the inlet. **Derivation (5 marks):** **Step 1:** The time available for a particle to settle is the gas residence time: $$t_g = \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 2:** The time required for a particle to settle through height $H$: $$t_s = \frac{H}{v_s}$$ **Step 3:** For 100% collection, $t_s \le t_g$: $$\frac{H}{v_s} \le \frac{L}{v_g}$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging: $$L \ge \frac{H \, v_g}{v_s}$$ **Step 5:** The minimum chamber length required is therefore: $$\boxed{L_{\min} = \frac{H \, v_g}{v_s}}$$ This is the **fundamental design equation** of a gravity settling chamber. --- **(b)** *A flue gas containing dust particles of density 1800 kg/m³ flows at 0.6 m/s through a settling chamber of length 8 m and height 2 m. The gas viscosity is 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s) and gas density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate the cut diameter and the collection efficiency for a 30 μm particle. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Given:** $v_g = 0.6$ m/s, $L = 8$ m, $H = 2$ m, $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $d_p = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s². For $d_p = 30 \, \mu\text{m}$, the Cunningham correction is essentially **1** (only sub-micron particles need it). Take $C_c \approx 1$. **Step 1 — Calculate the cut diameter $d_{pc}$ (3 marks):** $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, H \, v_g}{g \, L (\rho_p - \rho) \, C_c}}$$ Substituting: $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 0.6}{9.81 \times 8 \times (1800 - 1.2) \times 1}}$$ $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{3.888 \times 10^{-4}}{140\,740.6}}$$ $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{2.762 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$d_{pc} = 5.255 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m} = 52.55 \, \mu\text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate grade efficiency for 30 μm particle (3 marks):** $$\eta = \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{30}{52.55}\right)^2 = (0.5709)^2$$ $$\boxed{\eta = 0.326 = 32.6\%}$$ **Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The cut diameter is **52.55 μm**, so only particles **larger than 52.55 μm** are collected with 100% efficiency. A 30 μm particle is only **32.6%** collected, confirming that simple gravity chambers are **inefficient for fine particulates** — a key reason they are used only as pre-cleaners. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B] **(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a gravity settling chamber. Discuss how adding horizontal trays improves its collection efficiency. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Construction (3 marks):** A gravity settling chamber is essentially an **empty rectangular chamber** with the following components: - An **inlet duct** that introduces dust-laden gas. - A **sudden expansion** at the entrance to reduce velocity. - A **rectangular settling zone** of length $L$ and height $H$, where the gas flows slowly in the horizontal direction. - **Hoppers** at the bottom with sloped walls (typically 45°–60°) for dust collection. - A **dust discharge mechanism** (rotary valve or screw conveyor) at the bottom. - A **clean gas outlet** at the opposite end leading to a stack. - *Optional:* **Horizontal baffles/trays** spaced at vertical intervals inside the chamber. **Working (2 marks):** Dusty gas enters at high velocity in the inlet duct. Upon entering the expanded chamber, the velocity drops sharply (by a factor of 5–10). With low turbulence, particles heavier than the gas fall under gravity. A particle is captured if it falls the height $H$ before the gas exits the chamber. Collected dust slides into hoppers and is removed; cleaned gas exits through the outlet. **Effect of Horizontal Trays (2 marks):** Adding $n$ horizontal trays divides the chamber into $n$ sub-chambers, each of effective height $H/n$. The cut diameter becomes: $$d_{pc,\text{new}} = \frac{d_{pc}}{\sqrt{n}}$$ The overall efficiency of $n$ trays in series is: $$\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)^n$$ For example, with $n = 4$ trays, the cut diameter reduces by a factor of 2, and the overall efficiency for a given particle size increases substantially. --- **(b)** *A gas stream at 1.0 m/s contains dust of density 2000 kg/m³. The settling chamber is 10 m long and 1.5 m high. The gas has $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s) and $\rho = 1.0$ kg/m³. (i) Find the cut diameter. (ii) If 4 horizontal trays are installed, find the new cut diameter and the overall efficiency for a 25 μm particle. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Given:** $v_g = 1.0$ m/s, $L = 10$ m, $H = 1.5$ m, $\rho_p = 2000$ kg/m³, $\rho = 1.0$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $C_c \approx 1$, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Part (i) — Cut diameter without trays (3 marks):** $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 1.0}{9.81 \times 10 \times (2000 - 1.0) \times 1}}$$ $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{5.4 \times 10^{-4}}{196\,191.9}} = \sqrt{2.753 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$d_{pc} = 5.247 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m} = 52.47 \, \mu\text{m}$$ **Part (ii) — With 4 trays (4 marks):** New cut diameter: $$d_{pc,\text{new}} = \frac{d_{pc}}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{52.47}{2} = 26.24 \, \mu\text{m}$$ Grade efficiency for $d_p = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$ with trays: $$\eta_1 = \left(\frac{25}{26.24}\right)^2 = (0.9527)^2 = 0.9077 = 90.77\%$$ Overall efficiency for 4 trays in series: $$\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - 0.9077)^4 = 1 - (0.0923)^4$$ $$\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - 7.26 \times 10^{-5} = 0.9999 = \mathbf{99.99\%}$$ **Conclusion:** Adding 4 horizontal trays reduced the cut diameter from 52.47 μm to 26.24 μm and increased the collection efficiency for 25 μm particles from ~22.7% to nearly 100% — a dramatic improvement. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mistakes:** Particle diameter must always be in **METERS** in the formula, not micrometers. Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing up units. > 2. **Forgetting $C_c$:** For particles above ~5 μm, $C_c \approx 1$ can be assumed. But for sub-micron particles, ignoring $C_c$ leads to gross errors. > 3. **Density difference vs. particle density:** The formula uses $(\rho_p - \rho)$, **NOT** just $\rho_p$. The buoyancy correction is small but required. > 4. **Efficiency formula misuse:** $\eta = (d_p/d_{pc})^2$ is valid **only in the Stokes regime**. In transition or Newton regimes, use the actual $v_s$ from the appropriate formula. > 5. **Confusing cut diameter with mean diameter:** $d_{pc}$ is a **theoretical 100% collection size**, not an average particle size. > 6. **Sign error in derivation:** Ensure $L = H v_g / v_s$ (not $v_s / v_g$). Always do a **dimensional check**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Gravity separators** remove particles purely by gravitational settling — no moving parts, no water, no electricity. - **Core equation:** $L = H v_g / v_s$ — chamber length must be large enough for a particle to fall height $H$ in the gas residence time. - **Cut diameter:** $d_{pc} = \sqrt{18 \mu H v_g / [g L (\rho_p - \rho) C_c]}$ — the size of the smallest particle collected at 100% efficiency. - **Grade efficiency:** $\eta = (d_p / d_{pc})^2$ — quadratic in particle diameter (parabolic grade efficiency curve). - **Stokes' law applies** when $Re_p < 0.1$; for larger particles, use the transition or Newton formula. - **Cunningham correction** $C_c$ is significant for $d_p < 1 \, \mu\text{m}$; for typical KTU problems with $d_p \ge 5 \, \mu\text{m}$, take $C_c \approx 1$. - **Horizontal trays** reduce effective settling height, **improving efficiency** by factor of $n$ in the Stokes regime. - **Multiple-tray overall efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)^n$ (trays act in series, like a filter cascade). - **Recommended gas velocity:** $v_g < 0.5 \sqrt{d_p \rho_p g / \rho}$ to prevent re-entrainment of settled dust. - **Limitations:** Large floor space required; inefficient below ~50 μm; not suitable for sticky or hygroscopic dusts. - **Typical use:** **Pre-cleaner** in series with cyclone, scrubber, or ESP — never as a standalone final control device for fine particulates. - **Units mantra:** Always convert $d_p$ from $\mu\text{m}$ to m, $\rho_p$ from g/cc to kg/m³, and $\mu$ from cP to kg/(m·s) before substitution. - **Dimensional check:** Always verify $[L] = $ m, $[\eta] = $ dimensionless, $[d_{pc}] = $ m. This catches 90% of errors. - **Assumption list for derivations:** Uniform flow, spherical particles, Stokes' regime, no re-entrainment, plug flow — always state these in exam answers.

topics.title: Centrifugal Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Centrifugal Separators # Module 3 – Centrifugal Separators (Cyclones) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cyclone Separator (Centrifugal Separator):** A particulate control device that uses **centrifugal force** generated by a swirling gas stream to separate solid or liquid particulates from a carrier gas. The device has no moving parts; separation is achieved by creating a vortex that throws denser particles outward against the cyclone wall, where they lose momentum and slide down into a hopper under gravity, while the cleaned gas spirals upward through the central vortex finder and exits through the top outlet. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a cyclone is classified as a **dry mechanical particulate control device** operating on the principle of **inertial impaction augmented by centrifugal acceleration**. The dimensionless parameter that quantifies this effect is the **separation number** ($N_s$), defined as the ratio of centrifugal acceleration to gravitational acceleration. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Salad Spinner" Analogy:** Imagine spinning a wet salad in a perforated bowl. Water droplets, though heavier than air only by a small margin (like dust in flue gas), are flung outward against the wall and squeezed out through the perforations. A cyclone does exactly the same thing to dust-laden air — the swirling motion creates an artificial gravity that can be **hundreds to thousands of times stronger than Earth's gravity**, so even fine particles "feel" heavy and are pushed to the wall, while clean air exits from the center. ### 1.3 Classification of Centrifugal Separators | Family | Sub-Type | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Reverse-Flow Cyclones** | Standard (Lapple) | General industrial dust collection | | | High-Efficiency (Stairmand) | Fine dust, sub-micron applications | | | High-Throughput | Large volumetric gas streams | | **Uniflow / Axial Cyclones** | Vane-type axial cyclone | Pre-cleaning, high temperature | | **Tangential Inlet Cyclones** | Single-inlet | Most common industrial design | | **Dynamic Separators** | Rotating blade type | Cement, mineral processing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Constants used in Cyclone Design:** > - **Effective Number of Turns ($N_e$):** **5 turns** for high-efficiency; **10 turns** for high-throughput cyclones > - **Operating Velocity Range ($v_i$):** **15 m/s to 30 m/s** at inlet > - **Pressure Drop Limit ($\Delta P$):** Typically **< 1250 Pa** (≈ 5 inches water column) > - **Standard Cyclone Body Diameter ($D$):** Ranges from **0.1 m to 3 m** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Tangential Entry of Dust-Laden Gas:** The particulate-laden gas stream enters the cyclone barrel through a tangential rectangular inlet. This tangential entry imparts a swirling (vortex) motion to the gas, with an entry tangential velocity $v_i$ (typically **15–30 m/s**). 2. **Vortex Formation (Outer Vortex):** As the gas descends helically down the cyclone body, it forms a **descending outer vortex**. The angular velocity generates a strong **centrifugal acceleration** $a_c = v_t^2/r$ directed radially outward. 3. **Particle Migration to Wall:** Dust particles entrained in the gas, being much denser, acquire the same tangential velocity. The centrifugal force $F_c = m_p \, v_t^2 / r$ pushes them outward toward the cyclone wall.
