The **separation number** $N_s = v_t^2 / (g \, r)$ describes how many times stronger this artificial gravity is compared to Earth's gravity. 4. **Particle Collection at Wall:** On reaching the wall, particles lose their radial velocity component due to friction, slide downward along the conical wall under gravity, and collect in the **dust hopper** at the bottom. 5. **Inner Vortex (Ascending):** At the bottom, the gas reverses direction and spirals upward as the **inner vortex** (clean gas core) through the central **vortex finder** (also called *gas exit tube*). 6. **Clean Gas Discharge:** The cleaned gas exits through the top outlet duct. The **vortex finder** prevents short-circuiting of fresh inlet gas directly to the outlet. 7. **Hopper Discharge:** Collected dust is discharged via a rotary airlock valve to maintain system pressure integrity. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind the Design:** The vortex finder extends *into* the cyclone body, not just flush with the top, because without it, the inner ascending vortex would draw un-cleaned inlet gas straight out — a phenomenon called *short-circuiting* that drastically reduces collection efficiency. ## 2.2 Governing Physics of Cyclone Performance The **two governing performance parameters** for a cyclone (as per KTU 2024 syllabus) are: - **Collection Efficiency ($\eta$):** Fraction (or percentage) of inlet particulates captured. - **Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$):** Energy penalty (Pa) imposed on the gas stream. These two are **inversely related in design trade-off**: Higher inlet velocity increases both collection efficiency *and* pressure drop. The designer must balance them. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU University Examinations on Centrifugal Separators. | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols / Units | Applicability | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Centrifugal force on particle | $F_c = \dfrac{m_p \, v_t^{\,2}}{r}$ | $F_c$: N; $m_p$: kg; $v_t$: m/s; $r$: m | Fundamental | | 2 | Centrifugal acceleration | $a_c = \dfrac{v_t^{\,2}}{r}$ | $a_c$: m/s² | Fundamental | | 3 | Separation number | $N_s = \dfrac{v_t^{\,2}}{g \, r}$ | Dimensionless | Measure of centrifugal intensity | | 4 | Terminal settling velocity (gravity) | $v_s = \dfrac{g \, d_p^{\,2} \, \rho_p}{18 \, \mu}$ | $v_s$: m/s; $d_p$: m | Stokes regime ($Re < 0.1$) | | 5 | Lapple cut diameter ($d_{50}$, 50% collection size) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \, \mu \, B}{2 \, \pi \, N_e \, \rho_p \, v_i}}$ | $d_{50}$: m; $B$: inlet width (m); $\mu$: Pa·s; $\rho_p$: kg/m³; $v_i$: m/s | High-throughput, tangential inlet | | 6 | Stairmand cut diameter | $d_{50} = \dfrac{0.2 \cdot \sqrt{\mu}}{\sqrt{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, v_i}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{B \, D}{H}}$ | Same as above; $D$: body dia.; $H$: total height | High-efficiency design | | 7 | Pressure drop (general form) | $\Delta P = \xi \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g \, v_i^{\,2}}{2}$ | $\Delta P$: Pa; $\xi$: loss coefficient; $\rho_g$: kg/m³ | All cyclones | | 8 | Loss coefficient (Stairmand HE) | $\xi = 7.5$ (high efficiency); $\xi = 4.0$ (high throughput) | Dimensionless | Empirical | | 9 | Shepherd & Lapple pressure drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{v_i^{\,2} \, \rho_g}{2} \left(7.5 \cdot \dfrac{B \, H}{D^2}\right)$ | All in SI units | Reverse-flow cyclones | | 10 | Cyclone cut size (general Barth) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \, \mu \, D}{2 \, \pi \, N_e \, v_i \, \rho_p}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{B}{D}\right)^{1/2}$ | m | Quick design estimate | | 11 | Particle Reynolds number | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_g \, v_i \, d_p}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Regime check | | 12 | Cyclone collection efficiency (fractional) | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left[-2 \left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{50}}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | $\beta \approx 2$ typically | Grade efficiency | | 13 | Specific gas load (clue) | $Q_s = 1.3 \cdot v_i \cdot A_{inlet}$ | m³/s | Sizing check | | 14 | Stairmand HE dimensions (ratios) | $D/H = 0.5$; $D/B = 0.2$ | Dimensionless | Standard design | > **Note on Critical Units:** All SI base units apply. Particle density $\rho_p$ is for the dust (kg/m³); gas density $\rho_g$ is for the carrier gas (kg/m³, typically ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ for air at STP). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Particulate control** in cement kilns, power plants, steel mills, and grain elevators - **Pre-cleaner** upstream of fabric filters or ESPs to reduce bulk loading - **High-temperature gas streams** (up to **1000 °C**), where wet scrubbers and ESPs fail - **Corrosive / explosive atmospheres**, as there are no electrical components - **Recovery of valuable industrial dusts** (e.g., catalyst recovery in FCC units) - Used in **venting systems for storage silos** to prevent dust explosions > [!NOTE] > The **Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone** is the de-facto standard design tested in most KTU lab demonstrations because it offers a clean balance between efficiency and pressure drop for typical dust loads. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Force on a Suspended Particle **Step 1 — Identify the physical setup.** A particle of mass $m_p$ travels in a circular orbit of radius $r$ with tangential velocity $v_t$. **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law for circular motion.** For a body undergoing uniform circular motion, the net radial (centripetal) force equals mass times centripetal acceleration: $$ F_{net} = m_p \cdot a_c $$ **Step 3 — Express centripetal acceleration.** For tangential velocity $v_t$ and orbital radius $r$: $$ a_c = \frac{v_t^{\,2}}{r} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and define centrifugal force.** By Newton's Third Law, the *outward* (centrifugal) reaction on the particle equals the inward centripetal force in magnitude: $$ F_c = m_p \cdot \frac{v_t^{\,2}}{r} $$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of particle diameter.** Substituting $m_p = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_p$: $$ F_c = \frac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_p \cdot \frac{v_t^{\,2}}{r} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the tangential velocity quadruples the centrifugal force. Halving the cyclone radius doubles it. This is why **smaller cyclones with high inlet velocity** are more efficient at capturing fine dust. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Separation Number ($N_s$) **Step 1 — Define what we want to compare.** Compare the centrifugal acceleration to standard gravity. **Step 2 — Write the ratio:** $$ N_s = \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{v_t^{\,2} / r}{g} $$ **Step 3 — Final form:** $$ N_s = \frac{v_t^{\,2}}{g \, r} $$ **Worked Example (typical):** For $v_t = 20$ m/s and $r = 0.25$ m (cyclone body radius), $$ N_s = \frac{(20)^2}{9.81 \times 0.25} = \frac{400}{2.4525} \approx 163 $$ This means the particle experiences a centrifugal force **163 times greater than its weight** — explaining why even sub-10 µm particles can be captured in cyclones. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Cyclone Cut Diameter (Lapple Equation) This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU examination purposes. The cut diameter $d_{50}$ is the size of particle collected with 50% efficiency. **Step 1 — Equate the radial drift velocity to the settling velocity.** For a particle in equilibrium, the radial inward drift velocity under centrifugal field equals the terminal settling velocity $v_s$ in the equivalent gravitational field. **Step 2 — Write the terminal settling velocity under gravity (Stokes regime):** $$ v_s = \frac{g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_p^{\,2}}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Step 3 — Scale the gravity term by $N_s$ for centrifugal field:** $$ v_{drift} = v_s \cdot N_s = \frac{g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_p^{\,2}}{18 \, \mu} \cdot \frac{v_t^{\,2}}{g \, r} $$ **Step 4 — Define the residence distance and time.** The particle travels an axial distance $L$ (cyclone length) at average axial velocity $\bar{v}_z$. The residence time is: $$ \tau = \frac{L}{\bar{v}_z} = \frac{N_e \cdot (2 \pi r)}{v_t} $$ where $N_e$ is the **effective number of turns** (≈ 5 for HE, 10 for HT). The path length is $N_e$ full circumferences. **Step 5 — Write the radial displacement during residence time:** $$ \Delta r = v_{drift} \cdot \tau = v_{drift} \cdot \frac{2 \pi r \, N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 6 — Set $\Delta r$ equal to the inlet width $B$ for 50% capture:** $$ B = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_{50}^{\,2} \, v_t^{\,2}}{18 \, \mu \, r} \cdot \frac{2 \pi r \, N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 7 — Cancel $r$ and $v_t$:** $$ B = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_{50}^{\,2} \cdot 2 \pi \, N_e \, v_t}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Step 8 — Solve for $d_{50}$:** $$ d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \, \mu \, B}{\pi \, N_e \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, v_t}} $$ **Step 9 — For dust in air, $(\rho_p - \rho_g) \approx \rho_p$, and including the 2 in denominator as some texts derive (Stairmand form), the canonical KTU form is:** $$ \boxed{\,d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \, \mu \, B}{2 \, \pi \, N_e \, \rho_p \, v_i}}\,} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Pressure Drop (Shepherd & Lapple) **Step 1 — Recognize the source of pressure loss.** Pressure drop in a cyclone arises from (a) inlet expansion, (b) kinetic energy in the exit vortex, and (c) wall friction. **Step 2 — Energy balance approach.** Apply the steady-flow energy equation between inlet (1) and outlet (2). Neglecting elevation change and heat transfer: $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho_g} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2} + \Delta P / \rho_g $$ **Step 3 — Recognize that at the exit, gas still has tangential kinetic energy:** For a reverse-flow cyclone, the exit gas retains tangential velocity $v_t \approx v_i$, contributing to energy loss. **Step 4 — Empirical correlation for total loss coefficient:** For a high-efficiency Stairmand cyclone, the loss coefficient $\xi \approx 7.5$. **Step 5 — General pressure drop equation:** $$ \Delta P = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho_g \, v_i^{\,2}}{2} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute Stairmand's loss coefficient correlation $\xi = 7.5 \cdot (BH/D^2)$:** $$ \boxed{\,\Delta P = \dfrac{v_i^{\,2} \, \rho_g}{2} \cdot \left(7.5 \cdot \dfrac{B \, H}{D^2}\right)\,} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Combining $d_{50}$ and $\eta$ The **grade efficiency** is given by: $$ \eta(d_p) = 1 - \exp\left[-2 \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{50}}\right)^{2}\right] $$ This is the Leith-Licht / classical first-order exponential. Verification at $d_p = d_{50}$: $$ \eta = 1 - e^{-2} = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647 \quad (\text{86.5\%}) $$ > **Note:** Some textbooks define the exponent differently (e.g., $-(d_p/d_{50})^2$ without the factor 2), so always check the exact form given in the KTU module textbook before the exam. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Validation Example (Kerala Exam Standard) **Problem:** A Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone has $D = 0.4$ m, $B = 0.08$ m, total height $H = 0.8$ m, $N_e = 5$. Inlet velocity $v_i = 20$ m/s, particle density $\rho_p = 2000$ kg/m³, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas density $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³. **Step 1 — Calculate cut diameter:** $$ d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.08}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 2000 \times 20}} $$ **Numerator:** $9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.08 = 1.296 \times 10^{-5}$ **Denominator:** $2 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 2000 \times 20 = 1.2566 \times 10^{6}$ **Ratio:** $1.296 \times 10^{-5} / 1.2566 \times 10^{6} = 1.0314 \times 10^{-11}$ $$ d_{50} = \sqrt{1.0314 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.21 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} \approx 3.2 \, \mu m $$ **Step 2 — Calculate pressure drop:** $$ \Delta P = \frac{(20)^2 \times 1.2}{2} \times \left(7.5 \times \frac{0.08 \times 0.8}{0.4^2}\right) $$ $$ = \frac{400 \times 1.2}{2} \times \left(7.5 \times \frac{0.064}{0.16}\right) $$ $$ = 240 \times (7.5 \times 0.4) = 240 \times 3 = 720 \, \text{Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** The cyclone will capture **50% of 3.2 µm particles** and experience a pressure drop of **720 Pa**, which is well within the typical design limit of 1250 Pa. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cyclone Component Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Dust-Laden Gas Inlet] --> B[Tangential Inlet Duct
Width B, Height H] B --> C[Outer Vortex
Descending Helical Flow] C --> D[Particulate Impact on Wall
Centrifugal Migration] D --> E[Sliding Dust Layer
Down Conical Wall] E --> F[Dust Hopper
with Rotary Airlock] F --> G[Recovered Particulate Discharge] C --> H[Inner Vortex
Ascending Helical Flow] H --> I[Vortex Finder
Central Exit Tube] I --> J[Clean Gas Outlet
Top Duct] D --> K[Pressure Drop Loss
= xi x rho x vi squared / 2] H --> K style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Cyclone Design Trade-Off (Block Flow) ```mermaid graph LR A[Inlet Velocity vi] --> B{High vi Design} B -- High --> C[High Collection Efficiency] B -- High --> D[High Pressure Drop] B -- Low --> E[Low Pressure Drop] B -- Low --> F[Poor Collection Efficiency] C --> G[Optimum vi Range 15 to 25 m/s] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H[Selected Cyclone Type] H -- HE --> I[Stairmand High Efficiency
N e = 5 turns] H -- HT --> J[Stairmand High Throughput
N e = 10 turns] H -- HT --> K[Smaller B, Larger D] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Performance Parameters ```mermaid graph TD P1[Geometric Parameters
D, B, H, De, vortex finder length] --> P[Performance] P2[Operating Parameters
vi, Q, rho_g, mu] --> P P3[Particulate Parameters
d_p, rho_p, beta] --> P P --> P4[Cut Diameter d50] P --> P5[Pressure Drop dP] P --> P6[Collection Efficiency eta] P4 --> P6 P5 --> P7[Operating Cost
Blower Energy] P6 --> P8[Emission Compliance] P7 --> P9[Design Trade Off] P8 --> P9 style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P9 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** **Q: Define the term "cut diameter" ($d_{50}$) for a cyclone separator. Why is it considered a key design parameter?** **Model Answer:** The **cut diameter** ($d_{50}$) is defined as the aerodynamic diameter of a particle that is collected by the cyclone with **50% collection efficiency** under specified operating conditions. It is a measure of the cyclone's ability to separate fine particles — a smaller $d_{50}$ indicates a more efficient cyclone. It is the key design parameter because: 1. It characterizes the **sharpness and overall efficiency** of the cyclone in a single number. 2. It directly determines the **grade efficiency curve** through the relation $\eta = 1 - \exp[-2(d_p/d_{50})^2]$. 3. It allows the designer to select a cyclone type (HE, HT, etc.) based on the **particle size distribution of the dust**. 4. It depends on the cyclone's **geometry** ($B$, $D$, $H$) and **operating conditions** ($v_i$, $\rho_p$, $\mu$). **(3 Marks — Definition: 1, Explanation: 2)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** **Q: List any three advantages and two limitations of cyclone separators as air pollution control devices.** **Model Answer:** **Advantages:** 1. **Simple construction** — no moving parts, no electrical components, low capital cost. 2. **Operates at high temperatures** (up to 1000 °C) and in **corrosive atmospheres**, unlike ESPs and fabric filters. 3. **Low maintenance** — only the airlock valve and hopper need periodic servicing; long service life. **Limitations:** 1. **Low efficiency for sub-10 µm particles** — typically less than 50% for fine dusts below 5 µm. 2. **High pressure drop** (500–1500 Pa), which increases blower operating cost. 3. *(Optional 3rd limitation)* Cannot handle **sticky or fibrous dusts** that clog the cyclone wall. **(3 Marks — 3 Advantages: 1.5 marks, 2 Limitations: 1 mark, Total: 3)** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a reverse-flow cyclone separator**. Explain the role of the vortex finder. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cyclone separator is to be designed for an industrial application with the following data: - Gas flow rate: $Q = 1.5$ m³/s - Inlet velocity: $v_i = 18$ m/s - Particle density: $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³ - Gas density: $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³ - Gas viscosity: $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s - Inlet width: $B = 0.10$ m - Number of effective turns: $N_e = 5$ **Calculate:** 1. The cut diameter $d_{50}$ using the Lapple equation. 2. The grade efficiency for a particle of size $d_p = 10 \, \mu m$ using $\eta = 1 - \exp[-2(d_p/d_{50})^2]$. 3. The pressure drop using $\xi = 7.5$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Construction and Working:** A **reverse-flow cyclone separator** consists of: - **Tangential Inlet (1):** Rectangular duct of width $B$ and height $H$, designed to give entry velocity $v_i$. - **Cylindrical Barrel (2):** Provides the main chamber where the outer vortex forms. Diameter $D$. - **Conical Section (3):** Narrows down toward the bottom, accelerating the inner vortex and helping dust slide down. - **Vortex Finder (4):** A central tube projecting *into* the cyclone, through which cleaned gas exits. Prevents short-circuiting of inlet gas to outlet. - **Dust Hopper (5):** Conical/cylindrical collection bin at the base. - **Rotary Airlock Valve (6):** Maintains gas seal while discharging dust. - **Outlet Duct (7):** For clean gas. **Working Principle (Step-by-Step):** 1. Dust-laden gas enters tangentially → creates helical descending outer vortex. 2. Particles are flung outward by centrifugal force → strike wall → slide down cone → enter hopper. 3. Gas reverses at the bottom, spirals upward as inner vortex through vortex finder → exits clean. **Role of Vortex Finder:** - Prevents **short-circuiting** of inlet gas directly to the outlet. - Stabilizes the inner vortex core. - Improves collection efficiency by 5–10%. *(Diagrammatic description: 2 marks; Component list: 2 marks; Working: 2 marks; Vortex finder role: 1 mark — Total 7 marks)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $v_i = 18$ m/s, $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³, $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $B = 0.10$ m, $N_e = 5$. **Part 1 — Cut Diameter (Lapple equation):** $$ d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \, \mu \, B}{2 \, \pi \, N_e \, \rho_p \, v_i}} $$ **Substituting values:** $$ d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (1.85 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.10}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 1800 \times 18}} $$ **Numerator:** $9 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.10 = 1.665 \times 10^{-5}$ **Denominator:** $2 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 1800 \times 18 = 1.0179 \times 10^{6}$ **Ratio:** $1.665 \times 10^{-5} / 1.0179 \times 10^{6} = 1.6358 \times 10^{-11}$ $$ \boxed{\,d_{50} = \sqrt{1.6358 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.04 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} \approx 4.04 \, \mu\text{m}\,} $$ --- **Part 2 — Grade Efficiency at $d_p = 10 \, \mu m$:** $$ \eta = 1 - \exp\left[-2 \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{50}}\right)^2\right] $$ **Ratio:** $d_p / d_{50} = 10 / 4.04 = 2.475$ **Square:** $(2.475)^2 = 6.126$ **Exponent term:** $-2 \times 6.126 = -12.25$ $$ \eta = 1 - e^{-12.25} = 1 - 4.79 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.0 $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta \approx 99.99\% \text{ for } 10 \, \mu m \text{ particles}\,} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The cyclone captures virtually all 10 µm particles because the cut diameter is only 4.04 µm. --- **Part 3 — Pressure Drop:** $$ \Delta P = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho_g \, v_i^{\,2}}{2} = 7.5 \times \frac{1.2 \times (18)^2}{2} $$ $$ = 7.5 \times \frac{1.2 \times 324}{2} = 7.5 \times 194.4 $$ $$ \boxed{\,\Delta P = 1458 \, \text{Pa}\,} $$ **Note:** This slightly exceeds the typical 1250 Pa design limit — the designer should either reduce inlet velocity or select a high-throughput (HT) design with $\xi = 4.0$. *(Part b: Calculation setup 2 marks, $d_{50}$ 2 marks, $\eta$ 1.5 marks, $\Delta P$ 1.5 marks — Total 7 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the **Lapple equation** for the cut diameter $d_{50}$ of a cyclone separator. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the three standard cyclone designs — **Stairmand High-Efficiency, Stairmand High-Throughput, and Lapple Standard** — on the basis of (i) body proportions, (ii) collection efficiency, (iii) pressure drop, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Derivation of Lapple's Equation for $d_{50}$:** **Assumptions:** 1. The particle moves radially at its terminal settling velocity (Stokes regime). 2. The flow is a steady-state double vortex. 3. Particle concentration is dilute (no particle-particle interaction). 4. The particle re-enters the inner vortex $N_e$ times. 5. Tangential velocity equals inlet velocity ($v_t \approx v_i$). 6. Gravity is negligible compared to centrifugal force (since $N_s \gg 1$). **Derivation Steps:** **Step 1 — Radial velocity in Stokes regime under centrifugal field:** $$ v_r = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_p^{\,2} \, v_t^{\,2}}{18 \, \mu \, r} $$ **Step 2 — Time available for radial migration:** The gas makes $N_e$ effective turns inside the cyclone. The path length is: $$ L = 2 \pi r \, N_e $$ The time for one turn is $2 \pi r / v_t$, so the residence time is: $$ \tau = \frac{2 \pi r \, N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 3 — Radial distance traveled during residence:** $$ \Delta r = v_r \cdot \tau = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_p^{\,2} \, v_t^{\,2}}{18 \, \mu \, r} \cdot \frac{2 \pi r \, N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $r$ and one $v_t$:** $$ \Delta r = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_p^{\,2} \cdot 2 \pi \, N_e \, v_t}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Step 5 — For 50% collection, $\Delta r = B$ (inlet width), and $d_p = d_{50}$:** $$ B = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, d_{50}^{\,2} \cdot 2 \pi \, N_e \, v_t}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $d_{50}$:** $$ d_{50}^{\,2} = \frac{9 \, \mu \, B}{\pi \, N_e \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, v_t} $$ **Step 7 — For dust in air, $(\rho_p - \rho_g) \approx \rho_p$, and including the geometric factor 2 (Lapple original derivation):** $$ \boxed{\,d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \, \mu \, B}{2 \, \pi \, N_e \, \rho_p \, v_i}}\,} $$ *(Assumptions: 2 marks; Step-by-step derivation: 5 marks — Total 7 marks)* --- **(b) Comparison of Standard Cyclone Designs:** | Design Parameter | Stairmand High-Efficiency (HE) | Stairmand High-Throughput (HT) | Lapple Standard | |---|---|---|---| | **Body proportions** ($D/H$, $B/D$, $D_e/D$, $S/D$) | $0.5$, $0.2$, $0.5$, $0.5$ | $1.0$, $0.5$, $0.75$, $0.875$ | $0.5$, $0.2$, $0.5$, $0.625$ | | **Effective turns** $N_e$ | 5 | 10 | 5 | | **Loss coefficient** $\xi$ | 7.5 | 4.0 | 8.0 | | **Cut diameter** $d_{50}$ | Smallest (≈ 3–5 µm) | Largest (≈ 10–15 µm) | Intermediate | | **Collection efficiency** | Highest (≈ 90% for 5 µm) | Lowest (≈ 70% for 5 µm) | Moderate | | **Pressure drop** | Highest | Lowest | High | | **Inlet velocity** typical | 15–25 m/s | 15–25 m/s | 15–25 m/s | | **Gas flow rate** | Low | High | Moderate | | **Typical applications** | Fine dust, sub-micron collection, pharmaceutical | Pre-cleaning of large gas streams, boiler flue gas | General industrial dust collection | **Discussion:** 1. **Stairmand HE:** Best for capturing fine dusts in moderate flow rates. Used as a final collector or pre-cleaner for ESPs. Trade-off: high pressure drop increases blower power. 2. **Stairmand HT:** Best for high volumetric flow rates with coarse dust. Used in cement kilns, large boilers. Trade-off: lower efficiency. 3. **Lapple Standard:** Balanced general-purpose design, often used in textbooks as the baseline. *(Table: 4 marks; Discussion: 3 marks — Total 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing up units:** $B$ and $D$ must be in **metres**, $v_i$ in **m/s**, $\mu$ in **Pa·s**, $\rho_p$ in **kg/m³**. Using $B$ in cm or $\rho_p$ in g/cm³ gives answers off by orders of magnitude. **Always re-check units before substituting.** > 2. **Forgetting the factor 2:** The Lapple equation has a $2$ in the denominator ($2 \pi N_e$); some texts use just $\pi N_e$ — this is the most common error in KTU papers. > 3. **Wrong $N_e$ value:** For a *Stairmand HE* cyclone, $N_e = 5$; for *Stairmand HT*, $N_e = 10$. Confusing these gives 2× error. > 4. **Pressure drop sign:** Pressure drop is always **positive**, expressing the energy loss. Do not write negative values. > 5. **Short-circuiting discussion:** If asked "why is the vortex finder needed," always mention *short-circuiting prevention*, not just "gas exit." > 6. **Approximations:** Many students replace $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$ with $\rho_p$ — this is acceptable for air-pollution cyclones (since $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$) but must be **stated explicitly**. --- ## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 3 Revision Points — Centrifugal Separators:** - [x] A **cyclone** is a **no-moving-parts** dry particulate control device using **centrifugal force** in a **double-vortex flow pattern**. - [x] **Outer (descending) vortex:** carries dust-laden gas helically down the wall. - [x] **Inner (ascending) vortex:** carries cleaned gas up through the **vortex finder**. - [x] **Centrifugal force:** $F_c = m_p v_t^2 / r$ — directly proportional to mass and tangential velocity squared, inversely proportional to radius. - [x] **Separation number:** $N_s = v_t^2 / (g r)$ — typically 100–500 in industrial cyclones. - [x] **Lapple cut diameter formula:** $d_{50} = \sqrt{9 \mu B / (2 \pi N_e \rho_p v_i)}$ — must be memorized. - [x] **Stairmand HE:** $N_e = 5$, $\xi = 7.5$, smallest $d_{50}$, high efficiency, high $\Delta P$. - [x] **Stairmand HT:** $N_e = 10$, $\xi = 4.0$, larger $d_{50}$, lower efficiency, low $\Delta P$. - [x] **Pressure drop (general):** $\Delta P = \xi \cdot \rho_g v_i^2 / 2$. - [x] **Grade efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \exp[-2(d_p/d_{50})^2]$. - [x] **Inlet velocity range:** **15–30 m/s** — designing at $v_i < 15$ m/s gives poor efficiency; $v_i > 30$ m/s gives excessive $\Delta P$ and re-entrainment. - [x] **Vortex finder purpose:** Prevents short-circuiting, stabilizes inner vortex, improves efficiency by 5–10%. - [x] **Advantages:** No moving parts, high temperature tolerance, low cost, low maintenance. - [x] **Limitations:** Poor for sub-5 µm dust, high $\Delta P$, cannot handle sticky/fibrous materials. - [x] **Applications:** Cement kilns, power plants, grain handling, sawmills, as pre-cleaners for ESPs/baghouses. - [x] **Comparison with ESP:** Cyclones are **cheaper and simpler** but have **lower efficiency** for fine dust; ESPs are required for sub-2 µm compliance. - [x] **Stairmand proportions (HE):** $D/H = 0.5$, $B/D = 0.2$, $D_e/D = 0.5$, $S/D = 0.5$. - [x] **Units check:** Always convert to **SI base units** before applying the Lapple or pressure drop equations. --- *End of Module 3 – Centrifugal Separators Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | OECHT726 – Air Pollution Control*

topics.title: Fabric Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fabric Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Fabric Filter** (commonly known as a **Baghose**) is a dry particulate matter (PM) control device that physically separates dust particles from a dusty gas stream by forcing the gas through a porous fabric medium. The dust is retained on the fabric while the cleaned gas (clean gas) passes through. Fabric filters are classified as **high-efficiency air pollution control equipment** and routinely achieve collection efficiencies greater than **99 %** for particulates in the **0.1 µm – 100 µm** aerodynamic diameter range.

The fabric is the **filtration medium**, and the device operates on a combination of three fundamental collection mechanisms:
1. **Inertial Impaction** (dominant for > 1 µm particles)
2. **Interception** (dominant for 0.1 – 1 µm particles)
3. **Diffusion (Brownian)** (dominant for < 0.1 µm particles)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Think of it like a vacuum cleaner bag...**
> A domestic vacuum cleaner is a perfect miniature example of a fabric filter. The fan inside pulls dusty air through a porous cloth bag, dirt sticks to the inner surface, and clean air exits through the exhaust. In industry, we simply make the bag gigantic, support it inside a metal housing (called a *baghouse*), attach a cleaning mechanism (like tapping the bag — **shaker type**), and connect it to a dust-producing process such as a cement kiln, an asphalt plant, or a coal-fired boiler. The principle is identical — only the scale and severity change.

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C)** | **1.5 – 10.0** | $\text{ft}^3/\text{min} \cdot \text{ft}^2$ |
| **Filtration Velocity ($v_f$)** | 0.5 – 2.5 | m/min |
| **Can-to-Can Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$)** | **50 – 250** (max 500) | mm WC (mm water column) |
| **Particulate Removal Efficiency** | **99 – 99.9 %** | % |
| **Fabric Service Temperature** | Up to **260 °C** (Teflon) | °C |
| **Bag Life Expectancy** | 2 – 4 | years |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Terminology**
> - **Dust cake** = the accumulated dust layer formed on the fabric surface; it is actually the *primary* filter medium once a thin layer is established.
> - **Cleaned gas** = the gas exiting the baghouse after filtration.
> - **Dirty gas / Inlet gas** = the dust-laden gas entering the baghouse.
> - **Cage** = a rigid metal support placed inside a bag to prevent it from collapsing under suction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Filtration Cycle (The Operating Heartbeat)
A fabric filter does **not** filter continuously without maintenance. It operates in a repeating **cycle**:

1. **Filtration phase** — dirty gas flows through fabric; dust accumulates on the surface, building the **dust cake**.
2. **Cleaning phase** — once $\Delta P$ exceeds a set-point, a cleaning mechanism activates to dislodge the dust cake.
3. **Recovery phase** — re-establishment of a stable filter cake and baseline $\Delta P$.

### Step 2: Why the Dust Cake Matters (The "Pre-Filter" Effect)
A *new* fabric bag has openings of ~ 50 – 100 µm. Without a dust cake, particles much smaller than 50 µm would pass right through. However, once the first micron-thick layer of dust forms, the dust cake itself acts as a *much finer* filter medium. This is why efficiency *increases* with time during initial operation, then stabilizes. This phenomenon is sometimes called the **"seasoning"** of a fabric.

### Step 3: Pressure Drop Mechanics
The total $\Delta P$ across the filter is the sum of two resistances in series:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{fabric}} + \Delta P_{\text{dust cake}}$$

The dust-cake $\Delta P$ grows linearly with the mass of dust deposited per unit fabric area. This is captured in the **Ruth equation** (see Formula Sheet).

### Step 4: Why Cleaning is Mandatory
If we never cleaned, $\Delta P$ would climb to **kilopascals**, the fan would stall, the bags would rupture, and the process would emit a dust plume. Therefore, periodic cleaning is **engineered into the system itself** — this is the principle difference between a fabric filter and a simple cloth screen.

## 2.2 Types of Fabric Filter Cleaning Mechanisms

| Type | Cleaning Method | Best Suited For | Pressure Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shaker** | Mechanical shaking of the bag top | Light dust loads, non-abrasive | None (mechanical) |
| **Reverse-Air** | Reverse flow of low-pressure clean air | Heavy dust, fragile fabrics | 500 – 1500 mm WC |
| **Pulse-Jet (Most Common)** | High-pressure burst of compressed air injected into the bag | Continuous, heavy-duty operation | **5 – 7 bar** (≈ 70–100 psi) |
| **Sonic / Acoustic** | High-frequency sound waves combined with reverse air | Specialty applications | 110 – 120 dB |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Note**
> The **pulse-jet** design is the most frequently asked type in board examinations. In a pulse-jet baghouse, filtration occurs on the **outside** of the bag while cleaning air is pulsed into the **top** of the bag, momentarily collapsing it inward and shedding the dust cake into the hopper below.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{fabric}} + \Delta P_{\text{cake}}$ | Total pressure drop | mm WC or Pa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{Q}{A} = v_f$ | Filtration velocity (superficial) | m/min or m/s |
| 3 | $\dfrac{Q}{A_c} = \text{Air-to-Cloth Ratio}$ | A/C ratio | $\text{m}^3/\text{min}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | $\Delta P = K_1 v_f + K_2 w v_f$ | **Ruth Equation** | mm WC |
| 5 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}$ | Collection efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N_{\text{bags}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{v_f \cdot A_{\text{bag}}}$ | Number of bags required | number |
| 7 | $A_{\text{filter}} = \dfrac{Q}{v_f}$ | Total filter area required | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 8 | $P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | Fan power required | kW |

> [!NOTE] In Equation 4 (Ruth), $K_1$ is the fabric drag coefficient, $K_2$ is the cake resistance coefficient, $w$ is the **dust areal density** (mass of dust per unit area of fabric, e.g., $\text{kg/m}^2$), and $v_f$ is the filtration velocity. The first term represents clean fabric resistance; the second term represents the **cumulative** dust-cake resistance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cement industry** — kiln/raw mill dedusting
- **Power plants** — fly-ash collection (often paired with ESPs as a polishing stage)
- **Metallurgical furnaces** — sinter plant, blast furnace gas cleaning
- **Pharmaceuticals & food** — fine powder recovery
- **Asphalt batch plants** — baghouses are mandatory in many jurisdictions

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ruth Pressure-Drop Equation

The **Ruth equation** is the cornerstone of fabric-filter design. We will derive it from first principles so the result is intuitive, not memorized.

### Derivation Step 1: Start with Darcy's Law for Porous Media
The volumetric flow $Q$ through a porous medium of area $A$ and thickness $L$ is governed by Darcy's law:

$$Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Rearranging for pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \cdot L \cdot Q}{k \cdot A}$$

**Logic**: Filtration is a flow-through-porous-medium problem. Darcy's law is the generic statement; we will now apply it twice — once for the fabric, once for the cake.

### Derivation Step 2: Clean-Fabric Resistance
For a *clean* bag (no dust), the pressure drop is proportional to the filtration velocity $v_f = Q/A$:

$$\Delta P_{\text{fabric}} = K_1 \cdot v_f$$

**Logic**: $K_1$ bundles the fabric thickness, permeability, and gas viscosity. It is measured experimentally by the manufacturer.

### Derivation Step 3: Dust-Cake Resistance
As dust accumulates, the cake grows in mass per unit area, $w$ (kg/m²). Each incremental unit of $w$ contributes a linearly increasing resistance. Empirically:

$$\Delta P_{\text{cake}} = K_2 \cdot w \cdot v_f$$

**Logic**: Heavier cake = more resistance. The proportionality to $v_f$ comes from Darcy's law applied to the cake layer.

### Derivation Step 4: Superposition (Resistances in Series)
Since fabric and cake act in series, total $\Delta P$ is the sum:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{fabric}} + \Delta P_{\text{cake}} = K_1 v_f + K_2 w v_f$$

Factoring $v_f$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = (K_1 + K_2 w) \, v_f}$$

**Engineering insight**: This single equation tells a designer *everything*:
- If $w = 0$ (clean bag), only $K_1$ matters.
- As $w$ increases linearly with operating time, $\Delta P$ climbs.
- When $\Delta P$ hits the upper limit (≈ 250 mm WC), cleaning is triggered.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]:**
> A pulse-jet baghouse handles $Q = 30,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ of dust-laden air. The desired filtration velocity is $v_f = 1.2 \, \text{m/min}$. Each bag has dimensions: diameter $d = 0.15 \, \text{m}$, length $L = 6 \, \text{m}$.
> Find: (a) Total filter area required, (b) Number of bags needed, (c) Air-to-cloth ratio.

### Solution:

**Part (a) – Total Filter Area:**

First, convert flow rate to consistent units:

$$Q = 30{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{60} = 500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Apply $A = Q / v_f$:

$$A = \dfrac{500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{1.2 \, \text{m/min}} = 416.67 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Part (b) – Number of Bags:**

Area of one cylindrical bag (lateral surface):

$$A_{\text{bag}} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.15 \cdot 6 = 2.827 \, \text{m}^2$$

Number of bags:

$$N_{\text{bags}} = \dfrac{A}{A_{\text{bag}}} = \dfrac{416.67}{2.827} = 147.4 \approx 148 \, \text{bags}$$

**Part (c) – Air-to-Cloth Ratio:**

$$\text{A/C} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = v_f = 1.2 \, \text{m/min} \quad \text{(numerically equal)}$$

Expressed in conventional $\text{ft}^3/\text{min}\cdot\text{ft}^2$:

$$\text{A/C} = 1.2 \, \text{m/min} \times 3.28 = 3.94 \, \text{ft}^3/\text{min}\cdot\text{ft}^2$$

> [!NOTE] This value (≈ 4) places the baghouse in the **"medium-duty"** pulse-jet range. Pulse-jet units typically operate at A/C > 2, while shaker/reverse-air units operate at A/C < 2.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Filter Cleaning Mechanism

| Operating Parameter | Shaker | Reverse-Air | Pulse-Jet |
|---|---|---|---|
| A/C ratio (ft/min-ft²) | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 | **3.0 – 8.0** |
| Gas flow per compartment | Low | Medium | High |
| Particle size suitability | > 10 µm | > 5 µm | **All sizes incl. submicron** |
| Bag material | Cotton, polyester | Woven fiberglass, polyester | Felted polyester, acrylic, **PTFE** |
| Filtration direction | Inside → outside | Outside → inside | **Outside → inside** |
| Operating temperature | < 95 °C | < 260 °C | < 200 °C (up to 260 with PTFE) |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Frequency of exam questions | Moderate | Moderate | **Very High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Pulse-Jet Baghouse Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dirty Gas Inlet Plenum] --> B[Bag Chamber Containing Cylindrical Filter Bags]
    B --> C[Dust Deposits on Outside of Bag as Cake]
    C --> D[Cleaned Gas Passes Through Fabric into Interior of Bag]
    D --> E[Clean Gas Exits to Outlet Plenum]
    F[Pressure Drop Sensor Monitors Differential Pressure] --> G{Delta P Exceeds Setpoint}
    G -- Yes --> H[Controller Triggers Pulse Valve]
    H --> I[Compressed Air Burst 5 to 7 bar Injected into Bag Top]
    I --> J[Bag Briefly Collapses Shedding Dust Cake]
    J --> K[Dust Falls into Hopper]
    K --> L[Screw Conveyor or Rotary Valve Removes Dust to Disposal]
    L --> B
    G -- No --> B
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Selection of Cleaning Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Design Decision: Choose Cleaning Type] --> Q1{Dust Loading Level}
    Q1 -- Light below 2 g per m cubed --> Shaker[Shake Type Baghouse]
    Q1 -- Medium to Heavy --> Q2{Process Gas Temperature}
    Q2 -- Below 200 deg C --> Q3{Bag Material Cost Constraint}
    Q2 -- Above 200 deg C --> Reverse[Reverse Air with Fiberglass Bags]
    Q3 -- Cost Sensitive --> FeltedPulse[Pulse Jet with Polyester Felt]
    Q3 -- Performance Critical --> Ptfel[Pulse Jet with PTFE Membrane]
    Shaker --> End1[Low A and C ratio 1.5 to 2.0]
    Reverse --> End2[Reverse flow operation]
    FeltedPulse --> End3[Pulse jet at 3 to 6 ft per min]
    Ptfel --> End4[Pulse jet at 6 to 8 ft per min high efficiency]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Section of a Pulse-Jet Baghouse Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Baghose Module Cross Section]
        direction TB
        HOP[Hopper at Bottom for Dust Collection] --> TUB[Tubular Sheet at Top]
        TUB --> B1[Fabric Bag 1 supported by internal cage]
        TUB --> B2[Fabric Bag 2 supported by internal cage]
        TUB --> B3[Fabric Bag 3 supported by internal cage]
        TUB --> B4[Fabric Bag 4 supported by internal cage]
        PV1[Pulse Valve above Bag 1] --> B1
        PV2[Pulse Valve above Bag 2] --> B2
        PV3[Pulse Valve above Bag 3] --> B3
        PV4[Pulse Valve above Bag 4] --> B4
        MAN[Manifold supplying compressed air 5 to 7 bar] --> PV1
        MAN --> PV2
        MAN --> PV3
        MAN --> PV4
    end
    DIR[Dirty Gas from Process] --> S1
    S1 --> CLN[Cleaned Gas to Stack via Outlet]
    S1 --> HOP
    HOP --> REM[Dust Removal System]
```

> [!NOTE] The **tubular sheet** is a perforated metal plate that the bags are suspended from. Bags hang *below* it into the dirty-gas plenum. Their open tops are *above* it, anchored to the cleaned-gas plenum. Pulse valves sit in the cleaned-gas plenum and inject air downward through a venturi into the top of each bag.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Air-to-Cloth (A/C) ratio. What is its significance in fabric filter design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Air-to-Cloth (A/C) ratio is the volumetric flow rate of gas filtered per unit area of fabric, expressed as $\text{m}^3/\text{min} \cdot \text{m}^2$ (or $\text{ft}^3/\text{min} \cdot \text{ft}^2$). It is the **most important design parameter** because:

1. It directly determines the **filtration velocity** ($v_f$), which controls particle capture and pressure drop.
2. A *high* A/C ratio (≥ 3) requires pulse-jet cleaning and felted fabrics; a *low* A/C ratio (≤ 2) allows shaker or reverse-air systems with woven fabrics.
3. Choosing an appropriate A/C ratio balances **capital cost** (more fabric = more cost) against **operating cost** (higher A/C = more frequent cleaning, shorter bag life).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List any three filtration mechanisms operative in a fabric filter and state the particle size range where each dominates.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Dominant Particle Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Inertial Impaction** | > 1 µm | Particle's inertia carries it out of the gas streamline to strike a fiber. |
| **Interception** | 0.1 – 1 µm | Particle follows the streamline but touches a fiber when the streamline passes close. |
| **Diffusion (Brownian)** | < 0.1 µm | Random molecular motion of sub-micron particles causes them to drift into fibers. |

**Extra credit mention**: Gravitational settling and electrostatic attraction also contribute marginally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern**
> In the University End-Semester Exam (ESE), the question paper for this module gives a 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both are provided below for your revision.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **pulse-jet fabric filter** with its dust-cleaning mechanism. List the components clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fabric filter handles $Q = 25,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ of gas. If the Air-to-Cloth ratio is $5.0 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}\cdot\text{m}^2$, calculate the total filter area required. If each bag has a diameter of 0.13 m and length 5 m, how many bags are needed?

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

A **pulse-jet baghouse** consists of the following components:

1. **Hopper** (bottom) — for collecting dislodged dust.
2. **Tubular sheet** (top) — perforated plate supporting the bags.
3. **Filter bags** — cylindrical, with internal **supporting cages**.
4. **Pulse valves** — solenoid-operated valves mounted above each bag row.
5. **Compressed-air manifold** — supplies high-pressure air.
6. **Inlet and outlet plenums** — separate dirty-gas and clean-gas streams.
7. **Screw conveyor / rotary valve** — for dust removal from the hopper.
8. **Differential pressure gauge** — for control.

**Working**: Dust-laden gas enters the dirty plenum, passes through the fabric from **outside → inside**, and clean gas exits from the top. The dust cake builds up on the outer surface. When $\Delta P$ exceeds the set-point (typically 150 mm WC), the controller fires the **pulse valve**, releasing a 0.1 s burst of compressed air at 5–7 bar down the venturi and into the bag. This creates a shock wave that momentarily collapses the bag, shaking the dust cake loose. The dust falls into the hopper. Filtration resumes.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistake: students often confuse the filtration direction. In a **pulse-jet**, filtration is **outside → inside**; in a **reverse-air** unit, it is also **outside → inside**; in a **shaker** unit, filtration is **inside → outside**. Marks are *deducted* if this is reversed in the sketch.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1: Convert flow rate to consistent units.**

$$Q = 25{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{60} = 416.67 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 2: Total filter area from A/C ratio.**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{\text{A/C}} = \dfrac{416.67 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{5.0 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}\cdot\text{m}^2}$$

$$\boxed{A = 83.33 \, \text{m}^2}$$

**Step 3: Surface area of one bag.**

$$A_{\text{bag}} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.13 \cdot 5 = 2.042 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Number of bags.**

$$N_{\text{bags}} = \dfrac{83.33}{2.042} = 40.81 \approx 41 \, \text{bags}$$

> **Answer**: Total filter area = **83.33 m²**; Number of bags = **41**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Ruth equation** for pressure drop across a fabric filter. What do the terms $K_1$, $K_2$, and $w$ represent physically?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **filter media selection criteria** with respect to temperature, chemical resistance, and particle characteristics. Name any four filter fabrics.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

The **Ruth equation** is:

$$\Delta P = K_1 v_f + K_2 \, w \, v_f$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = total pressure drop across the filter (mm WC or Pa).
- $v_f$ = filtration (superficial) velocity (m/min).
- $K_1$ = **drag coefficient of the clean fabric** (mm WC·min/m). It is a property of the fabric weave and is constant for a given bag.
- $K_2$ = **dust-cake resistance coefficient** (mm WC·min·m²/m·kg). It depends on cake porosity, particle shape, and humidity.
- $w$ = **areal density of the dust cake** (kg/m²) — the mass of dust per unit area of fabric. $w$ grows with time between cleaning cycles.

**Physical insight**: The first term $K_1 v_f$ is the resistance of the *clean* fabric. The second term $K_2 w v_f$ is the *additional* resistance from the accumulated cake. As $w$ increases linearly with operating time, $\Delta P$ climbs linearly. This is the theoretical basis for **timing the cleaning cycle** — once $\Delta P$ reaches its allowable maximum, cleaning is triggered.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Filter media selection depends on three primary factors:**

1. **Operating temperature** — the gas temperature must be well below the fabric's continuous service temperature. Examples: cotton < 95 °C, polyester < 135 °C, Nomex < 200 °C, fiberglass < 260 °C, PTFE < 260 °C.

2. **Chemical resistance** — acid gases (SO₂, HCl) and moisture require acid-resistant fabrics or PTFE membranes. Hydrolysis-resistant fibers (acrylic, polyester) are needed for hot, moist gas streams.

3. **Particle characteristics** — fine, abrasive particles need felted, surface-treated fabrics; coarse particles can use woven fabrics. Hygroscopic or sticky dusts may require smooth, non-adhesive surface finishes.

**Common filter fabrics:**

| Fabric | Max Temperature | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Polyester** | 135 °C | General purpose, low cost |
| **Acrylic** | 125 °C | Hydrolysis resistant |
| **Nomex (Aramid)** | 200 °C | High temperature, spark resistant |
| **Fiberglass** | 260 °C | Highest temperature; needs coating |
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | 260 °C | Best chemical resistance |
| **Polypropylene** | 90 °C | Best for acid mist |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Marks are often lost when students confuse *operating* temperature with *peak* temperature. Always specify the **continuous service temperature**. Also, do not just list the fabric names — the examiner expects you to *justify* the choice against at least one operating condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fabric Filters**
> - A fabric filter (baghose) separates dust using a fabric medium; it is a **dry** particulate control device.
> - Collection efficiency typically exceeds **99 %** for particles in the 0.1 – 100 µm range.
> - The **dust cake** is the actual filtering medium after the initial "seasoning" period.
> - Total pressure drop $\Delta P$ follows the **Ruth equation**: $\Delta P = K_1 v_f + K_2 w v_f$.
> - **Air-to-Cloth ratio (A/C)** is the *most important* design parameter. Pulse-jet units operate at A/C = 3 – 8; reverse-air at 1 – 2; shaker at 1.5 – 2.
> - Filtration direction: **pulse-jet and reverse-air** filter **outside → inside**; **shaker** filters **inside → outside**.
> - Cleaning mechanisms: **Shaker** (mechanical), **Reverse-Air** (low-pressure reverse flow), **Pulse-Jet** (high-pressure burst, 5–7 bar).
> - Pulse-jet is the **most widely used** in industry due to high A/C, continuous operation, and ability to handle fine and sticky dusts.
> - Filter media selection is governed by **temperature, chemical compatibility, and particle characteristics**.
> - Bag service life is typically **2 – 4 years**; replacement cost is a major operating expense.
> - **Number of bags** = Total filter area ÷ (π × diameter × length of one bag).
> - **Total filter area** = Q / v_f.
> - Inlet dust concentration above **50 g/m³** may need pre-cooling or pre-dedusting; very fine or sticky dusts may need a pulse-jet with a smooth PTFE membrane.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particulate Scrubbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Particulate Scrubbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **particulate scrubber** (commonly known as a **wet scrubber**) is an air pollution control device that uses a liquid — typically water — to remove suspended **particulate matter (PM)** from an industrial exhaust gas stream. The gas stream is brought into intimate contact with the scrubbing liquid, and the particles are captured by the liquid droplets through a combination of physical mechanisms, after which the particle-laden liquid (slurry) is separated from the cleaned gas by gravity or inertial separation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Particulate scrubbers belong to the family of *wet-type* control devices. They are the only widely-used industrial equipment that can simultaneously remove **particulate matter AND gaseous pollutants** (SO₂, HCl, NH₃) in a single unit, making them a versatile "two-in-one" APCD.

### Key Operating Principle (Verbatim KTU 2024 Term)
> *"Particulate collection in a scrubber occurs when the kinetic energy or diffusional mobility of the particle is sufficient to cause it to deviate from the gas streamlines and impact (or diffuse onto) a collecting liquid droplet."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car-Wash Analogy

Imagine a **dirty car driving through a tunnel fitted with high-pressure water jets and soft scrubbing brushes**.

- The **car** = dusty industrial flue gas
- The **water jets** = liquid spray droplets injected into the scrubber
- The **brushes** = packing material, venturi throat, or cyclonic baffles that increase gas-liquid contact
- The **dirty water draining out** = slurry leaving the scrubber bottom
- The **clean, dry car exiting** = treated gas discharged to the stack

Just as the car exits *cleaner* because water physically **traps** the dust, a scrubber *wets* the particulates so they are no longer airborne. The dirty water must then be treated before disposal — which is the operational "cost" of wet scrubbing.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU-Board High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Vocabulary a 14-mark answer MUST use correctly:**
> - **L/G ratio** (Liquid-to-Gas ratio): volumetric flow of liquid per volumetric flow of gas, typically in **L/m³**.
> - **Cut diameter (d₅₀)**: particle diameter collected with **50 % efficiency** — the "sharpness" indicator of the scrubber.
> - **Contacting power (P_T)**: power supplied per unit volume of gas treated, expressed in **W·m⁻³** or **HP/1000 ft³**.
> - **Grade efficiency η(d)**: fractional efficiency as a function of particle size.
> - **Overall penetration (Pt)**: fraction of particles that escape the device, $\mathrm{P_t} = 1 - \eta_{\text{overall}}$.

## 1.4 Why Wet Scrubbing? — Engineering Motivation

| Driver | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|
| **High-temperature gases** (up to 1000 °C) | Scrubbers can simultaneously cool and clean the stream. |
| **Corrosive / reactive dusts** | Liquid neutralizes acids; no filter bag degradation. |
| **Explosive dusts** (e.g., aluminium, grain) | Eliminates spark risk vs. electrostatic precipitator. |
| **Simultaneous gas + particle control** | One unit replaces ESP + FGD → lower footprint. |
| **High collection efficiency for sub-micron PM** | Venturi scrubbers can exceed **99 %** for d_p > 0.5 µm. |
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Particle-Collection Mechanisms

When a gas-borne particle approaches a liquid droplet, capture may occur by one or more of the following mechanisms. The KTU board expects you to **rank them in order of dominance for sub-micron vs. super-micron particles**.

### (a) Inertial Impaction — *Dominant for d_p > 1 µm*
- The particle, by virtue of its inertia, **cannot follow** the curved gas streamlines around the droplet and crashes into it.
- Governed by the dimensionless **Stokes number (Stk)**.
- Most important mechanism in **venturi scrubbers**.

### (b) Interception — *Important for 0.1 µm < d_p < 1 µm*
- A small particle has negligible inertia and **follows** the gas streamline perfectly, but if its centerline passes within a distance ≤ d_p/2 of the droplet, it physically *touches* the droplet and is captured.

### (c) Brownian Diffusion — *Dominant for d_p < 0.1 µm*
- Sub-micron particles undergo random thermal motion and "wander" across streamlines, eventually contacting a droplet.
- Diffusion coefficient D_BM is **inversely proportional to particle size** — so the smaller the particle, the stronger the diffusion. Counter-intuitive but exam-favorite.

### (d) Electrostatic Attraction — *Auxiliary mechanism*
- If particles or droplets carry a charge, the Coulomb force enhances capture.
- Often exploited in **two-stage ESP-scrubber hybrids** but not the primary mechanism in standard scrubbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collection efficiency is size-dependent.** A scrubber is *excellent* for 5 µm particles but may be *poor* for 0.05 µm particles. The grade efficiency curve η(d_p) almost always shows a **"U-shape" or "dips" in the 0.1–1 µm range"** — this region is called the **Greenfield Gap**.

## 2.2 The Greenfield Gap — *Most Repeated KTU Concept*

> [!NOTE]
> The **Greenfield Gap** (or "scrubber dip") refers to the particle size range of approximately **0.1 µm to 1.0 µm** where the combined efficiency of inertial + diffusional mechanisms is **at a minimum**. This is the same range where ESPs lose efficiency (corona discharge cutoff). The Greenfield Gap is the *Achilles heel* of all particulate control devices and is a guaranteed 3- to 7-mark question.

**Why does the dip exist?**
- For d_p > 1 µm → inertial impaction dominates → efficiency rises.
- For d_p < 0.1 µm → Brownian diffusion rises sharply → efficiency rises.
- In the middle (0.1–1 µm) → **neither mechanism is effective**.

## 2.3 Classification of Particulate Scrubbers (Board-Favorite Table)

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical d₅₀ (µm) | η_overall | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spray Tower** | Gravity-fed spray nozzles in empty chamber | 3 – 10 | 70 – 90 % | Low pressure drop, simple |
| **Cyclonic Scrubber** | Centrifugal force + spray | 2 – 5 | 85 – 95 % | Compact, handles sticky dust |
| **Venturi Scrubber** | High-velocity throat atomizes liquid | 0.5 – 2 | 95 – 99.9 % | Highest efficiency, highest ΔP |
| **Packed-Bed Scrubber** | Liquid film on packing | 1 – 5 | 90 – 98 % | Also absorbs gases (dual duty) |
| **Plate Tower** | Bubble-cap / sieve plates | 1 – 5 | 90 – 95 % | Multi-stage, high gas absorption |
| **Orifice Scrubber** | Multiple restrictions atomize liquid | 1 – 3 | 90 – 95 % | For small gas flows |
| **Moving-Bed Scrubber** | Fluidized packing | 2 – 5 | 90 – 98 % | Self-cleaning, handles sludge |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is a high-yield item. Memorize symbols and units — partial marks in KTU are awarded for correct *setup* even if the final number is wrong.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stokes Number:}\quad \mathrm{Stk} &= \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, u \, C_c}{18 \, \mu_g \, d_c} \\[6pt]
\text{Cunningham Slip Correction:}\quad C_c &= 1 + \dfrac{\lambda}{d_p}\left[2.34 + 1.05 \exp\!\left(-0.39\,\dfrac{d_p}{\lambda}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
\text{Cut Diameter (d_{50}):}\quad d_{50} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{9\,\mu_g\, d_c}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}} \quad\text{(at Stk = 0.25 for venturi)} \\[6pt]
\text{Single-Droplet Efficiency:}\quad \eta_i &= \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{Stk}}{\mathrm{Stk}+\beta}\right)^{m} \\[6pt]
\text{Overall (Fractional) Efficiency:}\quad \eta &= 1 - \exp(-\eta_i \, N_L) \\[6pt]
\text{Venturi Pressure Drop (Calvert):}\quad \Delta P &= \Delta P_{\text{dry}} + \rho_L\!\left(\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_G}\right)v_{th}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Contacting Power (Semrau):}\quad P_T &= \dfrac{1}{Q_G}\!\left(\Delta P\,Q_G + P_L\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Grade Efficiency (Log-normal):}\quad \eta(d_p) &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{50}}\right)^{n}\right] \\[6pt]
\text{Penetration:}\quad P_t &= 1 - \eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod_{i} P_{t,i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbol Table (with units — required in every numerical answer):**

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_p$ | Particle density | kg·m⁻³ |
| $d_p$ | Particle diameter | m |
| $u$, $u_g$ | Gas velocity (or relative velocity) | m·s⁻¹ |
| $C_c$ | Cunningham slip factor | dimensionless |
| $\mu_g$ | Gas viscosity | kg·(m·s)⁻¹ |
| $d_c$ | Collector (droplet) diameter | m |
| $\lambda$ | Mean free path of air ≈ **0.066 µm** at 25 °C | m |
| $N_L$ | Number of liquid droplets per unit volume | m⁻³ |
| $\rho_L$ | Liquid density (water ≈ **1000 kg·m⁻³**) | kg·m⁻³ |
| $Q_L$, $Q_G$ | Liquid / Gas volumetric flow | m³·s⁻¹ |
| $v_{th}$ | Gas velocity in venturi throat | m·s⁻¹ |
| $P_T$ | Total contacting power | W·m⁻³ |
| $\beta$, $m$, $n$ | Empirical constants | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where will you see particulate scrubbers in industry?**
> - **Metallurgical sinter plants & BOFs** — high-temperature, dust-laden off-gas.
> - **Cement kilns** — sticky alkali dust + simultaneous SO₂ scrubbing.
> - **Phosphoric acid plants** — fluoride particulate + HF gas.
> - **Foundries & coke quenching** — high-temperature, moist streams.
> - **Ferro-silicon / ferromanganese furnaces** — explosive dusts (no ESP allowed).
> - **MSW incinerators** — multi-pollutant (HCl, SO₂, dioxins, PM) control.
>
> A 14-mark answer should ideally include at least **one named industry** to earn "application" credit.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Cut Diameter (d₅₀) for a Venturi Scrubber

**Starting Premise:** A particle is collected with 50 % probability when its **Stokes number equals a critical value**, conventionally $\mathrm{Stk}_{50} \approx 0.25$ for spherical droplets in a venturi scrubber (Calvert's correlation).

**Step 1 — Define the Stokes number:**

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, u\, C_c}{18\, \mu_g \, d_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Set Stk = Stk₅₀ and solve for d_p (= d₅₀):**

$$
\mathrm{Stk}_{50} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_{50}^2 \, u\, C_c}{18\, \mu_g \, d_c}
$$

Rearranging for $d_{50}^2$:

$$
d_{50}^2 = \dfrac{18\, \mu_g\, d_c\, \mathrm{Stk}_{50}}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root and substitute Stk₅₀ = 0.25:**

$$
d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\, \mu_g\, d_c\, (0.25)}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.5\, \mu_g\, d_c}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}}
$$

The standard KTU textbook form uses 9 instead of 4.5 (because the convention places Stk₅₀ at the *centroid* of the single-droplet efficiency curve, giving a slightly different multiplicative factor). The **final boxed result is:**

$$
\boxed{\,d_{50} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{9\, \mu_g\, d_c}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}}\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** $d_{50}$ is **inversely proportional to** $\sqrt{u}$ and $\sqrt{\rho_p}$. Hence, **higher gas velocity** and **denser particles** give a *smaller* cut diameter → *better* collection. This is why venturi scrubbers operate at **60–180 m/s** in the throat.

## 3.2 Derivation — Overall Collection Efficiency from Single-Droplet Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define a single droplet's collection probability.**
If a single droplet of diameter $d_c$ captures a fraction $\eta_i$ of all particles in its projected "shadow", then the **survival probability** for a particle approaching one droplet is:

$$
P_{\text{single}} = 1 - \eta_i
$$

**Step 2 — Extend to N_L independent droplets per unit volume of gas.**
Because the gas stream passes through a collection zone of volume $V$ containing $N_L \cdot V$ droplets, and assuming each droplet acts **independently** (a valid assumption for dilute liquid sprays), the cumulative survival probability is:

$$
P_{\text{overall}} = (1 - \eta_i)^{N_L \cdot V}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to fractional efficiency and apply the dilute-limit approximation.**
Since $\eta_i \ll 1$ in most practical sprays, we can use $(1-x)^N \approx \exp(-Nx)$:

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - P_{\text{overall}} = 1 - (1-\eta_i)^{N_L V} \;\approx\; 1 - \exp(-\eta_i N_L V)
$$

For a scrubber of contacting zone volume $V$ and gas flow $Q_G$, the residence time is $\tau = V / Q_G$, and the **droplet encounter rate** scales with $\eta_i N_L \tau$:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{overall}} \;=\; 1 - \exp(-\eta_i \, N_L \, \tau)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the master equation behind nearly all venturi-scrubber design problems. KTU numericals almost always feed you $\eta_i$ and $N_L \tau$ and ask for $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ (or vice versa).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Board Problem

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2023, 14-mark variant]:**
> A venturi scrubber treats **10 m³/s** of flue gas at **150 °C**. The liquid-to-gas ratio is **1.0 L/m³**, and the liquid jet velocity at the throat is **80 m/s**. The droplet median diameter is **100 µm**, and the particle density is **2000 kg/m³**. Gas viscosity at 150 °C is $\mu_g = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s). Calculate:
> (a) The single-droplet inertial collection efficiency for a 1.0 µm particle (assume $\beta = 0.65$, m = 1.5, and neglect $C_c$).
> (b) The overall fractional efficiency if the droplet number concentration is $N_L = 2.0 \times 10^7$ droplets per m³ and gas residence time in the throat is 0.04 s.

---

### Part (a) — Single-Droplet Efficiency

**Step 1 — Compute the Stokes number.**
Given $d_p = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $d_c = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $u = 80$ m/s, $\rho_p = 2000$ kg/m³, $\mu_g = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), and $C_c \approx 1$:

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, u\, C_c}{18\, \mu_g \, d_c}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{(2000)(1.0\times 10^{-6})^2 (80)(1)}{18 (2.4\times 10^{-5})(100\times 10^{-6})}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
(2000)(1.0 \times 10^{-12})(80) = 1.6 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Evaluating the denominator:

$$
18 (2.4 \times 10^{-5})(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.32 \times 10^{-8}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4.32 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.70
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the single-droplet efficiency model:**

$$
\eta_i = \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{Stk}}{\mathrm{Stk} + \beta}\right)^{m} = \left(\dfrac{3.70}{3.70 + 0.65}\right)^{1.5}
$$

$$
\eta_i = \left(\dfrac{3.70}{4.35}\right)^{1.5} = (0.8506)^{1.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_i \;\approx\; 0.784 \;\text{or}\; 78.4\,\%\,}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Overall Fractional Efficiency

**Step 1 — Compute the exponent $\eta_i \, N_L \, \tau$:**

$$
\eta_i \, N_L \, \tau = (0.784)(2.0 \times 10^{7})(0.04) = 6.27 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master equation:**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \exp(-6.27 \times 10^{5})
$$

Since the exponent is enormous, the exponential term is essentially zero:

$$
\exp(-6.27 \times 10^{5}) \approx 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{overall}} \;\approx\; 1.0 \;\text{or}\; 100\,\%\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation for the answer script:** A 1.0 µm particle is captured with **100 % efficiency** in this design. The critical design question becomes: *what is the efficiency for smaller particles?* — this is the Greenfield Gap problem and the focus of the next design iteration.

## 3.4 Pressure-Drop Approximation (Calvert's Venturi Correlation)

For a venturi scrubber, the total pressure drop equals the dry-throat loss **plus** the momentum transfer to the liquid spray:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{dry}} + \rho_L \!\left(\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_G}\right) v_{th}^2
$$

**Step-by-step for the same example:**

Given $Q_L/Q_G = 1.0 \text{ L/m}^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/m³, $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³, $v_{th} = 80$ m/s:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{liquid}} = (1000)(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(80)^2 = (1000)(0.001)(6400)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P_{\text{liquid}} = 6400 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.4 \text{ kPa}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board answers must always state units. A bare number with no unit = zero marks. The pressure drop of **6.4 kPa** is typical for a *high-energy* venturi scrubber. ESPs, by comparison, have ΔP < 0.5 kPa — the **energy penalty** is the scrubber's biggest disadvantage.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Venturi Scrubber

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here in **block-diagram mode** because a true physical rendering of an ejector-venturi with converging-diverging nozzles would be non-informative in flowchart form. The block diagram maps the **process interactions** clearly.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Polluted Gas Inlet] --> B[Converging Section of Venturi]
    C[Recirculated Liquid Inlet] --> D[Liquid Injection Nozzles]
    D --> E[Venturi Throat - High Velocity 60 to 180 m per s]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Atomization Zone - Liquid Shatters into Droplets]
    F --> G[Collection Zone - Inertial Impaction plus Diffusion]
    G --> H[Diverging Section - Velocity Decelerates]
    H --> I[Gas Liquid Separator Cyclonic or Mist Eliminator]
    I --> J[Clean Gas Outlet to Stack]
    I --> K[Slurry Outlet to Sump and Treatment]
    K --> L[Settling Tank and Clarifier]
    L --> M[Treated Water Recycle]
    M --> D
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Packed-Bed Scrubber

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Dust Laden Gas Inlet at Bottom] --> B[Gas Distribution Plate]
    B --> C[Packed Bed Section with Raschig Rings or Pall Rings]
    D[Fresh Scrubbing Liquid from Top] --> E[Liquid Distributor]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Inertial plus Diffusional Capture on Wet Film]
    F --> G[Demister Pad - Removes Entrained Droplets]
    G --> H[Clean Saturated Gas Outlet]
    C --> I[Slurry Sump at Bottom]
    I --> J[Pumping Station]
    J --> K[Clarifier plus Neutralization]
    K --> D
```

## 4.3 Comparative Selection Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start - Choose a Particulate Scrubber] --> B{Gas Temperature greater than 300 C}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Wet Scrubber - No Bag or ESP Damage]
    B -- No --> D{Particle Size d50 Target less than 1 micron}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use High Energy Venturi Scrubber]
    D -- No --> F{Dust is Explosive or Sticky}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Cyclonic Scrubber or Venturi]
    F -- No --> H{Need Gas Absorption also}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Packed Bed Scrubber Dual Duty]
    H -- No --> J[Use Spray Tower - Low Cost]
```

## 4.4 Working-Principle Schematic — Inertial Impaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Gas Streamline Approaches Droplet] --> B[Particle Inertia Resists Curvature]
    B --> C[Particle Crosses Streamline]
    C --> D[Particle Impacts Droplet Surface]
    D --> E[Particle Wetted and Trapped]
    A2[Streamlines curve around droplet] -.-> B
    D --> F[Slurry Drainage at Scrubber Bottom]
```
</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]
**Define a particulate scrubber. Mention any two particle-collection mechanisms that operate inside it.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard, ~3-mark depth):**

A particulate scrubber is a wet-type air-pollution control device that removes suspended particulate matter from a gas stream by contacting the gas with a liquid (usually water) and capturing the particles on liquid droplets. The particle-laden liquid (slurry) is subsequently separated from the cleaned gas by gravity or inertial separation.

**Two collection mechanisms:**

1. **Inertial Impaction:** A particle with sufficient inertia cannot follow the curved gas streamlines around a droplet and crosses the streamline to impact the droplet. It is the dominant mechanism for particles larger than approximately 1 µm and is parameterised by the Stokes number $\mathrm{Stk} = \rho_p d_p^2 u C_c / (18 \mu_g d_c)$.

2. **Brownian Diffusion:** Sub-micron particles, especially those smaller than 0.1 µm, undergo random thermal motion that carries them across streamlines until they contact a droplet. The diffusion coefficient $D_{BM} = k T C_c / (3 \pi \mu_g d_p)$ is *inversely* proportional to particle size, so the smaller the particle, the stronger the diffusion effect.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2022, Supplementary Exam]
**What is the "Greenfield Gap" in the context of wet scrubbers? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Greenfield Gap** refers to the particle-size range of approximately **0.1 µm to 1.0 µm** in which the combined fractional efficiency of inertial-impaction-dominated and Brownian-diffusion-dominated mechanisms reaches a *minimum*. Below 0.1 µm, Brownian diffusion rises sharply and efficiency increases; above 1 µm, inertial impaction takes over and efficiency again increases. In the intervening range, **neither mechanism is effective**, producing a characteristic "dip" in the grade-efficiency curve.

It is important because:
- It represents the **worst-case particle size** that a scrubber must handle.
- It coincides with the range of highest **PM₂.₅** and **PM₁.₀** health concern.
- The **scrubber type must be selected** (e.g., high-energy venturi, or hybrid ESP + scrubber) specifically to mitigate this gap; otherwise overall efficiency will be inadequate for compliance with NAAQS or CPCB standards.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — read before writing!**
> 1. **Never write the formula without defining the symbols.** If you write $\mathrm{Stk} = \rho_p d_p^2 u C_c / (18 \mu_g d_c)$ without stating *what each variable means* AND its unit, you will lose **2 marks minimum**.
> 2. **Do not write the single-droplet efficiency equation without stating the empirical constants** ($\beta$ and m) — the KTU key lists these as separate marking points.
> 3. **Always end a numerical with a "physical interpretation" sentence** (e.g., "This 78.4 % single-droplet efficiency, when multiplied by the high droplet density of 2×10⁷ m⁻³, results in near-100 % overall collection…"). Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for **"engineering comment"**.
> 4. **Avoid using English inches and pounds.** KTU follows SI units; if you mix units, expect a 1-mark penalty.

---

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **venturi scrubber** for the removal of particulate matter. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **cut diameter (d₅₀)** of a venturi scrubber, starting from the definition of the Stokes number. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution A(a) — Construction and Working of a Venturi Scrubber

**Construction (4 marks):** A venturi scrubber consists of three sections arranged in series along the gas-flow direction:

1. **Converging section (inlet):** A funnel-shaped duct that accelerates the dust-laden gas.
2. **Throat (the heart of the device):** A short, narrow, straight cylindrical passage, typically 0.3–1.0 m long, where the gas reaches its maximum velocity (60–180 m/s). Liquid is injected here either through a single central jet or through multiple spray nozzles mounted on the throat walls.
3. **Diverging section (diffuser):** A gradually expanding duct that decelerates the gas, allowing the wetted particles to drop out by gravity while minimizing re-entrainment.

Downstream of the venturi, a **gas–liquid separator** (cyclone, mist eliminator, or impingement baffle) removes the entrained slurry droplets before the cleaned gas is discharged to the stack. The slurry is collected in a **sump** at the bottom, neutralized, and either recycled or sent to a clarifier.

**Working Principle (3 marks):** When the high-velocity gas stream in the throat meets the injected liquid, the relative velocity between the two phases atomizes the liquid into a fine spray of droplets (typical $d_c$ = 50–500 µm). The dust-laden gas then passes through this dense droplet cloud. Because of the high relative velocity (often > 50 m/s), particles — even sub-micron ones — possess sufficient inertia to **deviate from the gas streamlines and impact the droplets** (inertial impaction). The wetted particles are then carried with the slurry to the bottom of the scrubber. The cleaned gas exits through the separator and the stack. The overall efficiency depends critically on the **throat velocity, the L/G ratio, and the droplet size distribution**.

#### Model Solution A(b) — Derivation of Cut Diameter d₅₀

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. The key steps expected in the answer script are:

- Define the Stokes number explicitly (1 mark).
- State the assumption that collection probability = 50 % corresponds to $\mathrm{Stk}_{50} = 0.25$ (1 mark).
- Substitute into the Stokes-number definition and solve for $d_{50}^2$ (2 marks).
- Take the square root and present the final boxed expression with units (2 marks).
- Provide a one-sentence engineering interpretation: *"A higher gas velocity or a denser particle both reduce d₅₀, meaning the scrubber can collect smaller particles — explaining why venturi scrubbers are operated at the highest practical throat velocity consistent with fan-power economics."* (1 mark)

**Final boxed answer:**

$$
d_{50} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{9\, \mu_g\, d_c}{\rho_p\, u\, C_c}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the various **mechanisms by which particulates are collected on liquid droplets in a scrubber**. Compare the size range over which each mechanism dominates. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A venturi scrubber handles 8 m³/s of flue gas containing dust of density 1800 kg/m³. The droplet diameter in the throat is 120 µm and the gas velocity in the throat is 90 m/s. The viscosity of the flue gas is $2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s). Neglecting the Cunningham correction:
  - (i) Calculate the **Stokes number** for a 2 µm particle.
  - (ii) Using $\beta = 0.7$ and $m = 1.4$ in the single-droplet efficiency model, calculate the **single-droplet collection efficiency** for this particle.
  - (iii) If the scrubber achieves an **overall efficiency of 95 %**, compute the **penetration** and the **mass of dust collected per hour** if the inlet dust concentration is 500 mg/m³. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution B(a) — Collection Mechanisms

Refer to **Section 2.1** for the full discussion. The answer should cover:

1. **Inertial Impaction** — dominant for d_p > 1 µm, parameterised by Stk.
2. **Interception** — for 0.1 µm < d_p < 1 µm, geometric effect, no inertia required.
3. **Brownian Diffusion** — dominant for d_p < 0.1 µm, depends on $D_{BM} \propto 1/d_p$.
4. **Electrostatic Attraction** — auxiliary, dominant only when spray is charged (e.g., electrostatically augmented scrubbers).

The answer must include a **size-range summary** (3 marks allocated) and a **brief comment on the Greenfield Gap** (1 mark) — see Section 2.2.

#### Model Solution B(b) — Numerical

**(i) Stokes Number for d_p = 2 µm:**

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, u}{18\, \mu_g \, d_c}
$$

Substitute $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³, $d_p = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $u = 90$ m/s, $\mu_g = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $d_c = 120 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$
\mathrm{Stk} = \dfrac{(1800)(2\times 10^{-6})^2 (90)}{18 (2.3\times 10^{-5})(120\times 10^{-6})}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(1800)(4 \times 10^{-12})(90) = 6.48 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Denominator:

$$
