18 (2.3 \times 10^{-5})(1.2 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.968 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\mathrm{Stk} \;\approx\; 13.04\,}
$$

**(ii) Single-Droplet Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_i = \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{Stk}}{\mathrm{Stk} + \beta}\right)^{m} = \left(\dfrac{13.04}{13.04 + 0.7}\right)^{1.4}
$$

$$
\eta_i = \left(\dfrac{13.04}{13.74}\right)^{1.4} = (0.9490)^{1.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_i \;\approx\; 0.931 \;\text{or}\; 93.1\,\%\,}
$$

**(iii) Penetration and Dust Mass Collected:**

Penetration:

$$
P_t = 1 - \eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \;=\; \boxed{5\,\%}
$$

Inlet dust mass flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = Q_G \cdot C_{\text{in}} = (8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s})(500 \text{ mg/m}^3) = 4000 \text{ mg/s} = 4 \text{ g/s}
$$

Mass collected per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{collected}} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}} = 4 \text{ g/s} \times 0.95 = 3.8 \text{ g/s}
$$

Mass collected per hour:

$$
m_{\text{collected, hr}} = 3.8 \text{ g/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 13\,680 \text{ g/hr} \approx 13.68 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,m_{\text{collected}} \;\approx\; 13.68 \;\text{kg/hr}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering comment to close the answer (1 bonus mark):** Although a 2 µm particle is captured with 93 % single-droplet efficiency, the **overall efficiency** of 95 % is the *system-level* figure after gas–liquid separation losses, mist-eliminator carry-over, and slurry re-entrainment. The 5 % penetration represents particles that escape to the stack and must be reported in the consent-to-operate compliance log.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-page summary the night before the exam.**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A particulate scrubber is a **wet APCD** that captures dust on liquid droplets.
- ☐ It is the **only APCD that simultaneously removes PM and gaseous pollutants** (SO₂, HCl).
- ☐ Four capture mechanisms: **inertial impaction, interception, Brownian diffusion, electrostatic attraction**.
- ☐ The **Greenfield Gap** is the 0.1 µm – 1.0 µm range of *minimum* collection efficiency.
- ☐ High-temperature, sticky, explosive, or corrosive dusts are the **primary justification** for choosing a wet scrubber over ESP or baghouse.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- ☐ $\mathrm{Stk} = \rho_p d_p^2 u C_c / (18 \mu_g d_c)$
- ☐ $d_{50} = \sqrt{9 \mu_g d_c / (\rho_p u C_c)}$
- ☐ $\eta_i = [\mathrm{Stk} / (\mathrm{Stk} + \beta)]^{m}$
- ☐ $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \exp(-\eta_i N_L \tau)$
- ☐ $\Delta P_{\text{venturi}} = \rho_L (Q_L / Q_G) v_{th}^2 + \Delta P_{\text{dry}}$
- ☐ $P_t = 1 - \eta_{\text{overall}}$

### Equipment Comparison (must-know trade-offs)
- ☐ **Venturi** → highest efficiency, highest ΔP, best for sub-micron PM.
- ☐ **Cyclonic** → compact, handles sticky dust, moderate efficiency.
- ☐ **Packed bed** → dual duty (particles + gases), used in fertilizer and acid plants.
- ☐ **Spray tower** → lowest cost, lowest efficiency, large footprint.

### Common Exam Slip-Ups to Avoid
- ☐ **Forgetting units** on every numerical quantity.
- ☐ **Forgetting to state empirical constants** ($\beta$, m) when using $\eta_i$.
- ☐ **Mixing up d_p (particle) and d_c (collector droplet)** — a 2-mark loss if confused.
- ☐ **Confusing d₅₀ (50 % cut) with d₁₀₀ (100 % capture size)**.
- ☐ **Neglecting the Cunningham correction** when d_p < 1 µm in a "with-correction" question.

### Industries Where Scrubbers Dominate
- ☐ Cement kilns, sinter plants, foundries, coke quenching, MSW incinerators, phosphoric acid plants, ferromanganese/ferro-silicon furnaces.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Precipitators - Operational Considerations - Factors Affecting Selection of Control Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Precipitators - Operational Considerations - Factors Affecting Selection of Control Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):** A particulate control device that uses electrical forces to move charged particles out of a flowing gas stream and onto collection plates. The collected particles are subsequently removed from the plates (typically by rapping) and discharged into hoppers for disposal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an ESP is classified as a **high-efficiency dry particulate control device** capable of handling **very large gas volumes (10⁵ – 10⁶ m³/hr)** with collection efficiencies reaching **> 99.9%** for sub-micron and micron-sized particulates. The standard operating range is **40–200 kV** DC applied across discharge electrodes, producing a non-uniform corona discharge that simultaneously **charges** and **collects** the dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Principle (Two-Stage Process):**
> - **Stage 1 — Corona Charging:** A high-voltage discharge electrode ionizes the surrounding gas, generating negative ions that attach to passing particles.
> - **Stage 2 — Electrostatic Collection:** The now-charged particles migrate under the influence of the electric field toward grounded collection plates, where they are retained.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Magnetic Comb" Concept

Imagine a flowing stream of dust-laden air passing through an invisible "electrostatic comb":

1. The **discharge wire** acts like a lightning rod in miniature — it sprays out invisible negative ions.
2. As dust grains pass this region, each grain becomes negatively charged (just like a balloon rubbed against wool).
3. The **collection plates** act like magnets, but instead of magnetism, they use the attraction between the negatively charged dust and the **grounded (positive-reference) plates**.
4. The dust sticks to the plates and is later knocked off (rapping) like tapping a dusty chalkboard eraser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in ESP Design:**
> - **Migration velocity (ω):** 0.03 – 0.15 m/s (typical industrial range)
> - **Gas velocity in ESP (v):** 1.0 – 2.5 m/s
> - **Residence time:** 2 – 10 seconds
> - **Corona onset voltage:** ~30 kV (for wire-in-tube geometry)
> - **Particle resistivity operating window:** **10⁴ – 10¹¹ Ω·cm** (critical band)
> - **Specific Collection Area (SCA):** 30 – 100 m² per 1000 m³/hr of gas
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Considerations of Electrostatic Precipitators

### A. Corona Discharge Physics
The operational heart of an ESP is the **corona discharge**. When voltage exceeds the **corona onset voltage**, the gas in the immediate vicinity of the discharge electrode ionizes:

- **Negative corona** (most common): electrons are emitted and attach to gas molecules (mostly O₂), forming negative ions.
- The current density at the collection plate is governed by **Townsend's equation** and is critical because insufficient current ⇒ poor charging; excessive current ⇒ **spark-over / back-corona**.

### B. Particle Charging Mechanisms
Two mechanisms operate in parallel:

| Mechanism | Dominant Particle Size | Equation | Notes |
|-----------|------------------------|----------|-------|
| **Field Charging (Boltzmann)** | dₚ > 1 µm | qₚ = 3πε₀dₚ²E₀(t/t+τ) | Dominant for coarse dust |
| **Diffusion Charging** | dₚ < 1 µm | qₚ depends on ion concentration | Dominant for sub-micron fume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** $q_p$ = particle charge (C), $d_p$ = particle diameter (m), $E_0$ = charging field (V/m), $t$ = charging time (s), $\tau$ = time constant (s).

### C. Collection Efficiency — The Deutsch-Anderson Equation (Most Critical KTU Formula)

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\omega \cdot A}{Q}\right)
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency (0 to 1)
- $\omega$ = **migration velocity** (m/s) — drift speed of charged particle toward plate
- $A$ = total collection plate area (m²)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)

**Engineering Interpretation of ωA/Q:**
The ratio $\omega A / Q$ is the **number of transfer units (NTU)**. It is the single most important design parameter an ESP engineer manipulates to hit a target efficiency.

### D. Migration Velocity Derivation (Drift Velocity)

$$
\omega = \frac{q_p \cdot E_p}{3\pi \mu d_p}
$$

Where:
- $q_p$ = particle charge (C)
- $E_p$ = collection field strength at the plate (V/m)
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This equation tells designers that to increase ω, you must (a) increase plate voltage, (b) reduce dust resistivity, or (c) accept higher gas viscosity (warmer gas). This is why **gas conditioning (SO₃ injection)** is used in cement and utility ESPs.

### E. Operational Factors Affecting ESP Performance

| Factor | Effect | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| **Dust Resistivity** | High ρ ⇒ back-corona; Low ρ ⇒ re-entrainment | Gas conditioning with SO₃, NH₃, or H₂O |
| **Particle Size** | < 0.5 µm charged by diffusion; > 5 µm by field | Multi-stage fields |
| **Gas Velocity** | Too high ⇒ re-entrainment; Too low ⇒ cost | Maintain 1.0 – 2.5 m/s |
| **Temperature** | High T ⇒ low viscosity (good) but low ρ (bad) | Operate within 130–180 °C sweet spot |
| **Voltage/Current** | Insufficient ⇒ low charging; Excess ⇒ spark-over | Automatic Voltage Controllers (AVC) |
| **Plate Spacing** | Wider spacing allows higher voltage | 200–400 mm typical |
| **Rapping Frequency** | Over-rapping ⇒ re-entrainment; Under-rapping ⇒ dust build-up & back-corona | Optimize via opacity monitoring |
| **Gas Composition** | Moisture, SO₃ affect particle ρ | Burner tuning / additives |

### F. Back-Corona Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back-Corona Definition:** When dust layer resistivity exceeds **10¹¹ Ω·cm**, the accumulated charged layer on the collection plate generates its own local electric field, reversing polarity at the dust surface and emitting **positive ions** that neutralize incoming negative ions. Result: efficiency collapse (η can drop from 99% → 80%).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for the ESE — they appear in nearly every ESP numerical.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Deutsch-Anderson** | $\eta = 1 - \exp(-\omega A / Q)$ | η (–), ω (m/s), A (m²), Q (m³/s) | All ESP designs |
| 2 | Migration Velocity | $\omega = q_p E_p / (3\pi\mu d_p)$ | qₚ (C), Eₚ (V/m), μ (Pa·s), dₚ (m) | Stokes regime, Re < 1 |
| 3 | SCA (Specific Collection Area) | SCA = A/Q (m² per m³/s) | A (m²), Q (m³/s) | 30–100 m²/(1000 m³/hr) typical |
| 4 | Plate Area | A = n × 2 × L × H | n = number of passages, L (m), H (m) | Geometric design |
| 5 | Residence Time | t = L/v | L (m), v (m/s) | 2–10 s |
| 6 | Volume of ESP | V = A × b | A (m²), b = plate-to-plate spacing (m) | b = 0.2 – 0.4 m |
| 7 | Charging Time Constant | $\tau = 4\varepsilon_0 / (\rho_i \cdot d_p \cdot \mu_i)$ | ρᵢ = ion density, μᵢ = ion mobility | Field charging |
| 8 | Penetration | Pt = exp(-ωA/Q) = 1 - η | For emission calcs | Output basis |

### Real-World Utility of the Deutsch-Anderson Model

The Deutsch-Anderson equation is the **workhorse of ESP engineering** in:
- **Coal-fired thermal power plants** (fly-ash collection, India CPCB compliance)
- **Cement kilns** (kiln/raw mill ESPs)
- **Iron & steel sinter plants**
- **Pulp & paper recovery boilers**

A field engineer uses this equation to determine **how many fields / sections** are required to meet a regulatory emission (e.g., **30 mg/Nm³** PM limit), given a flue gas volume and measured dust resistivity.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deutsch-Anderson Equation

**Assumption:** Plug-flow gas through ESP, all particles have same migration velocity ω, uniform field.

**Step 1 — Particle Mass Balance on Differential Slice dx:**
Consider an ESP of length L, gas velocity v, plate height H, plate-to-plate spacing b.

For a thin slice of thickness dx at distance x from inlet:

$$
\frac{dN}{dx} = -\frac{\omega}{v \cdot b} \cdot N(x)
$$

Where N(x) = number of particles per unit volume at position x.

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables:**

$$
\frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{\omega}{v \cdot b}\, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Integration from x = 0 (inlet) to x = L (outlet):**

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N_L} \frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{\omega}{v \cdot b} \int_0^L dx
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{N_L}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{\omega L}{v b}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Length to Total Plate Area:**

Since each passage has 2 collection plates (one on each side), and there are n passages:
- Total plate area: A = 2 · n · L · H
- Also, gas flow: Q = v · (b · n · H) × n (depends on geometry)
- The geometric ratio $\omega L / (v b)$ simplifies to $\omega A / Q$

**Step 5 — Apply Definition of Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{N_0 - N_L}{N_0} = 1 - \frac{N_L}{N_0}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\omega A}{Q}\right)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Migration Velocity from Coulomb + Stokes Drag

**Step 1 — Forces on Charged Particle:**
Two opposing forces act on a charged particle in the collection zone:
- **Coulomb (electrical) force** pulling it toward the plate: $F_e = q_p E_p$
- **Stokes viscous drag** resisting motion: $F_d = 3\pi\mu d_p \, \omega$

**Step 2 — Terminal (Drift) Velocity Condition:**
At terminal velocity, accelerations vanish and forces balance:

$$
q_p E_p = 3\pi\mu d_p \, \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for ω:**

$$
\omega = \frac{q_p E_p}{3\pi\mu d_p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Since qₚ ∝ dₚ² (field charging regime), ω ∝ dₚ — larger particles are collected faster. This is why ESPs are **excellent for fly-ash** (large particles) but require **assistance (e.g., baghouse)** for very fine sub-micron fumes.

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Worked Example (14-mark style)

**Problem:** An ESP handles Q = 30 m³/s of flue gas. Plate dimensions: L = 10 m, H = 5 m, plate spacing b = 0.25 m, 8 passages (n = 8). Migration velocity ω = 0.10 m/s. Calculate efficiency. If regulation requires 99.5%, what additional plate area is needed?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total plate area:**

$$
A = 2 \cdot n \cdot L \cdot H = 2 \times 8 \times 10 \times 5 = 800 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ωA/Q:**

$$
\frac{\omega A}{Q} = \frac{0.10 \times 800}{30} = 2.667
$$

**Step 3 — Compute η:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp(-2.667) = 1 - 0.0699 = 0.9301 = 93.01\%
$$

**Step 4 — Required η = 99.5% (Pt = 0.005):**

$$
0.005 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\omega A_{\text{new}}}{Q}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(0.005) = -\frac{0.10 \cdot A_{\text{new}}}{30}
$$

$$
-5.298 = -\frac{0.10 \, A_{\text{new}}}{30}
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \frac{5.298 \times 30}{0.10} = 1589.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Additional area:**

$$
\Delta A = 1589.5 - 800 = 789.5 \text{ m}^2 \approx 790 \text{ m}^2
$$

## 3.4 Factors Affecting Selection of Control Equipment — Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks: "Discuss the factors affecting the selection of air pollution control equipment."** Use this 7-factor matrix as your exam template.

| # | Factor | What to Evaluate | Typical Range / Threshold | Preferred Equipment |
|---|--------|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Nature & Type of Pollutant** | Particulate (solid/liquid) vs. Gaseous | Particulate → ESP/Baghouse; Gas → Scrubber | Match to device specialty |
| 2 | **Particle Size Distribution** | dₚ range (µm) | < 2 µm → HEPA/ESPs; 2–20 µm → ESP/Cyclone | ESP: 0.5–50 µm |
| 3 | **Concentration (Inlet Loading)** | g/m³ or mg/Nm³ | < 1 g/m³ → ESP; > 10 g/m³ → Multi-cyclone pre-cleaning | ESP + pre-cleaner |
| 4 | **Required Collection Efficiency** | % removal needed | 90% → Cyclone; 99% → ESP; 99.9% → Baghouse | Higher η → higher cost |
| 5 | **Gas Flow Rate** | Nm³/hr | < 10,000 → Cyclone; 10⁴–10⁶ → ESP | ESP excels at high Q |
| 6 | **Temperature & Pressure** | °C, kPa | T > 400 °C → only ESP/cyclone survive | ESP: up to 700 °C |
| 7 | **Operating Cost & Space** | Capex + Opex; Footprint | Capital vs. Operating trade-off | Decision matrix |

### Additional Selection Criteria (8–12)

| # | Factor | Practical Implication |
|---|--------|------------------------|
| 8 | **Corrosiveness & Reactivity** | Acid mist → fibre-bed; aggressive dust → special alloys |
| 9 | **Humidity / Moisture Content** | Wet gas → scrubber; Dry → ESP / Baghouse |
| 10 | **Explosivity / Flammability** | Grain dust, coal dust → explosion-proof design |
| 11 | **Maintenance Downtime Tolerance** | Continuous process → ESP (low) vs. Baghouse (frequent) |
| 12 | **Regulatory Emission Limit** | mg/Nm³ standard drives η requirement |
| 13 | **Available Footprint** | ESP = large area; Cyclone = compact |
| 14 | **Energy / Utility Availability** | Water-scarce site → dry ESP, not scrubber |

### Decision Logic Flow (Use this in answer writing):

$$
\text{Particulate?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{High Q + High T?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{ESP}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Moderate Q?} \rightarrow \text{Baghouse / Fabric Filter}
$$

$$
\text{Gaseous?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Soluble in water?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Wet Scrubber}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Incinerator / Adsorber}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESP Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Dirty Gas Inlet] --> B[Gas Distribution Plates]
    B --> C[High Voltage Discharge Electrode]
    C --> D[Corona Discharge Zone - Particle Charging]
    D --> E[Collection Plates - Negatively Charged Particles Migrate]
    E --> F[Rapper System - Periodic Dust Removal]
    F --> G[Hopper - Collected Dust Discharge]
    E --> H[Clean Gas Outlet]
    I[High Voltage Transformer Rectifier Set] --> C
    J[Automatic Voltage Controller AVC] --> I
    K[Operational Monitoring - Voltage Current Opacity] --> J
    L[Resistivity Conditioning - SO3 NH3 H2O Injection] --> B
```

## 4.2 Equipment Selection Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pollutant Type?] --> P2{Particulate or Gaseous?}
    P2 -- Particulate --> P3{Particle Size?}
    P2 -- Gaseous --> P4{Water Soluble?}
    P3 -- Coarse > 10 um --> P5[Mechanical Collector or Cyclone]
    P3 -- Fine 0.5 to 10 um --> P6{High Temperature?}
    P3 -- Sub micron --> P7[HEPA or Baghouse]
    P6 -- Yes above 400 C --> P8[ESP - Dry]
    P6 -- No below 400 C --> P9{Baghouse vs ESP}
    P9 -- High Q Dry --> P8
    P9 -- Sticky Hygroscopic --> P10[Baghouses with PTFE membrane]
    P4 -- Yes --> P11[Wet Scrubber]
    P4 -- No --> P12{Combustible?}
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[Thermal Oxidizer]
    P12 -- No --> P14[Adsorption Carbon or Zeolite]
```

## 4.3 Operational Factors Influencing ESP Performance (Cause-Effect Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Dust Resistivity High] --> O1[Back Corona]
    F2[Gas Velocity High] --> O2[Re-entrainment]
    F3[Voltage too Low] --> O3[Poor Charging]
    F4[Voltage too High] --> O4[Spark Over]
    F5[Temperature too High] --> O5[Low Resistivity]
    F6[Moisture Low] --> O6[High Resistivity]
    F7[Particle Size Below 1 um] --> O7[Diffusion Charging Dominant]
    F8[Plate Spacing Wide] --> O8[Higher Allowable Voltage]
    O1 --> R1[Reduce Efficiency]
    O2 --> R1
    O3 --> R1
    O4 --> R1
    O5 --> R1
    O6 --> R1
    R1 --> A1[Gas Conditioning or Multi-field ESP Required]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**
**Q:** Define **Migration Velocity** in the context of an ESP. List any two operational factors that affect it.

**Model Answer:**
Migration velocity (ω) is the **terminal drift velocity** of a charged dust particle toward the collection plate under the influence of the electric field, balanced against Stokes' viscous drag.

$$
\omega = \frac{q_p \, E_p}{3\pi\mu d_p}
$$

**Two operational factors:**
1. **Particle charge (qₚ)** — depends on corona current, particle size, and exposure time.
2. **Gas viscosity (μ)** — function of flue gas temperature (higher T → lower μ → higher ω).

*(Examiner accepts: voltage, resistivity, particle size, gas composition.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**
**Q:** What is **back-corona**? How does dust resistivity influence this phenomenon?

**Model Answer:**
Back-corona is the **local electrical discharge originating from the dust layer** on the collection plate when the dust layer resistivity exceeds **~ 10¹¹ Ω·cm**. The accumulated charge layer generates a sufficiently high electric field across the dust to ionize the gas within the layer, releasing **positive ions** that neutralize incoming negative ions and incoming particles.

- **Low resistivity (< 10⁴ Ω·cm):** Particles leak charge → re-entrainment.
- **Optimal range (10⁴ – 10¹¹ Ω·cm):** Stable operation.
- **High resistivity (> 10¹¹ Ω·cm):** Back-corona → efficiency collapse.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – model paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a two-stage Electrostatic Precipitator**. Discuss the role of the **corona discharge** and **automatic voltage controller (AVC)** in maintaining efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wire-plate ESP handles **Q = 50 m³/s** of flue gas at **150 °C**. Plate area is **1200 m²**. Migration velocity measured is **0.12 m/s**. Compute:
- (i) Collection efficiency
- (ii) Required plate area to achieve **99.8%** efficiency
- (iii) SCA in m² per 1000 m³/hr.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A (a)

**Construction:**
- **Casing:** Steel shell housing the ESP, often with insulation.
- **Discharge Electrodes:** Tungsten/SS wires hanging vertically, connected to negative DC.
- **Collection Electrodes:** Vertically suspended flat plates (0.2–0.4 m apart) grounded.
- **Hoppers:** Pyramidal bottom for dust collection.
- **Rappers:** Impact devices on both wire frames and plates.
- **HV Transformer-Rectifier Set:** Provides –40 to –200 kV DC.

**Working:**
1. Dirty gas enters and passes between plates.
2. **Corona discharge** at the wire generates negative ions.
3. Particles acquire negative charge via field + diffusion charging.
4. Charged particles drift to grounded plates (Coulomb force).
5. Particles are retained on plates; rapped periodically into hoppers.

**Role of AVC:**
- Adjusts secondary voltage to remain **just below spark-over threshold**.
- Maximizes power input under varying dust load & resistivity.
- Prevents arc-over which would trip the ESP and reduce efficiency.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A (b)

**Given:** Q = 50 m³/s, A = 1200 m², ω = 0.12 m/s.

**(i) Collection efficiency:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\omega A}{Q}\right) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.12 \times 1200}{50}\right)
$$

$$
= 1 - \exp(-2.88) = 1 - 0.0561 = 0.9439 = \mathbf{94.39\%}
$$

**(ii) Required A for η = 99.8% (Pt = 0.002):**

$$
0.002 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.12 \cdot A_{\text{new}}}{50}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(0.002) = -\frac{0.12 \, A_{\text{new}}}{50}
$$

$$
-6.2146 = -\frac{0.12 \, A_{\text{new}}}{50}
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \frac{6.2146 \times 50}{0.12} = 2589.4 \text{ m}^2 \approx \mathbf{2590 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Additional area:** ΔA = 2590 − 1200 = **1390 m²** (≈ 1.16 × original).

**(iii) SCA calculation:**

First convert Q to standard units: 50 m³/s × 3600 = 180,000 m³/hr

$$
SCA = \frac{A \times 1000}{Q_{hr}} = \frac{1200 \times 1000}{180{,}000} = 6.67 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per 1000 m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — a typo correction: The convention is $SCA = A / (Q/1000) = A \times 1000/Q$. This gives **6.67 m² per (1000 m³/hr)**. However, KTU conventions vary; some textbooks express SCA in s/m. Standard industrial: **A/Q** in m²·s/m³ = seconds.

Industrial SCA = A/Q = 1200/50 = **24 s** (residence equivalent).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – model paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **operational considerations** of an electrostatic precipitator with emphasis on **dust resistivity, particle size, and gas velocity**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **factors affecting the selection of air pollution control equipment**. Compare the suitability of ESP, baghouse, and cyclone for a **coal-fired boiler** in a thermal power station.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B (a)

**1. Dust Resistivity (ρ):**
- Measured in Ω·cm.
- **Low ρ (< 10⁴):** Particles leak charge easily → re-entrainment → low η.
- **Optimal (10⁴ – 10¹¹):** Stable layer, maximum efficiency.
- **High ρ (> 10¹¹):** Back-corona → efficiency collapse.
- **Mitigation:** SO₃ conditioning, NH₃ conditioning, moisture injection, lowering T below 150 °C.

**2. Particle Size:**
- **Coarse (> 5 µm):** Field charging dominant; collected efficiently.
- **Sub-micron (< 1 µm):** Diffusion charging dominant; charge acquired slowly; harder to collect.
- Industrial observation: ESP efficiency drops sharply below **0.5 µm**.

**3. Gas Velocity:**
- Affects residence time: $t = L/v$.
- **Too high (> 2.5 m/s):** Re-entrainment of rapped dust, reduced efficiency, increased pressure drop.
- **Too low (< 1.0 m/s):** ESP becomes oversized, capital cost rises.
- **Optimum: 1.0 – 2.5 m/s.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors — ESP Efficiency Problems:**
> - **Forgetting** to convert Q from m³/hr → m³/s (factor 3600).
> - Mixing up **SCA** units (m²/(1000 m³/hr) vs. s/m).
> - Using **Pt = η** instead of **Pt = 1 − η** (penetration ≠ efficiency).
> - Ignoring that ω itself varies with T, ρ, and voltage — never treat it as a true constant.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B (b)

**Factors Affecting Selection:**

1. **Pollutant characteristics** (size, concentration, physical state)
2. **Gas flow rate and conditions** (T, P, humidity)
3. **Required collection efficiency** (regulatory limit)
4. **Capital and operating costs**
5. **Available footprint**
6. **Maintenance and downtime tolerance**
7. **Corrosiveness, explosivity, and gas composition**
8. **Energy and utility requirements** (water, electricity)

**Comparison for Coal-Fired Boiler:**

| Parameter | ESP | Baghouse | Cyclone |
|-----------|-----|----------|---------|
| **Efficiency** | 99 – 99.9% | 99.5 – 99.99% | 70 – 90% |
| **Particle Size** | 0.5 – 50 µm | < 1 µm (excellent) | > 10 µm |
| **Pressure Drop** | **Low (0.25 cm H₂O)** | High (15 cm H₂O) | Moderate (5 cm H₂O) |
| **Operating Temp** | Up to 700 °C | Up to 200 °C | Up to 450 °C |
| **Water Use** | None | None | None |
| **Footprint** | Very Large | Moderate | Compact |
| **Maintenance** | Low (off-line cleaning) | High (bag replacement) | Low |
| **Capex** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Opex** | Low | High (bag cost) | Very Low |
| **Suitability for Coal Boiler** | **★ Excellent (utility standard)** | ★ Good (auxiliary option) | Only as pre-cleaner |

**Conclusion:** For coal-fired thermal power plant fly-ash, **ESP is the industry standard** because it handles enormous gas volumes, tolerates high temperatures, has very low pressure drop (saves fan power), and provides > 99.7% efficiency on well-conditioned fly-ash (resistivity ~ 10⁹ Ω·cm).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3 (ESP & Equipment Selection)**

- ✅ **ESP** = high-efficiency dry particulate control device; uses corona + electric field.
- ✅ **Two stages:** Charging (corona) and Collection (Coulomb migration).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-\omega A/Q)$ — Deutsch-Anderson.
- ✅ **Penetration** Pt = exp(-ωA/Q) = 1 − η.
- ✅ **Migration velocity ω** is **NOT constant** — depends on voltage, ρ, T, particle size.
- ✅ **Dust resistivity sweet spot:** 10⁴ – 10¹¹ Ω·cm. Above ⇒ back-corona.
- ✅ **Gas velocity optimal:** 1.0 – 2.5 m/s. Too high ⇒ re-entrainment.
- ✅ **Plate spacing:** 0.2 – 0.4 m; wider spacing allows higher voltage.
- ✅ **Back-corona fix:** SO₃ / NH₃ gas conditioning, lower temperature.
- ✅ **ESP advantage:** Very large Q, low pressure drop, high T tolerance.
- ✅ **ESP disadvantage:** Cannot handle explosive or sticky dusts; large footprint.
- ✅ **Cyclone:** Pre-cleaner for coarse dust only (η = 70–90%).
- ✅ **Baghouse:** Best for sub-micron and sticky dusts; higher pressure drop.
- ✅ **Scrubber:** Only option for **gaseous pollutants** (especially soluble gases).
- ✅ **Selection order of priority:** Pollutant type → Size → Q → T → η required → Cost.
- ✅ **Typical SCA:** 30 – 100 m² per 1000 m³/hr (industrial design).
- ✅ **SCA = A/Q** — gives the *residence equivalent* in seconds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** write only "ESP is efficient." Always quote **numerical η values** (e.g., 99.7%) and reference the **Deutsch-Anderson equation** in long answers.
> - In selection questions, you **MUST mention at least 6 factors** with **examples**; one-line answers will be capped at **70% marks**.
> - Always distinguish **migration velocity ω** from **gas velocity v** — examiners penalize students who use ω and v interchangeably.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution Control Equipment — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution Control Equipment** refers to engineered devices that utilize specific physical or chemical principles (gravity, inertia, filtration, electrostatic force, or mass transfer) to remove particulate matter (PM) and gaseous pollutants from industrial gas streams before they are discharged into the atmosphere.

In **Module 4** of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the **Working Principle** topic demands a thorough understanding of **how each control device operates at a fundamental level** — not merely *what* it does, but *why* it works the way it does. Each device exploits a different force/phenomenon to separate pollutants from a gas stream.

## 1.2 Classification of Control Devices by Working Principle

| Governing Force / Phenomenon | Device | Target Pollutant |
|------------------------------|--------|------------------|
| **Gravity** | Settling Chamber | Coarse particulates (>\>50 µm) |
| **Inertial / Centrifugal** | Cyclone Separator | Medium particulates (5–50 µm) |
| **Interception & Diffusion** | Fabric Filter (Baghouse) | Fine particulates (0.1–10 µm) |
| **Electrostatic Force** | Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) | Fine particulates (0.01–10 µm) |
| **Mass Transfer (Gas–Liquid)** | Wet Scrubber | Particulates + Gaseous SO₂, HCl, NH₃ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen Sieve Metaphor**
> Think of an air pollution control system as a series of "kitchen sieves," each finer than the last. A **settling chamber** is like letting muddy water stand in a glass — heavy sand settles on its own. A **cyclone** is like spinning a salad spinner — water flies outward and leaves the lettuce. A **baghouse** is a fine-mesh tea strainer. An **ESP** is like rubbing a balloon on your hair and using static electricity to attract dust. A **scrubber** is like a car wash — water sprays wash the dirt off.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected)

- **Aeroodynamic Diameter ($d_p$):** Diameter of a sphere with density **1000 kg/m³** having the same settling velocity as the particle.
- **Collection Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of mass of pollutant collected to mass entering the device.
- **Penetration ($P_t$):** Fraction of pollutant escaping, $P_t = 1 - \eta$.
- **Cut Diameter ($d_{50}$):** Particle size collected with **50% efficiency**.
- **Migration Velocity ($\omega$):** Terminal drift velocity of a charged particle toward the collecting electrode in an ESP.
- **Isokinetic Sampling:** Sampling where inlet velocity = gas stream velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Settling Chamber (Gravity)

### Operating Logic
1. Dust-laden gas enters a wide, horizontal chamber at low velocity.
2. Sudden **expansion** drops gas velocity dramatically.
3. Particles, denser than gas, settle under gravity onto the collection hoppers.
4. Cleaned gas exits the opposite end.

### Why It Works
By reducing velocity, the **residence time** becomes greater than the **settling time**, allowing particles to reach the floor.

### Governing Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| Terminal settling velocity (Stokes' regime) | $v_t = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho) d_p^2}{18\mu}$ | $Re_p < 0.1$ (laminar) |
| Terminal settling velocity (general) | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4g(\rho_p - \rho) d_p}{3 C_D \rho}}$ | All regimes |
| Reynolds number of particle | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho v_t d_p}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| Chamber length (minimum) | $L = \dfrac{v_g \cdot H}{v_t}$ | $H$ = chamber height |
| Collection efficiency (single particle) | $\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{v_g \cdot H}$ | Fraction (0–1) |

where:
- $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
- $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho$ = gas density (kg/m³)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient
- $v_g$ = horizontal gas velocity (m/s)
- $H$ = chamber height (m)
- $L$ = chamber length (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Settling chambers are *useless* for particles below **~50 µm** because Stokes' settling velocity becomes negligibly small. They serve as **pre-cleaners** upstream of more efficient devices.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Cyclone Separator

### Operating Logic
1. Gas enters **tangentially** through an inlet duct, creating a swirling vortex.
2. Particles experience **centrifugal force** ($F_c = m v^2 / r$), throwing them outward to the wall.
3. Particles slide down the conical wall into a dust hopper.
4. Cleaned gas spirals upward through the central vortex finder and exits the top.

### Why It Works
The centrifugal acceleration $(v^2/r)$ can be **hundreds to thousands** of times greater than gravity ($g$), making cyclone collection effective for smaller particles than gravity settling.

### Governing Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Critical (cut) particle diameter | $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$ | $B$ = inlet width, $N_e$ = effective turns |
| Pressure drop | $\Delta P = K \cdot \dfrac{\rho v_i^2}{2}$ | $K \approx 8$ for standard cyclone |
| Cut diameter (50% efficiency) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$ | Inverse square-root relation with $v_i$ |
| Fractional efficiency (Lapple) | $\eta(d_p) = \dfrac{(d_p/d_{pc})^2}{1 + (d_p/d_{pc})^2}$ | Simplified model |
| Number of effective turns | $N_e = \dfrac{1}{H}\left[L_b + \dfrac{H}{2}\right]$ | $L_b$ = body length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Cyclone efficiency **decreases** with increasing gas temperature (viscosity rises) and **increases** with increasing inlet velocity — but excessive velocity causes re-entrainment.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Fabric Filter (Baghouse)

### Operating Logic
1. Dust-laden gas passes **outward (or inward) through fabric bags** supported on a wire cage.
2. Particles are captured by **sieving, interception, impaction, and diffusion**.
3. A dust **cake** builds on the fabric, which *itself* becomes the primary filter medium.
4. Periodically, the cake is dislodged by **reverse-air, shake-deflate, or pulse-jet** cleaning.

### Why It Works
Even extremely small particles (0.1 µm) are captured because the **cake structure** has tortuous pores smaller than the particle.

### Governing Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Total pressure drop | $\Delta P = \Delta P_{fabric} + \Delta P_{cake}$ | Both additive |
| Cake pressure drop | $\Delta P_{cake} = K_c \mu v_f w$ | $K_c$ = cake resistance coeff. |
| Fabric pressure drop | $\Delta P_{fabric} = S_e \mu v_f$ | $S_e$ = effective residual drag |
| Combined (Davies) | $\Delta P = S_e \mu v_f + K_c \mu v_f w$ | $w$ = areal dust density (kg/m²) |
| Air-to-cloth ratio | $A/C = Q / A_{cloth}$ | Typical: 0.5 – 2.0 m³/min per m² cloth |
| Filtration velocity | $v_f = Q / A_{cloth}$ | m/s or m/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Filter Cake Behavior:** Higher cake thickness ⇒ higher pressure drop ⇒ higher collection efficiency, BUT also higher operating cost. A balance is struck via periodic cleaning.

## 2.4 Working Principle — Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)

### Operating Logic
1. Particles pass between **discharge electrodes** (negatively charged wires) and **collecting plates** (grounded).
2. **Corona discharge** ionizes gas, generating free electrons that **charge** the particles.
3. Charged particles migrate toward the collecting plates under the electric field.
4. Particles are dislodged by **rapping** the plates and fall into hoppers.

### Why It Works
The electrostatic force ($F = qE$) acts on the **entire particle mass** regardless of inertia — making ESPs highly effective for sub-micron particles that evade cyclones and baghouses.

### Governing Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Deutsch–Anderson Equation** | $\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$ | The single most important ESP equation |
| Migration velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{q E C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction |
| Corona onset voltage | $V_c = m \cdot r_g \ln(S / r_w)$ | $m$ = wire surface irregularity factor |
| Particle charge (field) | $q_f = 3 \pi \varepsilon_0 \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2}\right) E d_p^2$ | Field charging |
| Particle charge (diffusion) | $q_d = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 d_p k T}{e} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{e^2 \bar{v} N_0 t}{8 \varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$ | For <0.5 µm |
| Specific collection area | $SCA = A / Q$ | m² per m³/s; typical: 30–90 s/m |
| Required plate area | $A = -\dfrac{Q}{\omega} \ln(1 - \eta)$ | Rearranged Deutsch–Anderson |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Gold:** The Deutsch–Anderson equation is asked in nearly every KTU examination. The form $\eta = 1 - \exp(-\omega A / Q)$ is the **most-tested** ESP equation.

## 2.5 Working Principle — Wet Scrubber

### Operating Logic
1. Dusty/gas-laden stream contacts a **liquid spray** (water or reagent).
2. Particles/droplets collide via **impaction, interception, diffusion**.
3. Pollutant transfers to liquid phase.
4. Washed gas passes through a **mist eliminator** to remove entrained droplets.
5. Spent liquid (slurry) goes to **wastewater treatment**.

### Why It Works
Liquid droplets are **much larger** and more inertial than gas molecules, making them effective collision partners for fine particles.

### Governing Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Cut diameter (Stokes number method) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_d v_g}{C \rho_p v_{rel}^2}}$ | Scrubber-dependent |
| Scrubber pressure drop | $\Delta P = \rho_g v_g^2 \cdot f$ | $f$ depends on design |
| Overall gas-phase mass balance | $G(Y - Y_0) = L(X_0 - X)$ | $Y$ = gas mol ratio |
| Kremser equation (gas absorption) | $\dfrac{Y - Y^*}{Y_0 - Y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | $A = L / mG$, $m$ = slope of eq. line |
| Collection efficiency (impaction) | $\eta_{imp} = \left(\dfrac{Stk}{Stk + 0.35}\right)^2$ | For $Stk > 0.5$ |

where $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 v_{rel}}{18 \mu_d d_d}$ (Stokes number for droplets of diameter $d_d$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Wet scrubbers are the **only single device** that can simultaneously remove particulates **and** gaseous pollutants (e.g., SO₂ via limestone slurry) — making them indispensable in **thermal power plants** and **metal smelters**.

## 2.6 Comparative Snapshot

| Device | Smallest Effective $d_p$ | Pressure Drop | Energy Use | Wet/Dry |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------|------------|---------|
| Settling Chamber | ~50 µm | Very low | Very low | Dry |
| Cyclone | ~5–10 µm | Moderate (500–1500 Pa) | Low | Dry |
| Baghouse | ~0.1 µm | Moderate–High (1000–2500 Pa) | Medium | Dry |
| ESP | ~0.01 µm | Very low (10–25 mm H₂O) | High (electrical) | Dry |
| Scrubber | ~0.5–2 µm | High (1000–8000 Pa) | High | **Wet** |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Regime)

Consider a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ falling under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$. At **terminal velocity**, the net force is zero.

$$\sum F = 0$$

The three forces acting on the particle are:

**1. Gravitational (downward):**
$$F_g = m g = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_p \, g$$

**2. Buoyancy (upward):**
$$F_b = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho \, g$$

**3. Viscous drag (upward, Stokes' law for $Re_p < 0.1$):**
$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_t$$

Applying force balance at terminal velocity:

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_p \, g - \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho \, g - 3 \pi \mu d_p v_t = 0$$

Simplifying the first two terms:

$$\dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} g (\rho_p - \rho) = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_t$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi d_p$:

$$\dfrac{d_p^2}{6} g (\rho_p - \rho) = 3 \mu v_t$$

Solving for $v_t$:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes' Law interpretation:** $v_t \propto d_p^2$ — halving the particle diameter reduces settling velocity by a factor of **four**. This quadratic dependence is why gravity settling is inefficient for sub-50 µm particles.

## 3.2 Derivation — Minimum Length of a Settling Chamber

We require that a particle of diameter $d_p$ entering at the **top** of the chamber (height $H$) just reaches the **floor** before exiting horizontally.

**Time to settle distance $H$:**
$$t_{settle} = \dfrac{H}{v_t}$$

**Time of gas residence in chamber (length $L$, velocity $v_g$):**
$$t_{gas} = \dfrac{L}{v_g}$$

**Condition for collection:**
$$t_{settle} \le t_{gas}$$

Substituting:
$$\dfrac{H}{v_t} \le \dfrac{L}{v_g}$$

Rearranging to find the **minimum chamber length** $L$ required to capture the particle:

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{v_g \cdot H}{v_t}$$

Substituting Stokes' terminal velocity:

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{18 \mu \, v_g H}{g (\rho_p - \rho) d_p^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To capture 10 µm particles instead of 50 µm particles, the chamber must be **25 times longer** (since $L \propto 1/d_p^2$). This is why settling chambers are physically impractical for fine dust.

## 3.3 Derivation — Deutsch–Anderson Equation for ESP Efficiency

Consider a differential slice of plate area $dA$ perpendicular to gas flow. The gas flow rate through the slice is $Q$ (assumed uniform).

The **migration velocity** $\omega$ is the drift speed of particles perpendicular to gas flow toward the collecting plate. The time a particle spends traversing the slice is:

$$dt = \dfrac{dA \, (\text{volume per unit area})}{Q / A_{total}} \quad \text{(using gas velocity relation)}$$

For a particle, the probability of being collected as it crosses $dA$ depends on how far it must travel versus the migration displacement.

Defining a **mass balance** on the slice: a fraction $d\eta$ of the remaining particles is removed.

The fraction of particles **NOT** collected in traversing $dA$:

$$1 - d\eta \approx e^{-(\omega / v_g) dA}$$

where $v_g = Q / A$ is superficial gas velocity.

Integrating over the total plate area $A$:

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$$

This is the famous **Deutsch–Anderson equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The Deutsch–Anderson equation assumes: (1) uniform gas flow, (2) uniform migration velocity, (3) no re-entrainment. In real ESPs, the **effective migration velocity** $\omega_e$ is empirically 50–80% of theoretical $\omega$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — ESP Sizing

**Problem:** An ESP is to handle a gas flow of $Q = 50$ m³/s. Required collection efficiency is $\eta = 99\%$. Migration velocity for the dust is $\omega = 0.10$ m/s. Determine the required collecting plate area.

**Solution:**

From the Deutsch–Anderson equation:

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$$

Rearranging for $A$:

$$1 - \eta = e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(1 - \eta) = -\dfrac{\omega A}{Q}$$

$$\dfrac{\omega A}{Q} = -\ln(1 - \eta)$$

$$A = -\dfrac{Q}{\omega} \ln(1 - \eta)$$

Substituting $\eta = 0.99$, $Q = 50$ m³/s, $\omega = 0.10$ m/s:

$$A = -\dfrac{50}{0.10} \ln(1 - 0.99)$$

$$A = -500 \cdot \ln(0.01)$$

$$A = -500 \cdot (-4.6052)$$

$$A = 2302.6 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Collection Area:**
> $SCA = A / Q = 2302.6 / 50 = 46.05$ s/m
> This is within the typical industrial range of **30–90 s/m**. ✓

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Cyclone Cut Diameter

**Problem:** A cyclone has inlet width $B = 0.20$ m, effective turns $N_e = 5$, inlet velocity $v_i = 15$ m/s. Particle density $\rho_p = 2200$ kg/m³, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Calculate the **cut diameter** $d_{50}$.

**Solution:**

Using the high-efficiency cyclone cut-diameter equation:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$$

Numerator:
$$9 \mu B = 9 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.20 = 3.24 \times 10^{-5}$$

Denominator:
$$2 \pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho) = 2 \pi \times 5 \times 15 \times (2200 - 1.2)$$
$$= 2 \pi \times 5 \times 15 \times 2198.8$$
$$= 1.036 \times 10^{6}$$

Ratio:
$$\dfrac{3.24 \times 10^{-5}}{1.036 \times 10^{6}} = 3.127 \times 10^{-11}$$

Square root:
$$d_{50} = \sqrt{3.127 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.59 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5.59 \; \mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This cyclone can collect ~50% of particles of size **5.6 µm**, which is typical for a high-efficiency cyclone design.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Device

| Application | Target Pollutant | Recommended Device | Reason |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------|
| Coal-fired boiler | Fly ash (1–100 µm) | ESP or Baghouse | High efficiency on fine PM |
| Cement kiln dust | Alkaline particulates (5–50 µm) | Cyclone (pre-cleaner) + Baghouse | Cost-effective cascade |
| Steel plant sinter | SO₂ + particulates | Wet scrubber (lime slurry) | Simultaneous PM + gas removal |
| Foundry sand-handling | Coarse dust (>50 µm) | Settling chamber | Simple, low-cost |
| Sulphuric acid plant | SO₂ + acid mist | Two-stage scrubber | Gas absorption + mist elimination |
| Wood-working shop | Sawdust (10–500 µm) | Cyclone | Robust, dry, low maintenance |
| Semiconductor cleanroom | Ultra-fine particles | HEPA/ULPA filter | Sub-micron requirement |
