# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of an electrostatic precipitator (ESP) with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An ESP removes particulate matter from a gas stream using **electrostatic force**. The working principle involves the following steps:

1. **Corona Discharge:** A high negative voltage (30–100 kV) is applied to discharge electrodes (wires). This creates a corona discharge that ionizes the surrounding gas, producing free electrons and negative ions.
2. **Particle Charging:** The gas-borne dust particles collide with these ions and acquire a net **negative charge**.
3. **Migration:** Under the influence of the strong electric field between the discharge electrodes (cathode) and the grounded collecting plates (anode), the charged particles migrate toward the plates at a velocity called the **migration velocity ($\omega$)**.
4. **Collection and Rapping:** Particles deposit on the plates, forming a dust layer. Periodic **rapping** dislodges the dust, which falls into hoppers.
5. **Clean Gas Exit:** Purified gas leaves the ESP and proceeds to the stack.

The **Deutsch–Anderson equation** governs collection efficiency: $\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Describe the working principle of a cyclone separator. Why is it preferred as a pre-cleaner in air pollution control systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cyclone separator uses **centrifugal force** to separate particulates from a gas stream.

**Working Principle:**
1. Dust-laden gas enters **tangentially** through a rectangular inlet, generating a swirling vortex inside the cylindrical body.
2. The high swirling velocity creates a strong **centrifugal force** on particles, propelling them outward to the wall.
3. Particles slide down the **conical wall** under gravity and are collected in a bottom **hopper**.
4. The cleaned gas reverses direction, spirals upward as an **inner vortex**, and exits through the central **vortex finder**.

**Why Preferred as Pre-Cleaner:**
- It removes **80–90% of coarser particulates** (>10 µm), reducing the load on downstream high-efficiency devices like baghouses or ESPs.
- It is **dry, low-cost, low-maintenance**, and has **no moving parts**.
- Extends the life of expensive downstream equipment by removing abrasive coarse dust.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**a)** Describe the working principle of a **fabric filter (baghouse)** with a neat diagram. Discuss the mechanisms of particle collection. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for the collection efficiency of an electrostatic precipitator. An ESP handles $Q = 80$ m³/s of flue gas. The migration velocity is $\omega = 0.12$ m/s and the collecting plate area is $A = 3000$ m². Calculate the **overall collection efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **fabric filter** (baghouse) is a dry filtration device that captures particulates by forcing gas through a porous fabric.

**Components:**
- Cylindrical filter bags (felted or woven fabric) supported on wire cages
- Sheet-metal housing divided into a *dirty gas plenum* and a *clean gas plenum*
- Cleaning mechanism (pulse-jet, reverse-air, or shake-deflate)
- Collection hoppers

**Working Principle:**
1. Dust-laden gas enters the **dirty plenum** and flows **outward** (or inward) through the fabric.
2. Particles are captured on the inner (or outer) surface of the bag by **four mechanisms**:
   - **Sieving:** Particles larger than fabric pores are trapped.
   - **Inertial impaction:** Heavy particles deviate from gas streamlines and strike fibers.
   - **Interception:** Particles following streamlines touch fibers as they pass.
   - **Diffusion:** Submicron particles undergo Brownian motion and contact fibers.
3. A **dust cake** forms on the fabric surface. This cake soon becomes the **dominant** filter medium, providing very high efficiency.
4. As the cake thickens, the **pressure drop** rises. Once $\Delta P$ exceeds a threshold, the cleaning cycle is triggered.
5. The dislodged cake falls into **collection hoppers** for disposal.

**Pressure Drop Equation (Davies):**

$$\Delta P = S_e \mu v_f + K_c \mu v_f w$$

where $S_e$ is the residual drag, $\mu$ is gas viscosity, $v_f$ is filtration velocity, $K_c$ is the cake drag coefficient, and $w$ is the areal dust density.

**Filter Classification by Cleaning Method:**

| Type | Mechanism | Application |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Mechanical (shake) | Vibration | Small industrial |
| Reverse-air | Flow reversal | Large utility |
| Pulse-jet | Compressed-air burst | Most common in industry |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of Deutsch–Anderson Equation:**

Consider a differential element of collecting plate of area $dA$ inside the ESP. Let the gas flow rate be $Q$ (m³/s) and let $n$ denote the number concentration of particles in the gas.

The particle **flux** entering the differential slice is $nQ$. The fraction of particles drifting to the plate in time $dt$ is:

$$\dfrac{dn}{n} = -\dfrac{\omega}{v_g} dA = -\dfrac{\omega}{Q/A} dA = -\dfrac{\omega \, dA}{Q / A}$$

Wait — more rigorously, define a **mass balance** on the slice. The probability that a particle is collected in $dA$ is:

$$d\eta = \dfrac{\omega \, dA}{Q}$$

(That is, the rate of removal per unit area is $\omega n$, normalized against the volumetric flow $Q$ — yielding a removal fraction.)

Integrating over the total plate area $A$:

$$\int_0^\eta d\eta = -\dfrac{\omega}{Q} \int_0^A dA$$

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$$

This is the **Deutsch–Anderson equation**.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $Q = 80$ m³/s
- $\omega = 0.12$ m/s
- $A = 3000$ m²

Compute the exponent:

$$\dfrac{\omega A}{Q} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 3000}{80} = \dfrac{360}{80} = 4.5$$

Substitute into Deutsch–Anderson:

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-4.5} = 1 - 0.01111 = 0.9889$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 98.89\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write $\eta = e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$ *with a positive* sign — **forgetting the negative sign in the exponent**. The correct form is $\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$. A sign error here costs **3 marks** immediately.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**a)** Explain the working principle of a **wet scrubber** with a sketch. Derive the **Kremser equation** for gas absorption. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A cyclone separator handles a gas flow of $Q = 1.5$ m³/s with inlet velocity $v_i = 18$ m/s. Inlet width is $B = 0.25$ m, effective turns $N_e = 6$, particle density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, gas viscosity $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Calculate: (i) the **cut diameter** $d_{50}$ and (ii) the **collection efficiency** for a particle of $d_p = 8$ µm using Lapple's model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **wet scrubber** removes particulates and/or gaseous pollutants by contacting the gas stream with a liquid (typically water or a chemical solution).

**Working Principle:**
1. **Dusty/loaded gas** enters the scrubber and contacts a **liquid spray** from nozzles.
2. **Liquid droplets** collide with particles via **impaction, interception, and diffusion** — capturing them.
3. **Gas absorption:** Soluble gases (e.g., SO₂, HCl, NH₃) dissolve into the liquid by mass transfer.
4. The combined **slurry** collects at the bottom for treatment.
5. A **mist eliminator** (demister pad) removes entrained droplets before the gas exits to the stack.

**Kremser Equation Derivation:**

Consider a counter-current absorption column with $N$ ideal stages. The operating line is:

$$Y = Y_0 + \dfrac{L}{G}(X - X_0)$$

For a dilute system with constant slope $m$ of the equilibrium line $Y^* = mX$, the **absorption factor** is:

$$A = \dfrac{L}{mG}$$

By stage-wise mass balance and using the absorption factor:

$$\dfrac{Y_N - Y^*_{N+1}}{Y_0 - Y^*_{N+1}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

For a **packed column** (continuous contact), replacing $N$ with HETP-based equivalent:

$$N_{OL} = \dfrac{Y_0 - Y_1}{\Delta Y_{lm}} \quad \text{(operating-line-based)}$$

Or in the **gas-phase** form:

$$\dfrac{Y_1 - m X_2}{Y_0 - m X_2} = e^{-N_{OG}(1 - 1/A)}$$

For a counter-current arrangement with $A \neq 1$:

$$\dfrac{Y_1 - Y^*}{Y_0 - Y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

This is the **Kremser equation**, where $A = L/(mG)$ is the absorption factor, $N$ is the number of ideal stages, $Y$ is the gas-phase mole ratio, and $X$ is the liquid-phase mole ratio.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $Q = 1.5$ m³/s, $v_i = 18$ m/s, $B = 0.25$ m
- $N_e = 6$, $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $d_p = 8 \;\mu\text{m} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m

**(i) Cut Diameter:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$$

Approximating $\rho_p \gg \rho$ (gas density):

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25}{2 \pi \times 6 \times 18 \times 2500}}$$

Numerator:
$$9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25 = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

Denominator:
$$2 \pi \times 6 \times 18 \times 2500 = 1.6965 \times 10^{6}$$

Ratio:
$$\dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.6965 \times 10^{6}} = 2.652 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{2.652 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{50} \approx 5.15 \; \mu\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Collection Efficiency for $d_p = 8$ µm using Lapple's model:**

First, find $d_{pc}$ (the diameter for which $\eta = 50\%$). Since the problem gives $N_e$ and $B$, we compute:

$$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$$

$$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25}{\pi \times 6 \times 2500 \times 18}}$$

Numerator:
$$9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25 = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

Denominator:
$$\pi \times 6 \times 2500 \times 18 = 8.482 \times 10^{5}$$

Ratio:
$$\dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{8.482 \times 10^{5}} = 5.305 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$d_{pc} = \sqrt{5.305 \times 10^{-11}} = 7.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 7.28 \; \mu\text{m}$$

Now compute $d_p / d_{pc}$:
$$\dfrac{d_p}{d_{pc}} = \dfrac{8}{7.28} = 1.099$$

Lapple's fractional efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{(d_p/d_{pc})^2}{1 + (d_p/d_{pc})^2} = \dfrac{1.099^2}{1 + 1.099^2} = \dfrac{1.208}{2.208} = 0.547$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 54.7\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Cyclone Problems):** Students often confuse the two equations for $d_{50}$ and $d_{pc}$. **$d_{50}$** uses the **factor 2 in the denominator** (i.e., $2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)$), whereas **$d_{pc}$** uses **no factor 2** (i.e., $\pi N_e \rho_p v_i$). Mixing these gives a wrong answer. KTU expects students to clearly state which formula they are using.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Working Principles of Air Pollution Control Equipment**

- **Settling Chamber:** Uses **gravity**; effective for $d_p > 50$ µm; $v_t \propto d_p^2$ (Stokes' law); $L_{min} = v_g H / v_t$.
- **Cyclone Separator:** Uses **centrifugal force**; tangential entry; $d_{50}$ inversely proportional to $\sqrt{v_i}$; Lapple model: $\eta = (d_p/d_{pc})^2 / [1 + (d_p/d_{pc})^2]$.
- **Fabric Filter (Baghouse):** Uses **filtration** + cake; 4 mechanisms (sieving, impaction, interception, diffusion); cleaning via pulse-jet / reverse-air / shake-deflate; $\Delta P = S_e \mu v_f + K_c \mu v_f w$.
- **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):** Uses **electrostatic force**; corona discharge charges particles; **Deutsch–Anderson equation** is the master equation: $\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$; migration velocity $\omega \propto E / \mu$.
- **Wet Scrubber:** Uses **mass transfer**; simultaneous PM + gas removal; gas absorption via **Kremser equation**; counter-current is most efficient.
- **Equations to memorize verbatim for KTU Board:**
  1. Deutsch–Anderson: $\eta = 1 - e^{-(\omega A / Q)}$
  2. Stokes' settling: $v_t = g(\rho_p - \rho) d_p^2 / (18\mu)$
  3. Cyclone cut diameter: $d_{50} = \sqrt{9 \mu B / [2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)]}$
  4. Baghouse $\Delta P$: $\Delta P = S_e \mu v_f + K_c \mu v_f w$
- **Key constants to remember:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², density of water = 1000 kg/m³, density of air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ at NTP.
- **Hierarchy of control efficiency (general):** Settling Chamber < Cyclone < Wet Scrubber < Baghouse ≈ ESP.
- **Cascade design philosophy:** Coarse particles removed first by low-cost devices, then fine particles by high-efficiency units.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always show the *principle of force* in the first line of any working-principle answer, then list the steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Performance Equations of Absorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Performance Equations of Absorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Absorption** is a unit operation in Air Pollution Control Engineering wherein one or more soluble components (solute/pollutant) of a **gas mixture** are selectively dissolved into a **liquid solvent (absorbent)**. The process exploits the difference in solubility between the carrier gas (usually air) and the pollutant species, driving mass transfer across the gas–liquid interface.

In air pollution control, absorption (also called **scrubbing**) is deployed for the removal of:
- **SO₂** from flue gas (using NaOH, Ca(OH)₂, or amine solutions)
- **H₂S** from biogas (using alkanolamines)
- **CO₂** from power-plant emissions (using MEA, MDEA)
- **NOx**, **HCl**, **HF**, and **VOCs** from industrial stacks

The **equilibrium condition** at the gas–liquid interface is governed by **Henry's Law**, which states that at a constant temperature, the partial pressure of a dissolved gas is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid.

$$p_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y_A^* = m \cdot x_A$$

where:
- $p_A$ = partial pressure of solute A in gas (Pa or atm)
- $H$ = **Henry's Law constant** (Pa·mol⁻¹·mol)
- $x_A$ = mole fraction of A in liquid
- $y_A^*$ = equilibrium mole fraction of A in gas
- $m$ = slope of the equilibrium curve = $H/P$ (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For dilute systems (typical of air pollution control where pollutant concentrations are small), the equilibrium relation is linear: $Y^* = mX$, where $Y$ and $X$ are **mole ratios** (not mole fractions) of the solute in the gas and liquid phases, respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a room that has just been sprayed with air freshener. The room air contains pleasant-smelling volatile molecules. Now, you drop a **wet towel** onto the table and leave it overnight. By morning, the air smells much fresher.

What happened?
- The **air freshener molecules** (the **solute/pollutant**) were distributed in the **air** (the **carrier gas**).
- The **water droplets on the towel** (the **liquid solvent/absorbent**) had a strong chemical affinity for these molecules.
- The molecules **migrated from the gas into the liquid** (a process we call **mass transfer**).
- This is exactly what happens in a packed absorption column used for SO₂ or H₂S removal!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The driving force for absorption is the **concentration gradient** between the bulk gas phase and the gas–liquid interface, which in turn is governed by the **equilibrium relationship** (Henry's Law). The greater the deviation from equilibrium, the faster the absorption.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (MUST MEMORISE)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Henry's Constant | $H$ | **atm·m³/kmol** or **Pa/mol-fraction** |
| Equilibrium slope | $m$ | dimensionless |
| Gas molar flow rate | $G$ or $G_s$ | **kmol/s** or **kmol/h** |
| Liquid molar flow rate | $L$ or $L_s$ | **kmol/s** or **kmol/h** |
| Mass transfer coefficient (gas film) | $k_G$ | **kmol/(m²·s·Pa)** |
| Mass transfer coefficient (liquid film) | $k_L$ | **m/s** |
| Overall coefficient (gas basis) | $K_G$ | **kmol/(m²·s·Pa)** |
| Overall coefficient (liquid basis) | $K_L$ | **m/s** |
| Height of a Transfer Unit | $HTU$ | **metre (m)** |
| Number of Transfer Units | $NTU$ | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)

The cornerstone of absorption design is the **Two-Film Theory**. It postulates that:
1. A **stagnant gas film** of thickness $\delta_G$ exists at the gas–liquid interface.
2. A **stagnant liquid film** of thickness $\delta_L$ exists at the same interface.
3. Mass transfer in the **bulk** of each phase is by **turbulent mixing** (negligible resistance).
4. All **resistance** to mass transfer is concentrated in these two thin films.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1:** Solute A in the bulk gas has partial pressure $p_{A,G}$.
- **Step 2:** It diffuses through the stagnant gas film to reach the interface at partial pressure $p_{A,i}$.
- **Step 3:** At the interface, equilibrium exists: $p_{A,i} = H \cdot x_{A,i}$ → so $y_{A,i} = m \cdot x_{A,i}$.
- **Step 4:** Solute then diffuses through the stagnant liquid film from interface concentration $c_{A,i}$ to bulk liquid concentration $c_{A,L}$.

## 2.2 Individual and Overall Mass Transfer Coefficients

The steady-state molar flux of A across both films is identical (no accumulation at interface):

$$N_A = k_G \left( p_{A,G} - p_{A,i} \right) = k_L \left( c_{A,i} - c_{A,L} \right)$$

By combining these with Henry's Law, we obtain **overall coefficients** that bypass the need to know the unknown interface compositions $p_{A,i}$ and $c_{A,i}$:

$$N_A = K_G \left( p_{A,G} - p_A^* \right) = K_L \left( c_A^* - c_{A,L} \right)$$

where $p_A^* = H \cdot c_{A,L}/C$ and $c_A^* = p_{A,G}/H \cdot C$ are **fictitious equilibrium pressures/concentrations** corresponding to bulk liquid/gas compositions.

### The Critical Relationship (KTU Favourite):

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \, k_G}$$

- When $m/k_L \ll 1/k_G$ → **gas-film controlling** (e.g., absorption of a **slightly soluble** gas like O₂ in water). The gas film offers the dominant resistance.
- When $1/(m k_G) \ll 1/k_L$ → **liquid-film controlling** (e.g., absorption of a **highly soluble** gas like NH₃ or SO₂ in water). The liquid film dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For air pollution control applications involving **SO₂ in water** or **CO₂ in amines**, the **liquid film typically controls** the rate of mass transfer. Designers therefore focus on enhancing $k_L$ (e.g., by using structured packings or higher liquid rates).

## 2.3 Operating Line (Material Balance) Derivation

For a counter-current packed absorption column with gas entering at the bottom and liquid entering at the top:

A solute balance over the **entire column** gives:

$$G_s \cdot Y_1 + L_s \cdot X_2 = G_s \cdot Y_2 + L_s \cdot X_1$$

Rearranged into the **linear operating line equation**:

$$Y = \frac{L_s}{G_s} X + \left( Y_2 - \frac{L_s}{G_s} X_2 \right)$$

with slope $= L_s/G_s$ (the **absorption factor L/G** basis). This straight line on an $X$–$Y$ plot, when plotted against the equilibrium curve $Y^* = mX$, defines the **pinch points** at the top and bottom of the column.

## 2.4 Absorption Factor and Stripping Factor

$$\boxed{A = \frac{L_s}{m \cdot G_s}} \quad \text{(Absorption Factor)} \qquad \boxed{S = \frac{1}{A} = \frac{m \cdot G_s}{L_s}} \quad \text{(Stripping Factor)}$$

- **$A > 1$:** Operating line slope > equilibrium line slope → strong **absorbing** condition.
- **$A = 1$:** Operating line and equilibrium line are **parallel** → infinite height required (operating problem).
- **$A < 1$:** Operating line slope < equilibrium slope → **stripping** regime (solute transfers from liquid to gas).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y^* = m x$ | Equilibrium relation (dilute systems) |
| 2 | $N_A = k_G(p_A - p_{A,i})$ | Gas-film flux |
| 3 | $N_A = k_L(c_{A,i} - c_A)$ | Liquid-film flux |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Resistances-in-series (overall) |
| 5 | $G_s(Y_1 - Y_2) = L_s(X_1 - X_2)$ | Material balance (operating line) |
| 6 | $A = L_s / (m G_s)$ | Absorption factor |
| 7 | $\dfrac{Y_1 - m X_2}{Y_2 - m X_2} = A^{N+1}$ | **Kremser Equation** (analytical stages) |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{\ln[(Y_1 - mX_2)/(Y_2 - mX_2)]}{\ln A}$ | Number of ideal stages (Kremser explicit) |
| 9 | $H = (HTU)_{OG} \times (NTU)_{OG}$ | Packed height |
| 10 | $(NTU)_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \dfrac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$ | Colburn equation for NTU |
| 11 | $(HTU)_{OG} = \dfrac{G_s}{K_G \cdot a \cdot P}$ | HTU definition (gas basis) |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{design}$ from Eckert GPD | Packing pressure drop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used to size industrial absorbers like the **Wet Limestone Gypsum (FGD)** scrubbers in thermal power plants (SO₂ removal efficiency > 95%), **Claus-process tail-gas** treaters (H₂S), and **amine-based CO₂ capture units** in CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage) installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient Relationship

We wish to combine the two individual film equations into a single overall flux equation that uses **measurable** bulk concentrations only.

**Given:**
$$N_A = k_G (p_A - p_{A,i}) \quad \cdots (1)$$
$$N_A = k_L (c_{A,i} - c_A) \quad \cdots (2)$$
$$p_{A,i} = H c_{A,i} \quad \cdots (3) \quad \text{(Henry's Law at interface)}$$

**Step 1 — Solve each film equation for the interface concentration:**

From (1):
$$p_{A,i} = p_A - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$

From (2):
$$c_{A,i} = c_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the equilibrium relation (3):**

$$p_A - \frac{N_A}{k_G} = H \left( c_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Expand and group $N_A$ terms on one side:**

$$p_A - H c_A = \frac{N_A}{k_G} + \frac{H \cdot N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $N_A$ on the right-hand side:**

$$p_A - H c_A = N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H}{k_L} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Note that $H c_A = p_A^*$ (fictitious equilibrium pressure):**

$$N_A = \frac{p_A - p_A^*}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{H}{k_L}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the overall gas-based coefficient:**

$$\boxed{N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*) \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H}{k_L}}$$

Since $H/P = m$ (where $P$ is total pressure), and using mole-fraction basis $y = p/P$:

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **resistances-in-addition** model — the most-tested result in KTU absorption problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kremser Equation (Analytical Solution for Stage Requirement)

Consider a counter-current absorption column with $N$ ideal stages. Define:
- $A = L_s / (m G_s)$ = absorption factor
- $Y_1, Y_2$ = inlet and outlet gas mole ratios
- $X_2, X_1$ = inlet and outlet liquid mole ratios

**Stage-by-stage operating line equations** (each stage behaves as an ideal equilibrium contactor):

For stage 1 (bottom): $Y_1 = (L_s/G_s) X_1 + (Y_2 - (L_s/G_s) X_2)$

Let us introduce a **change of variable**: $\bar{Y} = Y - mX$ (deviation from equilibrium).

**Step 1 — Recursive relationship across one ideal stage:**

After one ideal stage, the exiting gas is in equilibrium with the exiting liquid:

$$Y_1^* = m X_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{Y}_1 = Y_1 - mX_1 = 0$$

But the gas entering stage 1 was $Y_0$ (below the actual inlet). The general recursion across $n$ stages is:

$$\bar{Y}_{n+1} = \bar{Y}_n \cdot \left( \frac{L_s}{m G_s} \right) = \bar{Y}_n \cdot A$$

where $A = L_s/(m G_s)$.

**Step 2 — After $N$ ideal stages, the gas deviation from equilibrium is reduced by a factor of $A$ each time:**

$$\bar{Y}_{N+1} = \bar{Y}_1 \cdot A^N$$

where $\bar{Y}_1$ corresponds to the **bottom** entering gas and $\bar{Y}_{N+1}$ to the **top** leaving gas.

**Step 3 — Convert deviations to physical concentrations:**

$$\bar{Y}_1 = Y_1 - m X_1 \qquad \bar{Y}_{N+1} = Y_2 - m X_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the operating-line material balance $G_s(Y_1 - Y_2) = L_s(X_1 - X_2)$:**

Rearranging the operating line: $X_1 = X_2 + (G_s/L_s)(Y_1 - Y_2)$

Thus:
$$\bar{Y}_1 = Y_1 - m\left[ X_2 + \frac{G_s}{L_s}(Y_1 - Y_2) \right] = (Y_1 - mX_2) - \frac{mG_s}{L_s}(Y_1 - Y_2)$$
$$\bar{Y}_1 = (Y_1 - mX_2)\left[ 1 - \frac{mG_s}{L_s} \right] + \frac{mG_s}{L_s}(Y_1 - Y_2) \cdots$$

The clean, KTU-standard result is obtained more directly by **Kremser's original derivation** (see McCabe–Smith–Harriott):

$$\boxed{\frac{Y_1 - m X_2}{Y_2 - m X_2} = A^{N+1} = \left( \frac{L_s}{m G_s} \right)^{N+1}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve explicitly for $N$:**

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[ \dfrac{Y_1 - mX_2}{Y_2 - mX_2} \right]}{\ln(L_s / m G_s)} = \frac{\ln\left[ \dfrac{Y_1 - mX_2}{Y_2 - mX_2} \right]}{\ln A}$$

This is the **analytical number of ideal stages** required for the specified separation.

> [!NOTE]
> When $A = 1$, the denominator is zero, confirming that infinite stages are required. This corresponds to a vertical operating line parallel to the equilibrium curve, requiring infinite height for finite separation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Height of Packing (HTU–NTU Approach)

The **HTU–NTU method** is the **integral approach** to absorption design, used when the operating line and equilibrium line are curved (concentrated solutions, chemical reaction, etc.).

**Step 1 — Starting from a differential material balance on a section $dZ$ of packing:**

$$G_s \, dY = N_A \, a \, dZ$$

where $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume of packing (m²/m³), and $N_A$ is the local flux.

**Step 2 — Express the flux in terms of the overall gas-phase driving force:**

$$N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*) = K_G P (y - y^*) = \frac{K_G P}{1 + Y} (Y - Y^*)$$

For **dilute systems** ($Y \ll 1$, $1 + Y \approx 1$):

$$N_A = K_G P (Y - Y^*)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the differential balance:**

$$G_s \, dY = K_G P a (Y - Y^*) \, dZ$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate from $Y_2$ (top) to $Y_1$ (bottom):**

$$\int_0^Z dZ = \frac{G_s}{K_G P a} \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$$

$$\boxed{Z = (HTU)_{OG} \times (NTU)_{OG}}$$

where the definitions are:

$$(HTU)_{OG} = \frac{G_s}{K_G \cdot a \cdot P} \quad \text{(height of an overall gas-phase transfer unit, in metres)}$$

$$(NTU)_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*} \quad \text{(number of overall gas-phase transfer units, dimensionless)}$$

**Step 5 — For straight operating and equilibrium lines, the NTU integral evaluates to:**

$$(NTU)_{OG} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{\Delta Y_{lm}} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{(Y - Y^*)_{lm}}$$

where the **log-mean driving force** is:

$$(Y - Y^*)_{lm} = \frac{(Y_1 - Y_1^*) - (Y_2 - Y_2^*)}{\ln\left[ \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_1^*}{Y_2 - Y_2^*} \right]}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A packed column absorbs SO₂ from air using water. The inlet gas has $Y_1 = 0.02$ (mol SO₂/mol air) and must be reduced to $Y_2 = 0.0005$. The inlet water is solute-free ($X_2 = 0$). The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 30 X$. Gas molar flow rate $G_s = 0.05$ kmol/s, liquid flow rate $L_s = 1.5$ kmol/s.

**Find:** (a) Absorption factor $A$, (b) Number of ideal stages $N$, (c) Interpretation.

### Solution:

**Part (a):**
$$A = \frac{L_s}{m G_s} = \frac{1.5}{30 \times 0.05} = \frac{1.5}{1.5} = 1.0$$

**Part (b):** $A = 1$ implies **infinite stages required** — the operating line is parallel to the equilibrium curve. This is a **design problem** indicating we must either:
- Increase $L_s$ (higher liquid flow), or
- Decrease $G_s$ (lower gas flow / dilute further), or
- Change solvent to reduce $m$.

**Part (c):** In real engineering, this is why **pure water is rarely used for SO₂ absorption** in large-scale FGD systems. Instead, **alkaline solvents** (NaOH, limestone slurry) are employed to shift the equilibrium and reduce $m$ effectively, allowing finite column heights.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If you ever get $A = 1$ in an absorption problem, **do not panic** — comment on the **design flaw** and suggest a remedy. The examiner awards full marks for identifying the design issue.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Absorption Column — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a counter-current packed absorption column** with all major mass-transfer interfaces, streams, and design variables. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow mapping the interactions in the absorber.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INLETGAS["INLET GAS STREAM: Pollutant Y1 plus Carrier Air"]:::inlet
    OUTLETGAS["OUTLET CLEAN GAS: Reduced Pollutant Y2"]:::outlet
    INLETLIQ["INLET LIQUID SOLVENT: X2 minus Solute"]:::liqin
    OUTLETLIQ["OUTLET RICH SOLVENT: X1 plus Absorbed Solute"]:::liqout

    PKG["PACKED SECTION: Random or Structured Packing"]:::packing
    INTFC["GAS LIQUID INTERFACE: Two Film Theory Region"]:::interface
    REB["REGENERATION SECTION: Stripper or Desorber Optional"]:::reboiler

    KG["OVERALL MASS TRANSFER COEFFICIENT K_G OR K_L"]:::coeff
    HTU["HTU: Height of Transfer Unit m"]:::htu
    NTU["NTU: Number of Transfer Units dimensionless"]:::ntu
    KREM["KREMSER EQUATION: Stage Requirement N"]:::kremser
    OPLN["OPERATING LINE: Slope Ls over Gs"]:::opline
    EQCRV["EQUILIBRIUM CURVE: Y star equals m times X"]:::equil
    ABOX["ABSORPTION FACTOR A equals Ls over m times Gs"]:::absfact

    INLETGAS -->|Enters Bottom| PKG
    PKG -->|Exits Top| OUTLETGAS
    INLETLIQ -->|Enters Top| PKG
    PKG -->|Exits Bottom| OUTLETLIQ

    PKG -.->|Houses| INTFC
    INTFC -.->|Governed by| KG
    KG -.->|Determines| HTU
    HTU -.->|Multiplied by| NTU
    NTU -.->|Yields Packing Height| PKG

    OPLN -->|Compared with| EQCRV
    EQCRV -->|Drives| ABOX
    ABOX -->|Used in| KREM
    KREM -->|Validates| NTU

    OUTLETLIQ -.->|Sent to| REB
    REB -.->|Regenerated Solvent Returned| INLETLIQ

    classDef inlet fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef liqin fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000
    classDef liqout fill:#fff3d6,stroke:#d4ac0d,color:#000
    classDef packing fill:#e8daef,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000
    classDef interface fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#cb4335,color:#000
    classDef reboiler fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef coeff fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000
    classDef htu fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#229954,color:#000
    classDef ntu fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef kremser fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef opline fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef equil fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    classDef absfact fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Two-Film Mass Transfer Interface (Conceptual)

The following conceptual block diagram details the gas–liquid interface showing the **two-film resistance model** used in deriving the overall mass transfer coefficient.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GASBULK["BULK GAS: Partial Pressure pA"]:::gas
    GASFILM["STAGNANT GAS FILM: Resistance 1 over kG"]:::gfilm
    INTFC2["INTERFACE: Equilibrium Henry's Law"]:::intf
    LIQFILM["STAGNANT LIQUID FILM: Resistance 1 over kL"]:::lfilm
    LIQBULK["BULK LIQUID: Concentration cA"]:::liq

    GASBULK -->|Gradient pA to pAi| GASFILM
    GASFILM -->|Reaches| INTFC2
    INTFC2 -->|Equilibrium| LIQFILM
    LIQFILM -->|Gradient cAi to cA| LIQBULK

    classDef gas fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#af601a,color:#000
    classDef gfilm fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#a93226,color:#000
    classDef intf fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef lfilm fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef liq fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Design Procedure Block Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram outlines the **standard step-by-step design procedure** for a packed absorption column.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Define Inlet Outlet Gas Compositions Y1 Y2 plus X2"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2: Determine Equilibrium Slope m from Henry's Law"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3: Compute Minimum Liquid Rate Ls_min from Slope Equation"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4: Select Operating Ls as 1.2 to 2.0 times Ls_min"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5: Compute Absorption Factor A equals Ls over m Gs"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6: Apply Kremser Equation to Find Ideal Stages N"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7: Estimate Murphree Efficiency for Real Stages"]:::step
    S8["STEP 8: Compute Packing Height H equals HTU times NTU"]:::step
    S9["STEP 9: Determine Column Diameter from Flooding Correlation"]:::step
    S10["STEP 10: Check Pressure Drop via Eckert GPD Chart"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    classDef step fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: State and explain Henry's Law. What is its significance in gas absorption design? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Henry's Law states that at constant temperature, the **partial pressure of a dissolved gas in equilibrium with a liquid** is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid:

$$p_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{or} \quad y_A^* = m \cdot x_A$$

where $H$ is the Henry's constant and $m = H/P$ is the dimensionless equilibrium slope.

**Significance in absorption design:**
1. It provides the **equilibrium relationship** between gas and liquid compositions.
2. It determines the **position of the equilibrium curve** on the $X$–$Y$ plot, which is essential for graphical and analytical stage calculations.
3. The value of $m$ dictates whether the gas-film or liquid-film controls mass transfer.
4. A small $m$ (highly soluble gas) favours absorption; a large $m$ (sparingly soluble gas) makes absorption difficult.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between gas-film and liquid-film controlling conditions in absorption. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gas-Film Controlling | Liquid-Film Controlling |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling resistance | $\dfrac{1}{k_G} \gg \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | $\dfrac{m}{k_L} \gg \dfrac{1}{k_G}$ |
| Approximation | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} \approx \dfrac{1}{k_G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} \approx \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ |
| Solute solubility | Low (sparingly soluble gas) | High (very soluble gas) |
| Example | O₂ or CO₂ in water | SO₂ or NH₃ in water |
| Design focus | Increase gas-phase turbulence | Increase liquid rate / contact time |

When a gas is sparingly soluble, most resistance lies in the gas film. When a gas is highly soluble, the liquid film dominates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE CHOICE:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, attempt **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

A counter-current packed absorber is used to remove **H₂S from a biogas stream** using water. The inlet gas contains $Y_1 = 0.04$ kmol H₂S / kmol of clean gas, and the outlet gas must have $Y_2 = 0.0004$. The inlet water is solute-free ($X_2 = 0$). The gas molar flow rate is $G_s = 0.10$ kmol/s, and the liquid molar flow rate is $L_s = 1.8$ kmol/s. The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 50 X$.

**Part (a)** [7 marks]: Calculate the **absorption factor** $A$ and determine the **number of ideal stages** $N$ required using the Kremser equation. Comment on the design.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]: If the actual packing has an **overall Murphree gas-phase efficiency** of 0.75 per stage, determine the **actual number of real stages** required. State the physical significance of Murphree efficiency.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Compute the absorption factor:
$$A = \frac{L_s}{m G_s} = \frac{1.8}{50 \times 0.10} = \frac{1.8}{5.0} = 0.36$$

Since $A < 1$, the system is in a **near-stripping regime** — the solvent capacity is insufficient for the desired removal.

Step 2 — Apply the Kremser equation (since $X_2 = 0$):
$$\frac{Y_1 - m X_2}{Y_2 - m X_2} = A^{N+1}$$
$$\frac{0.04 - 0}{0.0004 - 0} = 0.36^{N+1}$$
$$100 = 0.36^{N+1}$$

Step 3 — Take natural log of both sides:
$$\ln 100 = (N+1) \ln 0.36$$
$$4.6052 = (N+1)(-1.0217)$$
$$N+1 = -4.506 \quad \text{(negative, indicating that for } A < 1 \text{ the equation must be inverted)}$$

Step 4 — **Corrected application (since A < 1):** Use the **inverted** form or solve for the minimum ratio:
$$(N+1) = \frac{\ln(100)}{\ln(1/0.36)} = \frac{4.6052}{\ln 2.778} = \frac{4.6052}{1.0217} = 4.51$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 3.51 \text{ ideal stages} \approx 4 \text{ stages (rounded up)}}$$

**Comment on design:** The value $A = 0.36 < 1$ indicates that the **liquid rate is too low** for effective absorption. In practice, this means the operating line has a flatter slope than the equilibrium line, and the **pinch occurs at the gas inlet**. To improve: increase $L_s$ to push $A > 1.4$ (recommended operating range).

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Apply Murphree gas-phase efficiency:
$$N_{actual} = \frac{N_{ideal}}{E_{MG}} = \frac{3.51}{0.75} = 4.68 \approx 5 \text{ real stages}$$

Step 2 — **Physical significance of Murphree efficiency $E_{MG}$:**
It represents the **fractional approach to equilibrium** achieved in a real contactor stage, defined as:
$$E_{MG} = \frac{Y_n - Y_{n+1}}{Y_n - Y_{n+1}^*}$$
where $Y_{n+1}^*$ is the gas composition that would be in equilibrium with the exiting liquid. It accounts for **finite contact time, non-ideal mixing, and mass transfer limitations** in real packings. Typical $E_{MG}$ for modern structured packings ranges from 0.7 to 0.95.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(Alternative Choice)**

An industrial flue gas containing **SO₂** is to be treated in a packed absorption column using water. The following data are given:
- Inlet gas mole ratio: $Y_1 = 0.025$
- Required outlet gas mole ratio: $Y_2 = 0.001$
- Inlet water is pure: $X_2 = 0$
- Equilibrium: $Y^* = 35 X$
- Gas molar flow rate: $G_s = 0.08$ kmol/s
- Overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient: $K_G a = 0.012$ kmol/(m³·s·atm)
- Operating pressure: $P = 1$ atm

**Part (a)** [7 marks]: Determine the **minimum liquid flow rate** $L_{s,min}$ for complete equilibrium at the gas inlet. Choose an operating $L_s = 1.5 L_{s,min}$ and compute the **absorption factor**.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]: Using the **HTU–NTU approach**, calculate the **required height of packing** for the absorption column, given that the operating and equilibrium lines are straight.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — **Find $L_{s,min}$:** The minimum liquid rate corresponds to the operating line passing through the **pinch point** at the gas inlet $(X_1^*, Y_1)$, where $X_1^* = Y_1/m = 0.025/35 = 7.14 \times 10^{-4}$.

The material balance with $X_2 = 0$ gives:
$$L_{s,min} (X_1^* - 0) = G_s (Y_1 - Y_2)$$
$$L_{s,min} = \frac{G_s (Y_1 - Y_2)}{X_1^*} = \frac{0.08 \times (0.025 - 0.001)}{7.143 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.08 \times 0.024}{7.143 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$L_{s,min} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{-3}}{7.143 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.688 \text{ kmol/s}$$

Step 2 — **Operating liquid rate:**
$$L_s = 1.5 \times L_{s,min} = 1.5 \times 2.688 = 4.032 \text{ kmol/s}$$

Step 3 — **Absorption factor:**
$$A = \frac{L_s}{m G_s} = \frac{4.032}{35 \times 0.08} = \frac{4.032}{2.8} = 1.44$$

This is in the **safe absorption range** ($1.2 < A < 2.0$), confirming a feasible design.

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Compute **HTU**:
$$(HTU)_{OG} = \frac{G_s}{K_G a \cdot P} = \frac{0.08}{0.012 \times 1} = 6.667 \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Compute **NTU** using the log-mean driving force:

First, find the exit liquid composition from the material balance:
$$X_1 = \frac{G_s(Y_1 - Y_2)}{L_s} = \frac{0.08 \times 0.024}{4.032} = 4.762 \times 10^{-4}$$

Equilibrium compositions at the top and bottom:
$$Y_1^* = m X_1 = 35 \times 4.762 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01667$$
$$Y_2^* = m X_2 = 0$$

Driving forces:
$$\Delta Y_1 = Y_1 - Y_1^* = 0.025 - 0.01667 = 0.00833$$
$$\Delta Y_2 = Y_2 - Y_2^* = 0.001 - 0 = 0.001$$

Step 3 — Log-mean driving force:
$$\Delta Y_{lm} = \frac{\Delta Y_1 - \Delta Y_2}{\ln(\Delta Y_1 / \Delta Y_2)} = \frac{0.00833 - 0.001}{\ln(0.00833/0.001)} = \frac{0.00733}{\ln(8.33)} = \frac{0.00733}{2.1198} = 0.00346$$

Step 4 — Number of transfer units:
$$(NTU)_{OG} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{\Delta Y_{lm}} = \frac{0.025 - 0.001}{0.00346} = \frac{0.024}{0.00346} = 6.94$$

Step 5 — **Required packing height:**
$$H = (HTU)_{OG} \times (NTU)_{OG} = 6.667 \times 6.94 \approx 46.27 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 46.3 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $A^{N+1}$ exponent** (writing $A^N$ instead of $A^{N+1}$) — this is a 2-mark trap in Kremser problems.
> 2. **Using mole fractions ($y, x$) instead of mole ratios ($Y, X$)** in dilute-system equations — the operating line equation changes form.
> 3. **Confusing HTU and NTU** — HTU is in metres; NTU is dimensionless. Their product gives packing height $H$ in metres.
> 4. **Inverting the Kremser equation** when $A < 1$ — many students write the negative log incorrectly and get a wrong stage count.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., using atm vs. Pa for $K_G$, or kg/s vs. kmol/s for flow rates).
> 6. **Not stating the assumption** of dilute system when using $Y^* = mX$ — always state this explicitly for examiner satisfaction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MUST MEMORISE for KTU 2024 ESE**

### A. Core Equations (each carries 2–4 marks)

- ✅ **Henry's Law**: $Y^* = mX$ (dilute systems)
- ✅ **Overall coefficient relationship**: $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$
- ✅ **Operating line**: $G_s(Y_1 - Y_2) = L_s(X_1 - X_2)$
- ✅ **Absorption factor**: $A = L_s/(m G_s)$; Stripping factor: $S = 1/A$
- ✅ **Kremser equation**: $\dfrac{Y_1 - mX_2}{Y_2 - mX_2} = A^{N+1}$
- ✅ **Packing height**: $H = (HTU)_{OG} \cdot (NTU)_{OG}$
- ✅ **HTU definition**: $(HTU)_{OG} = G_s / (K_G a P)$
- ✅ **NTU definition**: $(NTU)_{OG} = \int dY / (Y - Y^*) = (Y_1 - Y_2)/\Delta Y_{lm}$ (for straight lines)

### B. Conceptual Pillars

- ✅ Two-film theory gives a **resistances-in-series** model.
- ✅ $A = 1$ → infinite stages; $A > 1$ → absorption feasible; $A < 1$ → stripping regime.
- ✅ **Gas-film controlling** → sparingly soluble gases (CO₂, O₂ in water).
- ✅ **Liquid-film controlling** → highly soluble gases (SO₂, NH₃ in water).
- ✅ For dilute systems, mole-ratio $Y, X$ are interchangeable with mole fractions $y, x$.
- ✅ Murphree efficiency $E_{MG}$ converts ideal stages to real stages: $N_{real} = N_{ideal}/E_{MG}$.

### C. Numerical Safeguards

- ✅ Always quote the **assumptions** (dilute system, isothermal, steady-state, ideal gas).
- ✅ Use **consistent units** (kmol/s, atm, m, m², s).
- ✅ Check whether the column is **counter-current** (most common in KTU problems) or **co-current**.
- ✅ State physical significance of obtained result — examiners reward interpretation.

### D. Real-World Context (for 1–2 mark "Engineering Relevance" sub-questions)

- ✅ SO₂ absorption → **Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD)** in thermal power plants.
- ✅ CO₂ capture → **Amine-based scrubbing** in CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage).
- ✅ H₂S removal → **Amine contactors** in natural gas processing.
- ✅ NOx scrubbing → **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** precursors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface-based mass transfer operation in which molecules of a fluid phase (gas or liquid) — the **adsorbate** — accumulate and adhere onto the surface of a solid substrate — the **adsorbent** — through physical or chemical interactions. It is fundamentally a heterogeneous phenomenon, governed by intermolecular forces acting at the interface between two phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption ≠ Absorption.** In *adsorption*, molecules accumulate *on a surface* (2-D). In *absorption*, molecules penetrate *into the bulk* of a fluid (3-D). Together they form **sorption**.

In air pollution control, adsorption is widely employed for the removal of low-concentration **VOCs**, toxic gases, odors, and trace contaminants from industrial exhaust streams using porous solid adsorbents.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Build-Up)

Imagine you walk into a room sprayed with air freshener (gas molecules bouncing everywhere). You sprinkle fresh coffee powder (porous adsorbent) on a tray and leave it in the room. Within minutes, the strong smell fades — the volatile molecules have stuck to the porous coffee granules.

- **Adsorbate** → volatile fragrance molecules (gas).
- **Adsorbent** → coffee granules (porous solid with high surface area).
- **Adsorption** → molecules "parking" themselves on the internal pore walls.

A more industrial analogy: a **magnetic sponge**. The sponge (adsorbent) attracts iron filings (adsorbate) to its surface — once full, it can be "cleaned" (regenerated) and reused.

### 1.3 Types of Adsorption

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board examinations frequently test the difference between **Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)** and **Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)**. Memorize the distinguishing table.

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Binding Force | Van der Waals forces | Covalent / ionic bonds |
| Enthalpy of Adsorption | **20–40 kJ/mol** (low) | **80–400 kJ/mol** (high) |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Layers Formed | Multi-layer possible | Monolayer only |
| Temperature Effect | Favored at low T | Favored at high T |
| Activation Energy | Negligible | Significant |
| Reversibility | Easily reversible | Often irreversible |

### 1.4 Common Industrial Adsorbents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activated Carbon** is the workhorse of air pollution adsorption. Its internal surface area typically ranges from **500 to 1500 m²/g**.

- **Activated Carbon (AC):** Non-polar, excellent for organic vapors and odors.
- **Silica Gel:** Polar, used for moisture removal (drying).
- **Zeolites (Molecular Sieves):** Uniform pore size, used for gas separation.
- **Activated Alumina:** Used for fluoride and moisture removal.
- **Polymeric Resins:** Used for specialized VOC capture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Five Sequential Steps

1. **Bulk Transport (Film Diffusion):** Adsorbate molecules migrate from the bulk gas stream through the boundary layer surrounding the adsorbent particle.
2. **External Diffusion:** Molecules diffuse across the external film onto the outer surface of the particle.
3. **Intra-particle Diffusion (Pore Diffusion):** Molecules travel through the porous network to internal active sites.
4. **Surface Reaction / Site Attachment:** Molecules physically or chemically bond to active sites.
5. **Desorption (Reverse Path):** During regeneration, the reverse sequence occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU design problem, the **rate-limiting step** (often intra-particle diffusion) controls the overall design.

### 2.2 Adsorption Capacity & Equilibrium Loading

The **adsorption capacity** $q$ (mass of adsorbate per mass of adsorbent) at equilibrium is a function of concentration $C$ and temperature $T$:

$$q = f(C, T)$$

At constant $T$, the relation $q = f(C)$ is called an **adsorption isotherm**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** This table uses `\vert` instead of the pipe character `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (as per V10 protocol).

| # | Equation | Formula | Parameters / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q = \dfrac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$ | $q$: loading (mg/g); $q_m$: max capacity; $K_L$: Langmuir constant (L/mg) |
| 2 | **Langmuir (Linear Form)** | $\dfrac{C}{q} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_m} + \dfrac{C}{q_m}$ | Used for plotting to find $q_m$ and $K_L$ |
| 3 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $q = K_F C^{1/n}$ | $K_F$: capacity factor; $1/n$: intensity factor |
| 4 | **Freundlich (Linear Form)** | $\log q = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C$ | Plot $\log q$ vs $\log C$; slope = $1/n$ |
| 5 | **BET Equation** | $\dfrac{C}{q(C_0 - C)} = \dfrac{1}{K_B q_m} + \dfrac{(K_B - 1)}{K_B q_m}\cdot\dfrac{C}{C_0}$ | $C_0$: saturation concentration; multi-layer model |
| 6 | **Wheeler Breakthrough Equation** | $t_b = \dfrac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot q_m \cdot W}{Q \cdot C_0}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C}{C}\right)$ | $t_b$: breakthrough time (s); $W$: adsorbent mass; $Q$: volumetric flow |
| 7 | **Critical Bed Diameter** | $D_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q}{\pi u_s}}$ | $u_s$: superficial gas velocity (m/s) |
| 8 | **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** | $\text{MTZ} = \dfrac{t_s - t_b}{t_s}\cdot L$ | $L$: bed length; $t_s$: saturation time |
| 9 | **Regeneration Steam Req.** | $M_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{q \cdot W \cdot \lambda}{\Delta H_{\text{ads}} \cdot \eta}$ | $\lambda$: latent heat; $\eta$: thermal efficiency |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adsorption systems are the cornerstone of:
- **Solvent Recovery** in paint and pharma industries (capture of toluene, acetone).
- **Automotive Canisters** — activated carbon traps fuel vapors from petrol tanks.
- **Gas Masks / Respiratory Protection** — military & industrial-grade AC filters.
- **Volatile Organic Compound (VOC) Abatement** in semiconductor fabs.
- **Odor Control** in wastewater treatment plant vents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

**Assumptions (Irving Langmuir, 1918):**
1. The adsorbent surface contains a fixed number of identical, energetically equivalent adsorption sites.
2. Each site can accommodate **only one** adsorbate molecule (monolayer coverage).
3. No lateral interaction exists between adsorbed molecules on adjacent sites.
4. Adsorption is dynamic: molecules continuously adsorb and desorb; at equilibrium the rates are equal.

**Derivation:**

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface sites occupied by adsorbate.

Then $(1-\theta)$ = fraction of vacant sites available.

**Rate of adsorption** $\propto$ (partial pressure) $\times$ (vacant sites):

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{ads}} = k_a \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

**Rate of desorption** $\propto$ (occupied sites):

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{des}} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**At Dynamic Equilibrium**, the two rates are equal:

$$k_a \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

Expanding the left side:

$$k_a \cdot C - k_a \cdot C \cdot \theta = k_d \cdot \theta$$

Collecting $\theta$ terms on one side:

$$k_a \cdot C = \theta \cdot (k_d + k_a \cdot C)$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{k_a \cdot C}{k_d + k_a \cdot C}$$

Define the **Langmuir equilibrium constant** $K_L = \dfrac{k_a}{k_d}$:

$$\theta = \frac{K_L \cdot C}{1 + K_L \cdot C}$$

Since the volume (or mass) of gas adsorbed $q$ is directly proportional to the fraction of surface covered $\theta$, and the maximum monolayer capacity is $q_m$:

$$\frac{q}{q_m} = \theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; q = \frac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$$

**Linearized form (for experimental plotting):**

$$\frac{C}{q} = \frac{1}{K_L q_m} + \frac{C}{q_m}$$

A plot of $\dfrac{C}{q}$ (y-axis) versus $C$ (x-axis) yields a straight line with:
- **Slope** = $\dfrac{1}{q_m}$
- **Intercept** = $\dfrac{1}{K_L q_m}$

### 3.2 Freundlich Isotherm — Empirical Justification

The Freundlich equation has no rigorous derivation; it is empirical and based on the observation that adsorption sites are *heterogeneous* (non-uniform energies). It is expressed as:

$$q = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$$

The linear form is:

$$\log q = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C$$

The constant $n$ is the **heterogeneity index**:
- If $n > 1$: favorable adsorption (desired for industrial use).
- If $n = 1$: linear (reversible) adsorption.
- If $n < 1$: unfavorable adsorption.

### 3.3 Mass Balance on an Adsorption Fixed-Bed (Breakthrough Analysis)

Consider a packed bed of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, through which contaminated gas flows at superficial velocity $u_s$ with inlet concentration $C_0$.

**Step 1 — Total mass of adsorbate entering the bed** in time $t$:

$$M_{\text{in}} = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t = u_s \cdot A \cdot C_0 \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Mass actually captured by the bed** depends on the local loading $q(z,t)$:

$$M_{\text{cap}} = A \cdot \rho_b \int_0^{L} q(z,t) \, dz$$

where $\rho_b$ is the bulk density of the adsorbent bed.

**Step 3 — Mass exiting** through the bed:

$$M_{\text{out}} = Q \int_0^{t} C(L, t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Overall material balance:**

$$M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{cap}} + M_{\text{out}}$$

$$u_s \cdot A \cdot C_0 \cdot t = A \cdot \rho_b \int_0^{L} q(z,t) \, dz + Q \int_0^{t} C(L,t) \, dt$$

This fundamental equation is the starting point for the **Wheeler–Jonas breakthrough equation**, which (in simplified form) gives the **breakthrough time** $t_b$ at which outlet concentration reaches a threshold $C$:

$$t_b = \frac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \frac{\rho_b \cdot q_m \cdot W}{Q \cdot C_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0 - C}{C}\right)$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- First term = stoichiometric time (if every molecule were captured).
- Second term = correction for the non-ideal shape of the mass transfer zone (MTZ).
- As $C \to C_0$ (saturation), $t_b \to t_s = \dfrac{W q_m}{Q C_0}$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An exhaust stream contains **500 mg/m³** of toluene. Air flow rate = **10 m³/min**. Adsorbent bed contains **50 kg** of activated carbon with capacity $q_m = 200$ g/kg and $K_L = 0.05$ m³/mg. Calculate the equilibrium loading at the inlet.

**Solution:**

$$q = \frac{q_m \cdot K_L \cdot C_0}{1 + K_L \cdot C_0}$$

Plug values: $q_m = 200{,}000$ mg/kg; $K_L = 0.05$ m³/mg; $C_0 = 500$ mg/m³.

$$q = \frac{(200{,}000)(0.05)(500)}{1 + (0.05)(500)}$$

$$q = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1 + 25} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{26}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 192{,}308 \text{ mg/kg} \;\;(\approx 192.3 \text{ g/kg})}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Adsorption System

The figure below represents a complete adsorption train as a block diagram, illustrating the sequential process flow from contaminated inlet air to cleaned exhaust.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INFLOW [Contaminated Inlet Stream]
        A1["Polluted Air Inlet"] --> A2["Pre-Filter Particulate Removal"]
        A2 --> A3["Humidity Control Demister"]
    end
    A3 --> B1["Adsorber Vessel Bed"]
    B1 --> B2["Mass Transfer Zone MTZ Active"]
    B2 --> B3["Saturated Zone Exhausted"]
    B3 --> C1["Outlet Cleaned Air"]
    C1 --> C2["Stack Discharge to Atmosphere"]
    subgraph PARALLEL_REGEN [Parallel Regeneration Loop]
        R1["Steam or Hot Nitrogen Inlet"] --> R2["Desorber Vessel"]
        R2 --> R3["Condenser Recovery"]
        R3 --> R4["Separation Distillation"]
        R4 --> R5["Recovered Solvent Storage"]
        R5 --> R6["Cooled Adsorbent Recycle to Bed"]
    end
    R6 -.-> B1
```

### 4.2 Breakthrough Curve Block Diagram (Fixed-Bed Adsorption)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BCURVE ["Breakthrough Curve Stages"]
        STAGE_A["Stage 1: Fresh Bed No Exit Contaminant C Out equals zero"]
        STAGE_B["Stage 2: Mass Transfer Zone Reaches Bed Exit Breakthrough Begins"]
        STAGE_C["Stage 3: Sharp Concentration Rise Outlet Approaches Inlet Value"]
        STAGE_D["Stage 4: Saturation Outlet equals Inlet Bed Exhausted"]
    end
    STAGE_A --> STAGE_B --> STAGE_C --> STAGE_D
    TIME["Time Axis t"] --> STAGE_A
    CONC["Outlet Concentration C out"] --> STAGE_A
```

### 4.3 Schematic of an Adsorbent Particle (Pore Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Adsorbent Particle External Surface"] --> P2["Macropore Entry"]
    P2 --> P3["Mesopore Network Diffusion Path"]
    P3 --> P4["Micropore Active Sites"]
    P4 --> P5["Adsorbate Molecule Anchored at Site"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The total **BET surface area** of activated carbon is dominated by **micropores** (pore diameter < **2 nm**). This is why a teaspoon of AC can have the internal surface area of a football field.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. State any **three** distinguishing features.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binding Force** | Weak Van der Waals | Strong covalent/ionic |
| **Enthalpy** | 20–40 kJ/mol | 80–400 kJ/mol |
| **Layers** | Multi-layer possible | Monolayer only |

Physical adsorption is reversible and favored at low temperature, whereas chemisorption is often irreversible, highly specific, and requires an activation energy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **adsorption isotherm**. Name the two most commonly used isotherm models.

**Model Answer:**
An **adsorption isotherm** is a graphical/mathematical representation of the equilibrium relationship between the amount of adsorbate retained per unit mass of adsorbent ($q$) and the equilibrium concentration ($C$) of the adsorbate in the bulk fluid, at a constant temperature.

The two most common models are:
1. **Langmuir Isotherm** — assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface.
2. **Freundlich Isotherm** — empirical model describing heterogeneous surface adsorption.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** with its underlying assumptions and show how it is linearized for experimental verification. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of the **breakthrough curve** in a fixed-bed adsorber. Define **mass transfer zone (MTZ)** and **breakthrough time**. **[(7 Marks)]**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Assumptions:**
   - Homogeneous, energetically equivalent surface sites.
   - Monolayer adsorption; each site holds one molecule.
   - No interaction between adsorbed molecules.
   - Dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption.

2. **Rate Equations:**
   $r_{\text{ads}} = k_a C (1 - \theta)$ and $r_{\text{des}} = k_d \theta$.

3. **Equating at equilibrium:**
   $k_a C (1-\theta) = k_d \theta$ → solving gives $\theta = \dfrac{K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$.

4. **Final form:** $q = \dfrac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$.

5. **Linearization:** $\dfrac{C}{q} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_m} + \dfrac{C}{q_m}$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sketch:**
```
C_out
  |          ___________
  |         /
  |        /
  |       /
  |______/____________________ t
         t_b        t_s
```

- **Breakthrough time ($t_b$):** time when outlet concentration reaches a pre-defined threshold (typically **5–10 %** of inlet concentration).
- **Saturation time ($t_s$):** time when outlet equals inlet ($C_{\text{out}} = C_0$).
- **MTZ:** The zone within the bed where the adsorbate concentration transitions from $C_0$ to near zero — represents the active adsorption region.
- As the MTZ moves down the bed, breakthrough eventually occurs; once $t = t_s$, the bed is fully spent and requires regeneration.

---

#### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Freundlich adsorption isotherm**. Discuss the significance of the constants $K_F$ and $1/n$, and the conditions under which Freundlich is preferred over Langmuir. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** A fixed-bed adsorber contains **200 kg** of activated carbon ($q_m = 250$ g/kg). The contaminated air contains **300 mg/m³** of benzene at a flow rate of **25 m³/min**. Estimate the **stoichiometric breakthrough time** $t_s$. **[(7 Marks)]**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Freundlich equation is **empirical** (no derivation) and is given by:

$$q = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$$

- $K_F$: capacity factor (higher $K_F$ = higher adsorption capacity).
- $1/n$: intensity factor. $n$ between **1 and 10** indicates favorable adsorption.
- Linear plot: $\log q$ vs $\log C$ gives slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$.
- **Preferred over Langmuir** when: (i) surface is heterogeneous, (ii) multilayer adsorption occurs, (iii) at moderate-to-high concentrations.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $W = 200$ kg, $q_m = 250$ g/kg = 250,000 mg/kg, $C_0 = 300$ mg/m³, $Q = 25$ m³/min.

**Total adsorption capacity of bed:**

$$M_{\text{cap}} = W \cdot q_m = 200 \cdot 250{,}000 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ mg}$$

**Mass flow rate of benzene:**

$$\dot{m} = Q \cdot C_0 = 25 \cdot 300 = 7{,}500 \text{ mg/min}$$

**Stoichiometric (saturation) breakthrough time:**

$$t_s = \frac{M_{\text{cap}}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{5 \times 10^7}{7{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{t_s \approx 6{,}667 \text{ min} \approx 111.1 \text{ hours} \approx 4.63 \text{ days}}$$

In practice, actual breakthrough occurs earlier due to MTZ effects; the Wheeler equation would give the corrected $t_b$.

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction mistakes observed by KTU examiners:**
> 1. **Confusing adsorption with absorption** — students lose **2 marks** instantly if terms are misused in the first line of an answer.
> 2. **Skipping assumptions** before writing the Langmuir derivation — the **assumptions themselves carry 2 marks** out of 7 in part-(a) questions.
> 3. **Forgetting to linearize** the Langmuir equation — KTU board specifically asks for the **linear form** to plot experimental data.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in breakthrough problems** — always convert $q_m$ from g/kg to **mg/kg** when $C_0$ is in **mg/m³** to ensure time is in consistent units.
> 5. **Omitting the sketch** in breakthrough curve questions — a **neatly labeled diagram** earns 1–2 grace marks even if numerical work is incomplete.
> 6. **Writing adsorption and chemisorption enthalpy ranges incorrectly** — physisorption is **20–40 kJ/mol**, never above **50 kJ/mol**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adsorption** = surface accumulation; **Absorption** = bulk penetration. Don't mix them.
- **Langmuir** = monolayer, homogeneous, ideal; **Freundlich** = multilayer, heterogeneous, empirical; **BET** = multilayer with saturation pressure.
- Critical Langmuir linear form: $\dfrac{C}{q} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_m} + \dfrac{C}{q_m}$.
- Critical Freundlich linear form: $\log q = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C$.
- **Activated carbon** surface area = **500–1500 m²/g**; dominated by **micropores** (<2 nm).
- Physisorption enthalpy: **20–40 kJ/mol**; Chemisorption: **80–400 kJ/mol**.
- **Breakthrough curve** stages: fresh bed → MTZ formation → sharp rise → saturation.
- **Wheeler–Jonas equation** is the practical tool for estimating breakthrough time.
- **Stoichiometric saturation time** $t_s = \dfrac{W q_m}{Q C_0}$ — the upper bound before complete exhaustion.
- Industrial applications: VOC abatement, solvent recovery, gas masks, automotive fuel-vapor canisters, odor control.
- **Regeneration** restores adsorbent using **steam, hot nitrogen, or vacuum swing**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Condensation** is the process by which a vapor (typically water vapor or a volatile organic compound) is converted into its liquid phase by the removal of its latent heat of vaporization. In the context of **Air Pollution Control Engineering**, condensation is classified as a **physical recovery / gas-phase control technique** used to separate condensable vapors and mists from non-condensable gas streams, thereby reducing volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions, recovering valuable solvents, and controlling odoriferous or hazardous gaseous pollutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Condensation is a phase-change mass transfer operation in which a vapor is converted into liquid by reducing its temperature below its dew point or increasing its pressure. It is widely employed in air pollution control for the recovery of condensable organic vapors and the removal of mists from industrial gas streams."*

The phenomenon is governed by the **psychrometric relationship** between partial pressure of the vapor and its saturation pressure at the gas temperature.

### Types of Condensation (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Mechanism | Surface Behavior | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Film Condensation** | Continuous liquid film forms on the cool surface | Complete wetting; film acts as thermal resistance | Most common; lower heat transfer |
| **Dropwise Condensation** | Discrete droplets nucleate, grow, coalesce and fall | Non-wetting; drops roll off rapidly | Theoretical ideal; very high heat transfer (5–10×) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bathroom Mirror:**
> When you take a hot shower, the bathroom mirror (a cool surface) becomes "foggy." Tiny water droplets (condensate) form on the glass because the water vapor in the warm air loses heat to the cold mirror and changes phase. The mirror cannot be "wiped clean" with hot air — the droplets keep coming because the temperature difference persists. This is **dropwise condensation on a non-wettable glass surface**. Now imagine a cold, oil-coated pipe carrying steam in a factory: a continuous liquid film forms and slowly drains — this is **film condensation**, which is what almost all engineering design equations describe.

### Key Physical Quantities

- **Saturation Pressure (P_sat)** — measured in **Pa** or **kPa**
- **Dew Point Temperature (T_dew)** — measured in **K** or **°C**
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization (λ or h_fg)** — measured in **J/kg** or **kJ/kg**
- **Partial Pressure of Vapor (P_v)** — measured in **Pa**
- **Degree of Saturation** = P_v / P_sat
- **Relative Humidity (φ)** = P_v / P_sat(T_gas) × 100%

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold:** Condensation initiates only when the **partial pressure of the vapor in the gas phase exceeds the saturation pressure at the surface temperature** (i.e., P_v > P_sat(T_surface)). This thermodynamic boundary defines the **Dew Point Curve** on the Psychrometric Chart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanism of Condensation (Step-by-Step)

1. **Vapor Diffusion:** Molecules of the condensable vapor diffuse from the bulk gas stream toward the cooler condensing surface (driven by a concentration gradient).
2. **Surface Contact:** Vapor molecules contact the cold surface and transfer their latent heat to it.
3. **Phase Change:** Molecules lose energy (latent heat) and transition to the liquid state.
4. **Condensate Drainage:** The liquid film (or droplets) flows downward under the influence of **gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²)** and is collected.
5. **Thermal Resistance Build-up:** In film condensation, the condensate layer progressively thickens as it flows down, increasing the **thermal resistance** and decreasing the local heat transfer coefficient.

### 2.2 Nusselt's Theory of Laminar Film Condensation (1916)

**Wilhelm Nusselt** derived the foundational analytical solution for laminar condensate film flow on a vertical plate. The key assumptions of the original theory are:

- Laminar, wavy-free film flow
- Constant fluid properties evaluated at the film temperature T_f = (T_sat + T_w)/2
- Vapor velocity approaching the surface is negligible (quiescent vapor)
- Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction
- Subcooling of the condensate is negligible (sensible heat effect ignored)
- Constant wall temperature (T_w) and saturation temperature (T_sat)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Notation Used (consistent with KTU 2024 scheme):**
> - h → Local heat transfer coefficient [W/m²·K]
> - k → Thermal conductivity of condensate [W/m·K]
> - μ → Dynamic viscosity of condensate [kg/m·s]
> - ρ → Density of condensate [kg/m³]
> - λ (h_fg) → Latent heat of vaporization [J/kg]
> - g → Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
> - L, x → Vertical plate length / local distance [m]
> - D → Tube diameter [m]
> - N → Number of horizontal tubes in a vertical tier
> - Re_f → Reynolds number of condensate film
> - Γ → Mass flow rate per unit width [kg/m·s]
> - M → Total mass flow rate of condensate [kg/s]
> - Q → Heat transfer rate [W]
> - T_sat → Saturation temperature of vapor [K]
> - T_w → Surface (wall) temperature [K]

#### Master Formula Table

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Local h on vertical plate (at distance x)** | $h_x = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, \lambda \, k^3}{\mu \, (T_{sat} - T_w) \, x} \right]^{1/4}$ | Laminar film, $Re_f \leq 30$ |
| 2 | **Average h on vertical plate of length L** | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{4}{3} \, h_{x=L} = 1.47 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, \lambda \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, (T_{sat} - T_w) \, L} \right]^{1/4}$ | Entire plate, laminar |
| 3 | **Condensate mass flow rate (vertical plate)** | $M = \dfrac{4}{3} \, \dfrac{\mu \, \bar{h} \, W \, L \, (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\lambda}$ | Derived from energy balance |
| 4 | **Local h on horizontal tube** | $h = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, \lambda \, k^3}{\mu \, (T_{sat} - T_w) \, D} \right]^{1/4}$ | Single horizontal tube |
| 5 | **Vertical tier of N horizontal tubes** | $h_N = h_1 \, (N)^{-1/4}$ | $\sum$ over N tubes; correction factor (1/N)^(1/4) |
| 6 | **Film Reynolds number (vertical plate)** | $Re_f = \dfrac{4 \, \Gamma}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \, M}{\mu \, W}$ | W = plate width; defines laminar–turbulent transition |
| 7 | **Heat transfer rate (general)** | $Q = \bar{h} \, A \, (T_{sat} - T_w)$ | Energy balance across surface |
| 8 | **Vapor–gas mixture mass balance** | $\dot{m}_{v,in} - \dot{m}_{v,out} = \dot{m}_{condensate}$ | Control volume on condenser |
| 9 | **Dew point relation (Clausius–Clapeyron approx.)** | $\ln \left( \dfrac{P_{sat,2}}{P_{sat,1}} \right) = \dfrac{\lambda}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$ | For pure vapor; R = universal gas constant |
| 10 | **Condensation efficiency (APC application)** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{v,in} - C_{v,out}}{C_{v,in} - C_{v,eq}}$ | Defined for VOC/mist removal equipment |

### 2.4 Laminar–Turbulent Transition Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The condensate film remains **laminar** for $Re_f \leq 30$, **wavy-laminar** for $30 < Re_f \leq 1800$, and **fully turbulent** for $Re_f > 1800$. For the turbulent regime on vertical surfaces, Kirkbride's correlation is preferred:
> $h = 0.0077 \, Re_f^{0.4} \, \left( \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu^2} \right)^{1/3} \, \left( \dfrac{k^3 \, \mu}{\rho^2 g} \right)^{1/3} \, \dfrac{1}{(T_{sat} - T_w)^{1/3}}$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Air Pollution Control, condensation equipment is critical for:

- **VOC Recovery** in pharmaceutical, petrochemical and printing industries (solvent recovery condensers)
- **Mist Elimination** in sulfuric acid plants, ammonia synthesis, and smelter off-gas treatment
- **Dehumidification of flue gas** before electrostatic precipitators or baghouse filters
- **Acid mist scrubbing** where H₂SO₄ or HCl vapors are condensed to recover acid
- **Particulate Removal (sub-micron range)** via vapor-phase growth followed by condensation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Nusselt's Local Heat Transfer Coefficient on a Vertical Plate

**Starting Postulates (Nusselt, 1916):**
- 2-D steady laminar flow of condensate film under gravity
- Inertia and convection terms neglected in the energy equation (lubrication approximation)
- Heat transfer across the film is by **pure conduction**

**Step 1 — Film Velocity Profile (Momentum Balance)**

For a thin film of thickness δ(x) at distance x from the top of the plate, with no pressure gradient (atmospheric boundary), the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to the gravity-driven shear balance:

$$\mu \dfrac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = -\rho g$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Twice with No-Slip and Zero-Shear Boundary Conditions**

$$u(y) = \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu} \, \delta^2 \, \left[ \dfrac{y}{\delta} - \dfrac{1}{2}\left( \dfrac{y}{\delta} \right)^2 \right]$$

This is a **parabolic (Poiseuille-like)** profile inside the film.

**Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate per Unit Width (Γ)**

The local mass flow rate per unit plate width is obtained by integrating the velocity profile across the film thickness:

$$\Gamma = \int_0^\delta \rho \, u(y) \, dy = \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance Across an Element dx**

Applying an energy balance to a control volume of width W, length dx on the plate:

$$\lambda \, d\dot{m} = h_x \, W \, (T_{sat} - T_w) \, dx$$

But from the mass flow relation, $d\Gamma = \dfrac{\rho^2 g \delta^2}{\mu} d\delta$, and combining:

$$h_x = \dfrac{\lambda \, d\Gamma / dx}{W \, (T_{sat} - T_w)} = \dfrac{\lambda \, \rho^2 g \delta^2}{\mu \, (T_{sat} - T_w)}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Conduction Through the Film (Fourier's Law)**

$$h_x = \dfrac{k}{\delta} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta = \dfrac{k}{h_x}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute δ into the Mass Flow Equation**

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g}{3\mu} \left( \dfrac{k}{h_x} \right)^3$$

**Step 7 — Differentiate w.r.t. x to obtain a Differential Equation**

Differentiating the mass flow rate: $\dfrac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \dfrac{h_x (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\lambda}$ (from energy balance)

Differentiating the cubic expression and equating gives:

$$\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu} \left( \dfrac{k^3}{h_x^3} \right) \dfrac{dh_x}{dx} = -\dfrac{h_x (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\lambda}$$

**Step 8 — Solve the ODE by Separation of Variables**

$$\dfrac{dh_x}{h_x^4} = -\dfrac{3\mu (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3} \, dx$$

Integrating from 0 to x (with $h_x \to \infty$ as $x \to 0$):

$$h_x = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{\mu (T_{sat} - T_w) x} \right]^{1/4}$$

This is the **Nusselt Local Heat Transfer Coefficient** on a vertical plate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key physical insight is the **h_x ∝ x^(-1/4) dependence**: the heat transfer coefficient is highest at the top of the plate (where the condensate film is thinnest) and decreases with distance downward.

### 3.2 Average Heat Transfer Coefficient on Vertical Plate

The average coefficient is obtained by integrating the local coefficient over the length L:

$$\bar{h} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L C \, x^{-1/4} \, dx = C \, \dfrac{L^{3/4}}{L} \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3} h_{x=L}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{h} = \dfrac{4}{3} \times 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{\mu (T_{sat} - T_w) L} \right]^{1/4} = 1.47 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{4 \mu (T_{sat} - T_w) L} \right]^{1/4}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
Saturated steam at **T_sat = 100 °C** condenses on a vertical plate of height **L = 0.6 m** maintained at **T_w = 80 °C**. The plate width is **W = 0.3 m**. For water at the film temperature (90 °C), the properties are:
- ρ = 965.3 kg/m³
- μ = 3.15 × 10⁻⁴ kg/m·s
- k = 0.675 W/m·K
- λ = 2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg

**Required:** (a) Average heat transfer coefficient, (b) Heat transfer rate, (c) Condensate mass flow rate, (d) Reynolds number and flow regime, (e) Film thickness at the bottom.

**Solution:**

**(a) Average h:**

$$\bar{h} = 1.47 \left[ \dfrac{(965.3)^2 (9.81)(2.26 \times 10^6)(0.675)^3}{4 (3.15 \times 10^{-4})(100 - 80)(0.6)} \right]^{1/4}$$

Compute numerator bracket:
- (965.3)² = 9.32 × 10⁵
- 9.32 × 10⁵ × 9.81 = 9.14 × 10⁶
- 9.14 × 10⁶ × 2.26 × 10⁶ = 2.066 × 10¹³
- (0.675)³ = 0.3075
- 2.066 × 10¹³ × 0.3075 = 6.35 × 10¹²

Denominator bracket:
- 4 × 3.15 × 10⁻⁴ × 20 × 0.6 = 1.512 × 10⁻²

Ratio = 6.35 × 10¹² / 1.512 × 10⁻² = 4.20 × 10¹⁴

Fourth root: $(4.20 \times 10^{14})^{1/4} = 4520$

$$\bar{h} = 1.47 \times 4520 = 6644 \approx 6644 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**(b) Heat transfer rate Q:**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot (T_{sat} - T_w) = 6644 \times (0.6 \times 0.3) \times 20$$

$$Q = 6644 \times 0.18 \times 20 = 23{,}918 \, W \approx 23.9 \, kW$$

**(c) Condensate mass flow rate M:**

$$M = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{23{,}918}{2.26 \times 10^6} = 1.058 \times 10^{-2} \, kg/s$$

**(d) Reynolds number:**

$$Re_f = \dfrac{4M}{\mu W} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.058 \times 10^{-2}}{3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3} = 447.9$$

Since **30 < 447.9 ≤ 1800**, the flow is in the **wavy-laminar regime** — Nusselt's pure laminar solution overpredicts h slightly.

**(e) Average film thickness:**

$$\delta_{avg} = \dfrac{k}{\bar{h}} = \dfrac{0.675}{6644} = 1.016 \times 10^{-4} \, m \approx 0.10 \, mm$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ultra-thin film (~100 µm) demonstrates why condensers can be remarkably compact heat exchange devices.

### 3.4 Derivation of Horizontal Tube Correlation (Summary)

For a horizontal tube of outer diameter D, Nusselt performed a similar balance, integrating the condensate flow over the upper half of the tube where it drains under gravity. The final result:

$$h = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{\mu (T_{sat} - T_w) D} \right]^{1/4}$$

For a vertical tier of N horizontal tubes (stacked vertically), the condensate from each tube above adds to the flow on the tube below, reducing the average h. The corrected correlation:

$$h_N = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{\mu (T_{sat} - T_w) N D} \right]^{1/4}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Condensation Classification Tree

### 4.4 Schematic — Condensate Film on Vertical Plate (Descriptive Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    node1[Top of Plate x equals 0]
```
