
> [!NOTE]
> The progressive thickening of the condensate film and the corresponding decrease in local heat transfer coefficient along the flow direction is the central physical insight of Nusselt's analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between film condensation and dropwise condensation. Which one offers higher heat transfer coefficient and why?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Film condensation:** A continuous liquid film forms on the condensing surface due to complete wetting. The film acts as a thermal resistance, and heat transfer occurs by conduction through the film.
- **Dropwise condensation:** Discrete droplets form on a non-wettable surface, grow by coalescence, and roll off rapidly, exposing fresh surface for further condensation.
- **Dropwise condensation offers 5–10 times higher heat transfer coefficient** because the thermal resistance of the bulk liquid film is eliminated; only thin droplets conduct heat. However, maintaining dropwise condensation in practice is difficult, so engineering design is universally based on **film condensation** (Nusselt's theory).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Nusselt's assumptions for the analysis of film condensation on a vertical plate.*

**Model Answer:**
1. Laminar, steady, smooth film flow
2. Constant fluid properties evaluated at the film mean temperature
3. Negligible velocity of vapor approaching the surface
4. Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction
5. Negligible subcooling of condensate
6. Constant wall temperature and constant saturation temperature
7. Body force of gravity is the sole driving force for film flow
8. No kinetic energy changes; momentum equation reduces to gravity–viscous balance

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive Nusselt's expression for the average heat transfer coefficient for film condensation of a saturated vapor on a **vertical plate**. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure condenses on a vertical surface **0.5 m high and 0.4 m wide**, maintained at **75 °C**. The steam saturation temperature is **100 °C**. Evaluate the **(i)** average heat transfer coefficient, **(ii)** total heat transfer rate, and **(iii)** condensate mass flow rate, using the following film properties at **T_f = 87.5 °C**:
- ρ = 968 kg/m³, μ = 3.13 × 10⁻⁴ kg/m·s, k = 0.677 W/m·K, λ = 2.27 × 10⁶ J/kg **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Full Nusselt derivation as presented in Section 3.1 and 3.2 above — momentum balance gives parabolic velocity profile, mass flow rate per unit width Γ = ρ²g δ³ / 3μ, energy balance gives h_x = k/δ, combining yields differential equation which upon integration gives:

$$h_x = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{\mu (T_{sat} - T_w) x} \right]^{1/4}$$

Average over length L: $\bar{h} = \dfrac{4}{3} h_{x=L} = 1.47 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 g \lambda k^3}{4 \mu (T_{sat} - T_w) L} \right]^{1/4}$

All 8 assumptions to be listed (full marks require explicit statement).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Average h:**

$$\bar{h} = 1.47 \left[ \dfrac{(968)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.27 \times 10^6 \times (0.677)^3}{4 \times 3.13 \times 10^{-4} \times (100 - 75) \times 0.5} \right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator: (9.37 × 10⁵)(9.81)(2.27 × 10⁶)(0.310) = 6.35 × 10¹²
Denominator: 4 × 3.13 × 10⁻⁴ × 25 × 0.5 = 1.565 × 10⁻²
Ratio = 4.06 × 10¹⁴ → fourth root = 4485

$$\bar{h} = 1.47 \times 4485 \approx 6593 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**(ii) Total heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 6593 \times (0.5 \times 0.4) \times 25 = 6593 \times 0.2 \times 25 = 32{,}965 \, W \approx 33.0 \, kW$$

**(iii) Condensate mass flow rate:**

$$M = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{32{,}965}{2.27 \times 10^6} = 1.452 \times 10^{-2} \, kg/s \approx 52.3 \, kg/hr$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of film condensation on a vertical surface** and explain why the local heat transfer coefficient varies along the plate. Derive the Nusselt correlation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal tube of **25 mm outer diameter** and length **2 m** is used to condense saturated steam at **120 °C**. The tube surface is maintained at **T_w = 100 °C**. Compute the **(i)** average heat transfer coefficient, **(ii)** heat transfer rate, and **(iii)** average condensate film thickness. Use water film properties at T_f = 110 °C:
- ρ = 951.7 kg/m³, μ = 2.59 × 10⁻⁴ kg/m·s, k = 0.686 W/m·K, λ = 2.20 × 10⁶ J/kg **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism & Derivation (7 marks):**
Sketch showing wall temperature T_w, saturation temperature T_sat, condensate film of thickness δ(x), parabolic velocity profile, gravity-driven downward flow, mass flow per unit width Γ increasing with x. Explain that local h decreases as x^(-1/4) because film thickens, increasing conduction path. Then derive Nusselt's correlation step-by-step as in Section 3.1.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Average h on horizontal tube:**

$$h = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{(951.7)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.20 \times 10^6 \times (0.686)^3}{2.59 \times 10^{-4} \times (120 - 100) \times 0.025} \right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator: (9.058 × 10⁵)(9.81)(2.20 × 10⁶)(0.323) = 6.32 × 10¹²
Denominator: 2.59 × 10⁻⁴ × 20 × 0.025 = 1.295 × 10⁻⁴
Ratio = 4.88 × 10¹⁶ → fourth root = 14{,}900

$$h = 0.725 \times 14{,}900 \approx 10{,}803 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**(ii) Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = h \cdot (\pi D L) \cdot \Delta T = 10{,}803 \times (\pi \times 0.025 \times 2) \times 20$$

$$Q = 10{,}803 \times 0.1571 \times 20 \approx 33{,}942 \, W \approx 33.94 \, kW$$

**(iii) Average film thickness:**

$$\delta_{avg} = \dfrac{k}{h} = \dfrac{0.686}{10{,}803} = 6.35 \times 10^{-5} \, m \approx 63.5 \, \mu m$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Condensation Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong property evaluation temperature:** All fluid properties (ρ, μ, k) must be evaluated at the **film temperature T_f = (T_sat + T_w)/2**, not at T_sat alone. Examiners deduct up to **1.5 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4/3 factor:** Average h on a vertical plate = (4/3) × h_{x=L}, not h_{x=L} directly.
> 3. **Tube bundle correction:** For N horizontal tubes stacked vertically, apply the **(1/N)^(1/4)** correction. Students often use single-tube equation — penalty **1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing Nusselt assumptions:** Derivative carry **7 marks**; listing only 3–4 assumptions causes significant loss.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Heat transfer rate must be in **W** (not kW) when computing condensate mass rate. Convert λ to J/kg, not kJ/kg, in the M = Q/λ expression.
> 6. **No flow regime check:** Always compute Re_f and identify laminar / wavy / turbulent. Examiners expect this verification step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Condensation:**
>
> - Condensation is a **phase-change heat transfer** process fundamental to VOC recovery, mist elimination, and gas cleaning in APC.
> - Two regimes: **Film** (continuous liquid layer, Nusselt theory) and **Dropwise** (discrete droplets, 5–10× higher h, hard to maintain).
> - **Nusselt's assumptions** (8) must be stated in full for any derivation question.
> - Local h on vertical plate: $h_x \propto x^{-1/4}$ — **decreases downstream** as film thickens.
> - Average h on vertical plate = **(4/3) h_{x=L}** — always remember the 4/3 factor.
> - Horizontal tube: $h = 0.725 \left[ \rho^2 g \lambda k^3 / (\mu \Delta T D) \right]^{1/4}$.
> - N-tube vertical stack: multiply by **N^(-1/4)** for the average.
> - **Film Reynolds number:** $Re_f = 4\Gamma/\mu = 4M/(\mu W)$ defines flow regime.
> - Regimes: **Laminar** ($Re_f \leq 30$), **Wavy-Laminar** ($30 < Re_f \leq 1800$), **Turbulent** ($Re_f > 1800$).
> - Turbulent regime correlation: **Kirkbride equation** with $Re_f^{0.4}$ dependence.
> - All fluid properties evaluated at **T_f = (T_sat + T_w)/2** — this is a non-negotiable convention.
> - Heat transfer rate: **Q = h A ΔT**; condensate rate: **M = Q/λ**.
> - APC applications: solvent recovery condensers, mist eliminators, dehumidification, acid mist recovery.
> - Dew point: condensation begins when **T_surface < T_dew** of the gas-vapor mixture.
> - Condensation efficiency: $\eta = (C_{v,in} - C_{v,out}) / (C_{v,in} - C_{v,eq})$ — important for APC design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incineration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incineration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Incineration** is a high-temperature thermal oxidation process (typically operating between **800 °C and 1200 °C**) in which combustible waste materials (solid, liquid, or gaseous) are converted into ash, flue gas, and heat through controlled chemical reaction with oxygen. The governing reaction is the complete combustion of organic carbonaceous matter to carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water vapor (H₂O), while inorganic constituents are reduced to inert ash.

$$C_xH_yO_z + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + Heat$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Incineration is the *engineered thermal destruction of waste* in which the principal objectives are **(i) volume reduction (typically 90–95%)**, **(ii) mass reduction (typically 65–80%)**, and **(iii) detoxification/destruction of organic hazardous constituents** at residence times sufficient to ensure complete combustion, as defined by the **"Three T's" rule** — **Temperature, Time, Turbulence**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **traditional brick kiln in a Kerala pottery workshop**. The potter doesn't just throw wood and clay into a pile and light it — he carefully controls *how hot* the fire gets (Temperature), *how long* the firing lasts (Time), and *how the air swirls around* the pots inside the kiln (Turbulence). An incinerator is essentially a **highly engineered, scientific version of that same kiln**, but designed to burn *trash* (municipal solid waste, medical waste, hazardous chemicals) instead of clay.

Just as the potter's kiln transforms brittle clay into hard ceramic by rearranging molecules with heat, an incinerator breaks down complex waste molecules (plastics, pathogens, organic chemicals) into simple, safe end-products (CO₂, H₂O, ash). Without enough heat, time, or mixing — the result is the same as a smoldering pile of garbage: **incomplete combustion, toxic smoke, and leftover pollutants**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - **DRE (Destruction and Removal Efficiency):** ≥ 99.99% for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs) — this is the U.S. EPA Big Four Rule benchmark.
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio:** The chemically exact air required for *complete* combustion of a fuel/waste without excess.
> - **Excess Air (EA):** Typically **20% to 100%** above stoichiometric to ensure complete oxidation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature (natural gas/air) | ~2200 | K |
| Auto-ignition temperature of common organics | 300 – 600 | °C |
| Minimum incinerator temperature (municipal waste) | 850 | °C |
| Minimum temperature for POHC destruction | 1000 | °C |
| Recommended residence time at ≥ 1000 °C | ≥ 2 | seconds |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) of typical MSW | 8 – 12 | MJ/kg |
| Excess air for complete combustion | 50 – 100 | % above stoichiometric |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Three T's" — The Governing Pillars of Incineration

The entire design of any incinerator revolves around three interconnected parameters. If any one of them is inadequate, the combustion process becomes incomplete, producing CO, soot, dioxins, furans, and unburnt hydrocarbons.

**(a) Temperature (T)**
- Must be high enough to ignite all combustible gases and to destroy toxic organics.
- For MSW: **≥ 850 °C** (to prevent dioxin formation in cooler zones, a "2-second, 850 °C" rule is mandated in EU Directive 2000/76/EC).
- For hazardous waste (POHCs): **≥ 1000 °C** with **≥ 2 seconds** residence time.

**(b) Time (t) / Residence Time**
- The duration the gases and particles spend in the high-temperature zone.
- Long enough for complete oxidation of volatiles and fine particulates.

**(c) Turbulence (Mixing)**
- The degree to which oxygen, fuel, and combustion products are mixed.
- Achieved by burner geometry, refractory design, overfire air injection ports, and swirling flows.

### 2.2 Classification of Incinerators (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Best Suited For | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple-Chamber (Controlled-Air) Incinerator** | MSW, hospital waste, industrial solids | Two chambers: primary (starved-air, pyrolysis) + secondary (excess-air, afterburner). |
| **Fluidized-Bed Incinerator (FBI)** | Sewage sludge, hazardous waste | Sand bed fluidized by air; excellent heat transfer, narrow temperature range (700–900 °C). |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Hazardous, mixed, medical waste | Inclined rotating cylinder; long residence time, high destruction efficiency. |
| **Modular / Starved-Air Incinerator** | Institutional waste, small hospitals | Small, prefabricated, batch-fed; negative pressure in primary chamber. |
| **Liquid Injection Incincinerator** | Industrial liquid hazardous waste | Atomized liquid through nozzles into refractory chamber. |
| **Pathological / Crematory** | Biological, anatomical waste | One-chamber, ~760–870 °C, afterburner section. |

### 2.3 The Chemistry of Combustion

Complete combustion of a generalized organic compound CₐHᵦNᵧS₨OₒClᵪ follows:

$$C_aH_bN_cS_dO_eCl_f + O_2 \rightarrow aCO_2 + \frac{b}{2}H_2O + \frac{c}{2}N_2 + dSO_2 + fHCl$$

**The Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement** is calculated by atom balance on the carbon, hydrogen, sulfur, and chlorine atoms. For a fuel of known ultimate analysis, the mass of stoichiometric oxygen (kg O₂ per kg of waste) is:

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.667\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O \quad \text{(kg O}_2 \text{/kg fuel)}$$

where $w_C, w_H, w_S, w_O$ are the mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, sulfur, and oxygen in the waste.

Converting to stoichiometric **air** (by mass, air contains 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂ by mass):

$$m_{air,stoich} = \frac{m_{O_2,stoich}}{0.232} = 11.5\,w_C + 34.4\,w_H + 4.3\,w_S - 4.31\,w_O \quad \text{(kg air/kg fuel)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A/S = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{stoich}} = 1 + \frac{EA}{100}$ | Actual air-to-stoichiometric ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 2 | $m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.667\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O$ | Stoichiometric O₂ requirement | kg O₂/kg fuel |
| 3 | $m_{air,stoich} = \frac{m_{O_2,stoich}}{0.232}$ | Stoichiometric air requirement (mass basis) | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | $DRE = \frac{m_{in} - m_{out}}{m_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Destruction and Removal Efficiency | % |
| 5 | $HRT = \frac{V_{chamber}}{\dot{Q}_{gas}}$ | Hydraulic Residence Time = chamber volume / gas volumetric flow rate | s |
| 6 | $T_{adiabatic} = T_{in} + \frac{HHV \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas}}$ | Theoretical adiabatic flame temperature | K |
| 7 | $\eta_{thermal} = \frac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{released}}$ | Thermal efficiency of waste-to-energy system | % |
| 8 | $P = \frac{HHV}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} \times \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Power output of incineration WTE plant | kW or MW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always carry the **% Excess Air (EA)** in your final answer. A common trap: students write "air required = 5 kg/kg fuel" but forget whether that is *stoichiometric* or *actual*. Always specify.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incineration is the cornerstone technology for:
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) treatment** in urban Kerala cities where landfills are saturated.
- **Hospital and biomedical waste disposal** (driven by BMWM Rules, 2016 under EPA 1986 of India).
- **Hazardous waste management** for industrial chemicals, PCBs, and pesticides (regulated by Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2008).
- **Crematoria and pathological waste** disposal.
- **Waste-to-Energy (WtE) plants** where heat recovery via boilers, steam turbines, and generators converts waste into electricity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Stoichiometric Air and Excess Air Calculation

**Problem:** A hazardous waste has the ultimate analysis (by mass): C = 50%, H = 6%, O = 25%, S = 3%, N = 4%, Ash = 12%. Determine (a) stoichiometric O₂ required, (b) stoichiometric air required, and (c) actual air required at 60% excess air.

**Given:**
- $w_C = 0.50$, $w_H = 0.06$, $w_O = 0.25$, $w_S = 0.03$

#### Part (a): Stoichiometric Oxygen

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.667\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O$$

Substituting the values (text row 1: carbon contribution is 2.667 times the mass fraction of carbon):

$$\text{Term from C} = 2.667 \times 0.50 = 1.3335 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$\text{Term from H} = 8 \times 0.06 = 0.48 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$\text{Term from S} = 1 \times 0.03 = 0.03 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$\text{Term from O (subtracted, oxygen already in fuel)} = 0.25 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

Summing all contributions:

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 1.3335 + 0.48 + 0.03 - 0.25 = 1.5935 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**∴ Stoichiometric oxygen ≈ 1.594 kg O₂ / kg of waste.**

#### Part (b): Stoichiometric Air

$$m_{air,stoich} = \frac{m_{O_2,stoich}}{0.232} = \frac{1.5935}{0.232} = 6.868 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**∴ Stoichiometric air ≈ 6.87 kg air / kg of waste.**

#### Part (c): Actual Air at 60% Excess

The A/S ratio when excess air is 60%:

$$A/S = 1 + \frac{EA}{100} = 1 + 0.60 = 1.60$$

Therefore the actual air supplied is:

$$m_{air,actual} = 1.60 \times 6.868 = 10.989 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**∴ Actual air supplied ≈ 10.99 kg air / kg of waste.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why subtract $w_O$?** Some waste already contains chemically bound oxygen (e.g., plastics, cellulose, sugars). That oxygen is *available* for combustion inside the fuel itself, so the burner does not have to supply it. Ignoring this leads to over-aeration and energy wasted as hot nitrogen exhaust.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE)

**Problem:** A rotary kiln processes 200 kg/hr of a hazardous pesticide (POHC mass fraction = 0.04 in the feed). The flue gas after APCD contains 8 g/hr of the same POHC. Calculate DRE.

**Given:**
- Total feed = $\dot{m}_{feed} = 200$ kg/hr
- POHC mass fraction in feed = $w_{in} = 0.04$
- POHC in outlet flue gas = $\dot{m}_{out} = 8$ g/hr = 0.008 kg/hr

**Step 1:** Calculate the mass of POHC entering the system.

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{feed} \times w_{in} = 200 \times 0.04 = 8 \quad \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DRE definition.

$$DRE = \frac{\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}}{\dot{m}_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values (text row 2: convert to consistent units — both in kg/hr).

$$DRE = \frac{8 - 0.008}{8} \times 100\% = \frac{7.992}{8} \times 100\% = 99.90\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

**∴ DRE = 99.90%.** This is **just below** the U.S. EPA's mandated 99.99% (the "four-nines" rule) for principal organic hazardous constituents, meaning the incinerator is *non-compliant* and needs an improved APCD system.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Hydraulic Residence Time (HRT)

**Problem:** A secondary combustion chamber has a volume of 50 m³. The flue gas flow rate is 5,500 Nm³/hr at NTP. Estimate the residence time in seconds.

**Given:**
- $V_{chamber} = 50$ m³
- $\dot{Q}_{gas} = 5500$ Nm³/hr

**Step 1:** Convert gas flow rate to m³/s.

$$\dot{Q}_{gas} = \frac{5500}{3600} = 1.528 \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the HRT formula.

$$HRT = \frac{V_{chamber}}{\dot{Q}_{gas}} = \frac{50}{1.528} = 32.72 \quad \text{seconds}$$

**∴ HRT ≈ 32.7 s**, which is well above the 2 s minimum required for POHC destruction at ≥ 1000 °C. The chamber is over-designed for residence, allowing some margin for process upsets.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Engineering Question | Governing Equation | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| Will there be smoke (CO)? | Compare A/S ratio to theoretical (A/S ≈ 1.6–2.0) | CO < 50 ppm in stack |
| Will dioxins form? | Compare flue gas cooling rate between 850→300 °C | < 1 s quenching (post-combustion) |
| Is energy recovery feasible? | Compare HHV of waste to ~7 MJ/kg threshold | HHV > 7 MJ/kg = WtE viable |
| Will waste ignite in primary chamber? | Compare HHV to drying/de-volatilization energy | HHV > 6 MJ/kg self-sustaining |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Incineration Plant with APCD Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Waste Receipt and Segregation] --> B[Weighbridge and Storage Pit]
    B --> C[Feed Chute / Conveyor]
    C --> D[Primary Combustion Chamber]
    D --> E[Secondary Combustion Chamber Afterburner]
    E --> F[Waste Heat Boiler Heat Recovery]
    F --> G[Steam Turbine and Generator WtE]
    F --> H[Quench Spray 850C to 200C fast]
    H --> I[Baghouse Particulate APCD]
    I --> J[Scrubber SO2 HCl Acid Gas]
    J --> K[Selective Catalytic Reduction NOx]
    K --> L[Activated Carbon Dioxin Mercury Polishing]
    L --> M[Stack and Continuous Emissions Monitoring CEMS]
    F -. Heat Recycle .-> D
    G -. Power Export .-> Grid[State Electricity Grid]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Three T's Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Temperature 850 to 1200 C] --> X{Three Ts Equilibrium}
    T2[Time 2 s minimum at 1000 C] --> X
    T3[Turbulence Air Swirl Burner Mix] --> X
    X --> Outcome1[Complete Combustion]
    X --> Outcome2[DRE greater than 99.99 percent]
    X --> Outcome3[Low CO NOx Dioxin Emissions]
    A[Inadequate Ts] --> Y[Incomplete Combustion]
    Y --> Z1[CO and Soot]
    Y --> Z2[Dioxins and Furans]
    Y --> Z3[Unburnt Hydrocarbons]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Multiple Chamber Controlled Air Incinerator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Feed[Waste Feed] --> Pri[Primary Chamber Starved Air Pyrolysis 600 to 800 C]
    Pri --> Sec[Secondary Chamber Excess Air Afterburner 1000 plus C]
    Air1[Limited Combustion Air] --> Pri
    Air2[Secondary Air Injection Overfire Ports] --> Sec
    Sec --> Ash[Bottom Ash Discharge]
    Sec --> Boiler[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    Boiler --> Flue[Flue Gas Treatment Train]
    Flue --> Stack[Stack]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Decision Logic for Excess Air Setting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Determine Waste Ultimate Analysis]) --> Stoich[Calculate Stoichiometric Air via Mass Balance]
    Stoich --> Q{Waste Type?}
    Q -->|MSW with Inerts| EA1[Apply 80 to 100 percent Excess Air]
    Q -->|Hazardous Liquid| EA2[Apply 10 to 30 percent Excess Air]
    Q -->|Gaseous VOC| EA3[Apply 5 to 15 percent Excess Air]
    EA1 --> Burner1[Set Burner Air Register]
    EA2 --> Burner2[Set Burner Air Register]
    EA3 --> Burner3[Set Burner Air Register]
    Burner1 --> Monitor[Stack O2 and CO CEMS Feedback]
    Burner2 --> Monitor
    Burner3 --> Monitor
    Monitor --> Adjust[Trim Air for CO less than 50 ppm and O2 6 to 8 percent]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

State and explain the **"Three T's"** that govern the design and operation of an incinerator. Why is each one indispensable for the destruction of hazardous organic constituents?

**Model Answer:**

The Three T's are:

1. **Temperature:** The combustion chamber must reach and maintain a sufficiently high temperature to thermally break molecular bonds of organic compounds. For hazardous waste, the EPA mandates **≥ 1000 °C** in the secondary chamber.

2. **Time (Residence Time):** The flue gas and particulates must remain in the high-temperature zone for at least **2 seconds** at ≥ 1000 °C. This is the minimum duration for the molecular decomposition and oxidation of the POHCs to approach completion.

3. **Turbulence:** Air, fuel, and combustion gases must be intimately mixed to bring oxygen into contact with every combustible molecule. Without turbulence, pockets of unburnt gas escape intact, generating CO, smoke, and unburnt hydrocarbons.

If any one of the three is deficient, combustion becomes incomplete, resulting in the emission of CO, soot, dioxins, furans, and partially destroyed POHCs. The Three T's are interdependent — increasing temperature, for instance, can compensate for shorter time, but turbulence cannot be replaced by either.

*(3 marks: 1 for each T + 0.5 for interdependence note + 0.5 for example pollutant = 3)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **stoichiometric air** and **excess air** in an incinerator. Why is excess air always supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stoichiometric Air | Excess Air |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The exact mass of air that supplies just enough oxygen to completely combust the fuel. | The additional air supplied *beyond* stoichiometric to ensure complete combustion in practice. |
| **Formula** | $m_{air,stoich}$ from ultimate analysis. | $EA(\%) = \frac{m_{air,actual} - m_{air,stoich}}{m_{air,stoich}} \times 100$ |
| **Typical value** | Depends on C/H/S content. | 20 – 100 % for solid waste, 5 – 15 % for clean gaseous fuels. |
| **Combustion outcome** | Theoretical — would yield zero O₂ in flue gas. | Practical — residual O₂ of 2 – 8 % to guarantee complete oxidation. |

Excess air is supplied because, in real furnaces, mixing is never perfect, and the time–temperature history varies across the chamber. Sub-stoichiometric operation leads to CO, smoke, and unburnt hydrocarbons. However, too much excess air cools the flame and reduces thermal efficiency, so it must be optimized.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definitions, 0.5 for typical values, 1.0 for the "why" with consequences of over/under air)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with KTU ESE Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** The question paper offers the student a choice between **Question A** and **Question B** on the same module. Both questions are independently self-contained and carry **14 marks** split into two parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): Rotary Kiln Incinerator Design Calculations**

**Part (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A rotary kiln incinerator is fed with 800 kg/hr of a hazardous sludge having the following ultimate analysis: C = 38%, H = 5%, N = 3%, S = 2%, O = 22%, Ash = 30% (by mass). Determine:
- (i) the stoichiometric O₂ and air required per kg of waste,
- (ii) the actual air required at 80% excess air, and
- (iii) the volumetric flow rate of flue gas (Nm³/hr) at the stack assuming complete combustion.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Given:** $w_C = 0.38$, $w_H = 0.05$, $w_S = 0.02$, $w_O = 0.22$, $w_N = 0.03$, $w_{ash} = 0.30$

**(i) Stoichiometric O₂:**

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.667(0.38) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.02) - 0.22$$

$$= 1.0135 + 0.40 + 0.02 - 0.22 = 1.2135 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$

**Stoichiometric air:**

$$m_{air,stoich} = \frac{1.2135}{0.232} = 5.230 \quad \text{kg air/kg waste}$$

**(ii) Actual air at 80% excess:**

$$A/S = 1.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{air,actual} = 1.80 \times 5.230 = 9.414 \quad \text{kg air/kg waste}$$

For 800 kg/hr:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 9.414 \times 800 = 7531 \quad \text{kg air/hr}$$

**(iii) Flue gas volumetric flow rate:**

Mass of flue gas per kg of waste (using N₂ from air, CO₂ from C, SO₂ from S, H₂O from H):

$$\dot{m}_{flue} = \dot{m}_{air,actual} + \dot{m}_{waste} - \dot{m}_{ash}$$

$$= 7531 + 800 - (800 \times 0.30) = 7531 + 800 - 240 = 8091 \quad \text{kg/hr}$$

Converting to Nm³/hr using the average molecular weight of flue gas ≈ 29 kg/kmol and molar volume 22.4 Nm³/kmol:

$$\dot{V}_{flue} = \frac{8091}{29} \times 22.4 = 6251 \quad \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**∴ Stack flue gas flow ≈ 6,250 Nm³/hr.** (7 marks: 2 for stoich O₂, 2 for actual air, 3 for flue gas volumetric flow)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**

Discuss the **advantages and limitations of rotary kiln incinerators** for hazardous waste, with a labelled sketch of the cross-section. List the typical **air pollution control devices (APCDs)** used in the flue gas train after the kiln.

**Model Answer:**

*Advantages:*
1. Extremely versatile — handles solid, liquid, sludges, and tars in the same unit.
2. Long residence time (hours for solids, ≥ 2 s for gases) → high DRE.
3. Robust refractory lining tolerates high temperatures and corrosive feeds.
4. Can accept bulky, irregular, or containerized waste (e.g., drums of chemicals).
5. Good mixing via kiln rotation → high turbulence (the third "T").

*Limitations:*
1. High capital cost and refractory maintenance.
2. High excess air requirement (50 – 100%) → low thermal efficiency unless WtE added.
3. Particulate loading in flue gas is high → expensive APCD train required.
4. Sensitive to feed moisture — wet sludge reduces flame temperature.

*APCD Train (in order):*
- **Quench** (rapid cooling to 200 °C to prevent dioxin re-formation).
- **Baghouse / Electrostatic Precipitator** (particulate control, > 99.9% efficiency).
- **Wet scrubber** (acid gas removal — SO₂, HCl via lime/NaOH slurry).
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for NOₓ.
- **Activated Carbon injection** for mercury and dioxin polishing.
- **Stack with CEMS** (continuous emission monitoring system).

(7 marks: 2 for advantages, 2 for limitations, 3 for APCD train in correct order)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): Air-Fuel Balance & Combustion Stoichiometry**

**Part (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Municipal solid waste (MSW) arriving at a Kerala WtE plant has the ultimate analysis: C = 25%, H = 3.5%, S = 0.3%, N = 0.5%, O = 20%, Moisture = 35%, Ash = 15.7% (by mass). The plant operates with 90% excess air. Calculate:
- (i) the stoichiometric air per kg of dry waste,
- (ii) the actual air supplied per kg of *as-received* (wet) waste, and
- (iii) the HHV using Dulong's formula.

**Model Solution:**

**Given (on as-received basis):** $w_C = 0.25$, $w_H = 0.035$, $w_S = 0.003$, $w_N = 0.005$, $w_O = 0.20$, $w_{moisture} = 0.35$, $w_{ash} = 0.157$

**(i) Stoichiometric air (per kg of as-received waste):**

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.667(0.25) + 8(0.035) + 1(0.003) - 0.20$$

$$= 0.6668 + 0.280 + 0.003 - 0.20 = 0.7498 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg AR waste}$$

$$m_{air,stoich} = \frac{0.7498}{0.232} = 3.232 \quad \text{kg air/kg AR waste}$$

**(ii) Actual air at 90% excess:**

$$A/S = 1.90 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{air,actual} = 1.90 \times 3.232 = 6.141 \quad \text{kg air/kg AR waste}$$

**(iii) HHV by Dulong's formula:**

$$HHV = 33.9\,w_C + 144.2\,(w_H - \tfrac{w_O}{8}) + 9.3\,w_S \quad \text{(MJ/kg)}$$

HHV per kg of *as-received* (using ultimate analysis on AR basis):

$$HHV_{AR} = 33.9(0.25) + 144.2\left(0.035 - \frac{0.20}{8}\right) + 9.3(0.003)$$

$$= 8.475 + 144.2(0.035 - 0.025) + 0.0279$$

$$= 8.475 + 144.2(0.010) + 0.0279 = 8.475 + 1.442 + 0.0279 = 9.945 \quad \text{MJ/kg AR}$$

Per kg of *dry* waste (dividing by $1 - 0.35 = 0.65$):

$$HHV_{dry} = \frac{9.945}{0.65} = 15.30 \quad \text{MJ/kg dry}$$

**∴ HHV ≈ 9.94 MJ/kg (AR) and 15.3 MJ/kg (dry).** A value well above 7 MJ/kg confirms the waste is **self-sustaining for combustion** and a viable feedstock for Waste-to-Energy recovery.

(7 marks: 2 for stoich, 2 for actual air, 3 for HHV with proper application of Dulong's formula)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**

A continuous emissions monitoring system (CEMS) at the stack of a hazardous waste incinerator reports: NOₓ = 180 mg/Nm³, SO₂ = 35 mg/Nm³, CO = 65 mg/Nm³, HCl = 12 mg/Nm³, Dioxin TEQ = 0.08 ng/Nm³. Compare each value to the **Indian CPCB/EPACEQ standards for hazardous waste incinerators** and identify which parameters are non-compliant. Suggest one control technology for each non-compliant pollutant.

**Model Answer:**

| Pollutant | Measured | CPCB Limit (Hazardous Waste Incinerator) | Compliant? | Suggested Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NOₓ | 180 mg/Nm³ | 400 mg/Nm³ (1-hr average, ref. 6% O₂) | ✅ Yes | — |
| SO₂ | 35 mg/Nm³ | 50 mg/Nm³ | ✅ Yes | — |
| CO | 65 mg/Nm³ | 100 mg/Nm³ (1-hr) | ✅ Yes (marginal) | Improve combustion efficiency / O₂ trim |
| HCl | 12 mg/Nm³ | 50 mg/Nm³ | ✅ Yes | — |
| Dioxin TEQ | 0.08 ng/Nm³ | 0.1 ng/Nm³ | ✅ Yes (close to limit) | Strengthen activated carbon injection; ensure rapid quench < 200 °C |

All measured values are within CPCB/Indian EPACEQ limits, but **CO at 65 mg/Nm³ and Dioxin at 0.08 ng/Nm³ are close to the regulatory limits (within 30% and 20% margin respectively)**. Continuous operational tightening is needed to prevent excursions:

- **For CO:** install an **oxygen trim controller** on the secondary air register, target 6% O₂ dry. Tune burner for better fuel-air mixing.
- **For Dioxins:** improve the **quench rate** to < 1 second between 850 °C and 200 °C to prevent the *de novo* synthesis window (200 – 400 °C), and add/upgrade **activated carbon injection** at 50–200 mg/Nm³ dosage.

(7 marks: 4 for tabulated compliance check, 3 for appropriate control suggestions tied to specific pollutants)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE on Incineration:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the oxygen subtraction in the stoichiometric formula.** The term $-w_O$ is *not* optional. It accounts for the oxygen already bound in the fuel. Omitting this yields a 10–25% over-estimate of air required and a loss of ~1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up "stoichiometric air" and "actual air" in the final answer.** Always clearly label which is which. Examiners dock 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 3. **Using Dulong's formula on a wet (as-received) basis without converting to dry basis** when the question explicitly asks for HHV per kg of dry waste. Watch the wording: "per kg of waste" can mean either, depending on context.
> 4. **Writing 1000 °C and 850 °C interchangeably for the secondary chamber.** The 850 °C is the EU MSW threshold with 2 s residence; 1000 °C is the EPA hazardous-waste threshold. Both are correct, but tag the right waste type.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract ash mass** when computing flue gas mass balance. The ash leaves the *bottom* of the furnace, not via the stack. This is a frequent 0.5-mark deduction.
> 6. **In DRE questions, not converting g/hr to kg/hr.** A unit mismatch silently produces an answer off by 1000×. The KTU examiner is strict on units.
> 7. **APC train drawn in the wrong sequence.** A common error: placing the SCR *before* the scrubber. SCR must follow the wet scrubber to prevent catalyst poisoning by SO₂/slurry carryover.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Incineration = controlled, high-temperature thermal oxidation of waste** to ash, flue gas, and heat, governed by the **Three T's (Temperature, Time, Turbulence)**.
- **Key operating thresholds:** ≥ 850 °C for MSW (EU Directive), ≥ 1000 °C for hazardous waste (EPA Big Four Rule), with ≥ 2 s residence time in the secondary chamber.
- **DRE ≥ 99.99%** is the regulatory benchmark for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs).
- **Stoichiometric oxygen (per kg fuel):** $2.667\,w_C + 8\,w_H + w_S - w_O$. Stoichiometric air = O₂ requirement ÷ 0.232.
- **Excess air (EA):** typically 50–100% for solids, 5–15% for clean gases. More EA → more complete combustion but lower adiabatic flame temperature.
- **Major incinerator types:** Multiple-chamber (controlled-air), Fluidized-Bed, Rotary Kiln, Modular, Liquid Injection, Pathological. Rotary kilns dominate hazardous waste; multiple-chamber dominate MSW/hospital waste.
- **APC train (typical sequence):** Quench → Particulate control (ESP/Baghouse) → Acid gas scrubber → SCR for NOₓ → Activated carbon → Stack + CEMS.
- **Dulong's formula** for HHV: $HHV = 33.9\,w_C + 144.2\,(w_H - w_O/8) + 9.3\,w_S$ in MJ/kg.
- **HHV threshold for self-sustaining combustion:** roughly **6 – 7 MJ/kg**; below this, auxiliary fuel is needed.
- **Mass balance shortcut:** Flue gas mass = Air in + Fuel in − Ash out (excluding water condensation considerations).
- **Indian regulatory context:** Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2008, BMWM Rules 2016, EPA 1986 — all mandate incineration standards aligned with EU Directive 2000/76/EC.
- **Dioxin prevention rule:** quench flue gas from 850 °C to 200 °C in **less than 1 second** to bypass the de novo synthesis window (200 – 400 °C).
- **Rapid-recall unit conversions:** 1 Nm³ air = 1.293 kg; 1 kmol gas = 22.4 Nm³ at NTP; 1 MJ/kg = 238.85 kcal/kg.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Scrubbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Scrubbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bio Scrubber** (also called a *Biological Scrubber* or *Bioscrubber*) is a hybrid air pollution control device that integrates the physical-chemical process of **absorption (scrubbing)** in a packed/venturi column with the biochemical process of **microbial degradation (biodegradation)** in a separate biological reactor. It is specifically designed to treat **odorous compounds, reduced sulphur compounds (H₂S, DMS, DMDS), nitrogenous compounds (NH₃, amines), VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds), and other water-soluble biodegradable gaseous pollutants** from industrial and municipal exhaust streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *"A bio-scrubber is a two-stage air pollution control system in which a water-soluble contaminant is first absorbed from a polluted air stream into an aqueous scrubbing liquor, and the resulting pollutant-laden liquor is subsequently regenerated in a biological reactor where aerobic microorganisms metabolize the absorbed contaminant, producing harmless end-products such as CO₂, H₂O, biomass, and mineral salts."*

It is critically different from a *biotrickling filter* (where absorption and biodegradation occur simultaneously on a packed bed) because the **absorption and regeneration steps are physically separated** into two distinct units, allowing independent optimization of each.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a bio-scrubber as a "laundry + septic tank" combination for polluted air:**
>
> Imagine a stinky gym bag (the polluted air stream). You can't easily wash the bag itself, but you can hang it in a steamy bathroom where water droplets (the scrubbing liquid) capture the stink molecules and drip down into a drain. The drain doesn't just store the dirty water — it contains a colony of helpful bacteria (like in a septic tank) that *eat* the stinky compounds and turn them into harmless substances. The cleaned water is then recycled back to capture more stink. **That is exactly how a bio-scrubber works — the column is the "steamy bathroom" and the biological reactor is the "septic tank."**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical gas residence time (in absorber):** 1 – 4 seconds
- **Liquid-to-gas ratio (L/G):** **0.5 – 5 L/m³**
- **Superficial gas velocity:** **0.5 – 2.0 m/s**
- **Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT) in bioreactor:** 15 – 60 minutes
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) in bioreactor:** **≥ 2 mg/L**
- **Operating pH range:** 6.5 – 8.5 (neutral, optimal for mesophilic bacteria)
- **Operating temperature:** **20 – 40 °C** (mesophilic range)
- **Removal efficiency for H₂S:** **95 – 99 %**
- **Removal efficiency for NH₃:** **85 – 98 %**
- **Microbial loading (MLSS in regeneration tank):** **5,000 – 15,000 mg/L**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for what a bio-scrubber removes best:** *"HANS" → **H**ydrogen sulphide, **A**mmonia, **N**itrogenous amines, **S**oluble VOCs.*

---

### 1.4 Position in the Hierarchy of Air Pollution Control Devices

Bio-scrubbers occupy a unique mid-tier position:

| Position in Hierarchy | Device | Separation Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Highest (particulate) | Cyclone, ESP, Baghouse | Mechanical / Electrostatic |
| Mid (gaseous, physical) | Wet Scrubber, Venturi Scrubber | Absorption only |
| **Bio-Scrubber (this topic)** | **Absorption + Biodegradation** | **Hybrid Physico-Biological** |
| Mid (gaseous, chemical) | Chemical Scrubber | Absorption + Reaction |
| Highest (incineration) | Thermal Oxidizer, RTO/RCO | High-temperature oxidation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use a regular chemical scrubber?** Because chemical scrubbers continuously consume expensive reagents (NaOH, NaOCl, H₂O₂) and produce a *secondary liquid waste stream* that needs disposal. Bio-scrubbers regenerate the scrubbing liquor biologically, drastically reducing chemical consumption and converting pollutants into harmless end-products in one step.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The "WHY"

The bio-scrubber exploits a single governing truth of gas-liquid mass transfer:

> **Water-soluble, biologically degradable air pollutants can be transferred from the gas phase into the liquid phase (where they become food for microbes) and then permanently destroyed by aerobic metabolism.**

This works because the **Henry's Law constant (H)** of the target pollutant is sufficiently low (high water solubility) so that a large driving concentration gradient ($\Delta C$) can be established across the gas-liquid interface inside the absorber.

### 2.2 Two-Stage Operational Architecture — The "HOW"

A bio-scrubber consists of two coupled, continuously circulating subsystems:

#### **Stage 1 — Absorption Column (The "Capture" Stage)**
- Polluted air enters at the **bottom** of a packed or venturi-type absorber.
- Aqueous scrubbing liquor (usually water + nutrients + recycled biomass) is sprayed from the **top** in **counter-current** flow.
- Pollutant molecules dissolve into the liquid film on the packing surface.
- The cleaned air exits from the top.
- The **pollutant-rich scrubbing liquor** falls into a sump and is pumped to Stage 2.

#### **Stage 2 — Biological Regeneration Tank (The "Digestion" Stage)**
- A continuously stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) or activated-sludge basin receives the loaded liquor.
- **Aerobic chemo-heterotrophic microorganisms** (e.g., *Thiobacillus*, *Pseudomonas*, *Nitrosomonas*, *Beggiatoa*) metabolize the dissolved pollutant.
- Required inputs are bubbled or mixed in: **Oxygen (air/oxygen injection), nutrients (N, P, trace elements), and pH control.**
- End-products: **CO₂, H₂O, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, new biomass (excess sludge).**
- The regenerated liquor is recycled back to the absorber top.

> [!NOTE]
> **The closed loop is the genius of the system:** Water and biomass are continuously reused; only air, a small amount of nutrients, and the discharge of excess sludge are consumed/produced.

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Mass Transfer Logic (Why it works)

For any given pollutant $A$ in the gas phase:

1. **Bulk gas diffusion** of $A$ from air bulk to gas-liquid interface.
2. **Dissolution** of $A$ at the interface (governed by **Henry's Law equilibrium**).
3. **Liquid-film diffusion** of dissolved $A$ from interface into the bulk liquid.
4. **Convective transport** of the loaded liquor to the bioreactor.
5. **Biochemical uptake + oxidation** of $A$ by microbial cells.
6. **Diffusion of O₂** from sparged air into the bulk liquid (in bioreactor).
7. **Return of regenerated liquor** to absorber.

The overall system is governed by the **two-film theory of Whitman's gas-liquid mass transfer** combined with **Monod kinetics** in the biological stage.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield equations** that the KTU board examiner expects a student to reproduce and apply for numerical or descriptive problems on bio-scrubbers.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^*_A - C_A)$ | Steady-state mass flux of pollutant A across gas-liquid interface | mol/(m²·s) | $C^*_A > C_A$ (positive driving force) |
| 2 | $C^*_A = \dfrac{y_A \cdot P}{H}$ | Equilibrium liquid concentration via Henry's Law | mol/m³ | Valid for dilute systems; H in Pa·m³/mol |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{P_A^{vap}}{x_A \cdot P_{total}}$ | Henry's Law constant (definition) | Pa·m³/mol | Temperature-dependent; T ↑ usually → H ↑ |
| 4 | $R_{A} = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot C_A \cdot X}{K_s + C_A}$ | Monod growth-rate-based substrate removal rate | kg/(m³·d) | Valid for $C_A$ not inhibitory; $K_s \ll C_A$ simplifies to zero-order |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Overall single-pass removal efficiency | % | Independent of units of $C$ (use ppm, mg/m³) |
| 6 | $EBRT = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{Q_{gas}}$ | Empty Bed Residence Time in bioreactor | s or min | $V$ = liquid volume in reactor; $Q$ = gas flow rate |
| 7 | $\tau_{liq} = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{Q_L}$ | Mean liquid (hydraulic) residence time in regeneration tank | h or d | Determines sludge age |
| 8 | $L/G = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_G}$ | Liquid-to-gas volumetric flow ratio | L/m³ | Bio-scrubber: typically 0.5 – 5 |
| 9 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V_{reactor} \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (sludge age) | d | Critical for retaining slow-growing nitrifiers (≥ 10 d) |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{column} = f \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot u_g^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{d_p}$ | Pressure drop across packed absorber (Ergun-type) | Pa | $0.5 < u_g < 2$ m/s for typical packings |
| 11 | $OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^*_{O_2} - C_{O_2})$ | Oxygen Transfer Rate into bioreactor liquid | kg O₂/h | Must satisfy BOD-based O₂ demand |
| 12 | $P_{O_2}^* = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{total}$ | Equilibrium DO concentration at sparger gas composition | mg/L | At 25 °C, $P_{O_2}^* \approx 8.3$ mg/L for air |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall #1:** Do NOT confuse **Henry's Law Constant H (Pa·m³/mol)** with the dimensionless form $H' = H / (RT)$. Always check which form the data is given in.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bio-scrubbers are deployed in the following high-impact applications:

- **Municipal Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTPs):** Treatment of digester off-gas, sludge dewatering building off-gas — primarily H₂S and mercaptans.
- **Pulp & Paper Mills:** Kraft process odorous gases (TRS — Total Reduced Sulphur).
- **Rendering Plants & Slaughterhouses:** Odour abatement of amines, fatty acids, indoles.
- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Amine off-gas, VOC biotreatment.
- **Composting Facilities & Landfills:** Biofilter/bioscrubber for NH₃, H₂S, VOCs.
- **Semiconductor & Pharma Industries:** Treatment of dilute solvent and acid vapours.
- **Food Processing (Coffee roasting, fish meal, animal feed):** Strongly odorous emissions.

> [!TIP]
> **Why are bio-scrubbers preferred over thermal oxidizers for odour control?**
> *Thermal oxidation of dilute, low-LHV (Lower Heating Value) odour streams is fuel-intensive and economically unjustifiable. Bio-scrubbers operate at ambient temperature and pressure, with energy demand only for pumps and blowers, giving them the lowest **Operating Cost per 1,000 m³ of treated air** for low-concentration biodegradable streams.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Removal Efficiency for a Two-Stage Bio-Scrubber

The bio-scrubber is two coupled reactors. Let us derive the **overall removal efficiency** when the absorber and the biological regeneration tank are at steady state.

#### Step 1 — Define the notation

- $C_{G,in}$ : Inlet gas-phase pollutant concentration (g/m³)
- $C_{G,out}$ : Outlet gas-phase pollutant concentration (g/m³)
- $C_{L,in}$ : Inlet liquid-phase pollutant concentration (g/m³)
- $C_{L,out}$ : Outlet liquid-phase pollutant concentration from absorber (g/m³)
- $Q_G$ : Volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)
- $Q_L$ : Volumetric liquid flow rate (m³/s)
- $V_R$ : Volume of biological regeneration tank (m³)
- $k$ : First-order biodegradation rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $X$ : Biomass concentration in regeneration tank (mg/L)

#### Step 2 — Steady-state mass balance on the absorber

At steady state, the **mass of pollutant absorbed from gas = mass leaving absorber in liquid**:

$$Q_G \cdot C_{G,in} - Q_G \cdot C_{G,out} = Q_L \cdot C_{L,out} - Q_L \cdot C_{L,in}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_G (C_{G,in} - C_{G,out}) = Q_L (C_{L,out} - C_{L,in})$$

#### Step 3 — Absorber efficiency (single-pass, for water-soluble pollutant)

If the inlet liquid is "clean" (recycled from bioreactor, $C_{L,in} \approx 0$):

$$C_{L,out} = \dfrac{Q_G \cdot (C_{G,in} - C_{G,out})}{Q_L}$$

Define the **gas-phase removal efficiency of the absorber alone** as:

$$\eta_{abs} = \dfrac{C_{G,in} - C_{G,out}}{C_{G,in}}$$

#### Step 4 — Bioreactor mass balance (CSTR, first-order kinetics)

A continuously-stirred regeneration tank at steady state, with first-order biological removal:

$$Q_L \cdot C_{L,out} - Q_L \cdot C_L = V_R \cdot k \cdot C_L$$

where $C_L$ is the steady-state liquid concentration in the reactor. Solving for $C_L$:

$$Q_L \cdot C_{L,out} = V_R \cdot k \cdot C_L + Q_L \cdot C_L$$

$$Q_L \cdot C_{L,out} = C_L (Q_L + V_R \cdot k)$$

$$C_L = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot C_{L,out}}{Q_L + V_R \cdot k}$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate the bioreactor single-pass liquid-phase removal

$$\eta_{bio} = \dfrac{C_{L,out} - C_L}{C_{L,out}} = \dfrac{V_R \cdot k}{Q_L + V_R \cdot k}$$

This is a classic CSTR result for first-order kinetics.

#### Step 6 — Combine into overall removal efficiency

The cleanest form, for a recycled system at steady state, is obtained by noting that the **rate of pollutant destroyed in the bioreactor = rate of pollutant removed from the gas**:

$$Q_G \cdot C_{G,in} \cdot \eta_{overall} = V_R \cdot k \cdot C_L$$

Substituting the value of $C_L$ from Step 4:

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{V_R \cdot k}{Q_G} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_{G,in}}$$

After eliminating $C_{L,out}$ via the absorber balance and assuming $C_{L,in} \to 0$ (perfect regeneration), the final design equation used in practice is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R}\cdot \dfrac{H \cdot a_{int}}{Q_G}\right]}$$

where $H$ is the Henry's constant (dimensionless form) and $a_{int}$ is the gas-liquid interfacial area in the absorber (m²).

---

### 3.2 Illustrative Numerical Problem (Worked Example)

> **Problem:**
> A bio-scrubber treats a polluted air stream from a WWTP. Given:
> - $Q_G = 1.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
> - $Q_L = 2.0 \; \text{L/s} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
> - $C_{G,in} = 50 \; \text{ppmv}$ of H₂S
> - Henry's constant for H₂S (dimensionless): $H' = 0.10$
> - Biodegradation rate constant in regeneration tank: $k = 0.5 \; \text{min}^{-1} = 0.00833 \; \text{s}^{-1}$
> - Effective interfacial area in absorber: $a_{int} = 500 \; \text{m}^2$
>
> Calculate the required volume of the biological regeneration tank to achieve **95 % overall H₂S removal**.

#### Step 1 — Convert inlet concentration to molar

$$C_{G,in} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{P}{RT} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{101325}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$C_{G,in} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 40.87 = 2.04 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the overall efficiency equation

$$\eta = 0.95 = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R}\cdot \dfrac{H' \cdot a_{int}}{Q_G}\right]$$

Let $X = \dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R}\cdot \dfrac{H' \cdot a_{int}}{Q_G}$

Then $\exp(-X) = 0.05 \Rightarrow X = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996$

#### Step 3 — Solve for the k·V_R term

$$X = 2.996 = \dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R}\cdot \dfrac{0.10 \times 500}{1.0}$$

$$2.996 = \dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R}\cdot 50$$

$$\dfrac{k \cdot V_R}{Q_L + k \cdot V_R} = \dfrac{2.996}{50} = 0.0599$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for V_R

$$k \cdot V_R = 0.0599 \cdot (Q_L + k \cdot V_R)$$

$$k \cdot V_R = 0.0599 \cdot Q_L + 0.0599 \cdot k \cdot V_R$$

$$k \cdot V_R (1 - 0.0599) = 0.0599 \cdot Q_L$$

$$k \cdot V_R (0.9401) = 0.0599 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$k \cdot V_R = \dfrac{1.198 \times 10^{-4}}{0.9401} = 1.274 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for V_R

$$V_R = \dfrac{1.274 \times 10^{-4}}{0.00833} = 0.0153 \; \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_R \approx 15.3 \; \text{L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For these operating conditions, only a small regeneration tank (~15 L for treating 1 m³/s of gas) is needed. However, in practice, designers choose $V_R$ that gives a **hydraulic residence time $\tau_{liq} = V_R / Q_L$** of at least **15 – 30 minutes** to ensure robust biological activity and shock-load tolerance.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bio-scrubber vs. Biotrickling Filter vs. Biofilter

| Parameter | Bio-scrubber | Biotrickling Filter | Biofilter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Location of biodegradation | Separate regeneration tank | On packing biofilm | On packed organic medium (compost/wood) |
| Biomass location | Suspended in liquid | Attached (immobilized) on packing | Attached on medium particles |
| Typical packing | Pall rings, Intalox saddles | Structured plastic, lava rock, polyurethane foam | Compost, peat, wood chips, soil |
| L/G ratio | 0.5 – 5 L/m³ | 1 – 10 L/m³ | 0 (no continuous liquid flow) |
| Nutrient dosing | Required (in liquid) | Required (in trickling liquid) | Released by medium itself |
| pH control | Easy (in tank) | Easy (in trickling liquid) | Difficult (medium-buffered) |
| Acidic products (H₂SO₄) handling | Easy (tank) | Moderate (pH drops in biofilm) | Difficult (medium acidification) |
| Biomass control | Wasted as excess sludge | Periodic backwash | Medium replacement every 2–5 years |
| Footprint | Larger (2 units) | Smaller (1 unit) | Smallest (1 unit) |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate | Low |
| Operating cost | Low (no chemicals) | Low | Moderate (medium replacement) |
| Best suited for | High H₂S, NH₃; rapidly degradable compounds | Hydrophobic VOCs; chlorinated compounds | Low concentration, slowly varying loads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** A bio-scrubber is preferred when **acidic end-products (like H₂SO₄ from H₂S oxidation)** would damage a single-bed biofilter/biotrickling filter. The separate, well-buffered regeneration tank handles this problem elegantly.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a bio-scrubber. List any two advantages it has over a chemical scrubber.**

**Model Answer:**
A bio-scrubber is a two-stage air pollution control device in which a water-soluble biodegradable gaseous pollutant is first absorbed from the polluted air into a scrubbing liquor in an absorption column, and the pollutant-laden liquor is then regenerated in a separate biological reactor where aerobic microorganisms oxidize the absorbed pollutant into harmless end-products such as CO₂, H₂O, biomass, and mineral salts.

**Two advantages over a chemical scrubber:**
1. **No consumption of expensive reagents** (NaOH, NaOCl, H₂SO₄) — the regeneration is biological, hence operating cost is very low.
2. **No secondary chemical sludge** — the pollutant is converted to CO₂, H₂O, and a small amount of biological sludge, eliminating hazardous liquid waste disposal problems.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Mention the four essential operating conditions required in the biological regeneration tank of a bio-scrubber for effective pollutant degradation.**

**Model Answer:**
The four essential conditions in the regeneration tank are:
1. **Adequate Dissolved Oxygen (DO ≥ 2 mg/L):** maintained by diffused aeration or mechanical aerators to support aerobic metabolism.
2. **Neutral pH (6.5 – 8.5):** maintained by automated dosing of acid/base, since pH drift (especially acidification from H₂SO₄ production) inhibits microbial activity.
3. **Sufficient nutrients (Nitrogen, Phosphorus, trace elements):** dosed to maintain the proper C:N:P ratio (~100:5:1) for microbial growth.
4. **Adequate hydraulic and sludge residence time:** $\tau_{liq} \geq 15 - 30$ min and $\theta_c \geq 10$ days to retain a healthy, acclimatized microbial population.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the working principle and construction of a bio-scrubber used for odour control. Identify the dominant microbial groups involved.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A bio-scrubber handles an H₂S-laden air stream from a WWTP. The inlet H₂S concentration is 80 ppmv and the desired outlet concentration is 2 ppmv. If the gas flow rate is 0.5 m³/s and the Henry's constant (dimensionless) for H₂S is 0.10, calculate (i) the overall removal efficiency, and (ii) the steady-state mass of H₂S absorbed per second in the absorber. (Assume standard conditions: T = 25 °C, P = 1 atm, M(H₂S) = 34 g/mol)

---

##### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Working Principle:**
A bio-scrubber operates on the two-stage principle of **physical absorption followed by biological regeneration**. The system comprises two coupled units: an absorption column and a biological regeneration tank, with the scrubbing liquor continuously recirculating between them.

**Construction (refer to the schematic in Section 4.3):**
- **Absorption Column:** A vertical cylindrical vessel (PVC, FRP, or SS) packed with Pall rings, Intalox saddles, or structured packing. Polluted air enters at the bottom through a distributor, while scrubbing liquor is sprayed from the top through nozzles in **counter-current** contact. A mist eliminator at the top captures entrained droplets. A sump at the bottom collects the loaded liquor.
- **Biological Regeneration Tank:** A continuously stirred aerobic tank equipped with submerged diffused aerators, dissolved oxygen sensors, pH probes, nutrient dosing pumps, and a clarifier for biomass separation and recycle.
- **Recirculation System:** Pumps, piping, flow meters, and control valves for continuous liquor circulation.
- **Instrumentation:** DO meter, pH controller, flow transmitters, and level switches.

**Working Steps:**
1. Polluted air (containing H₂S, NH₃, etc.) enters the column from the bottom.
2. Scrubbing liquor flows downward, absorbing the water-soluble pollutants across the gas-liquid interface.
3. Cleaned air exits the top of the column.
4. The pollutant-loaded liquor is pumped to the regeneration tank.
5. Aerobic microorganisms (suspended as activated sludge) metabolize the dissolved pollutant using the supplied O₂.
6. End-products (CO₂, H₂SO₄ from H₂S, NH₄⁺ from amines, new biomass) are produced.
7. The regenerated liquor overflows the clarifier and is recycled to the column top.

**Dominant Microbial Groups:**
- **Sulphur-oxidizing bacteria:** *Thiobacillus thioparus*, *Thiobacillus denitrificans*, *Beggiatoa* — oxidize H₂S, mercaptans, and dimethyl sulphide to sulphate.
- **Nitrifying bacteria:** *Nitrosomonas* (NH₃ → NO₂⁻) and *Nitrobacter* (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻) — oxidize ammonia and amines.
- **Heterotrophic VOC-degraders:** *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes*, *Arthrobacter* — degrade a wide range of VOCs as carbon and energy source.

---

##### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- $C_{G,in} = 80 \; \text{ppmv}$
- $C_{G,out} = 2 \; \text{ppmv}$
- $Q_G = 0.5 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $T = 298 \; \text{K}, P = 101325 \; \text{Pa}$
- $M_{H_2S} = 34 \; \text{g/mol}$

**(i) Overall removal efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{G,in} - C_{G,out}}{C_{G,in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{80 - 2}{80} \times 100\% = \dfrac{78}{80} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 97.5\%}$$

**(ii) Mass of H₂S absorbed per second:**

First, convert ppmv to mol/m³ using ideal gas law:

$$C_{G,in} = y \cdot \dfrac{P}{RT} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{101325}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$C_{G,in} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 40.87 = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

Similarly:

$$C_{G,out} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 40.87 = 8.17 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

Molar flow rate absorbed:

$$\Delta \dot{n} = Q_G \cdot (C_{G,in} - C_{G,out})$$

$$\Delta \dot{n} = 0.5 \times (3.27 \times 10^{-3} - 8.17 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\Delta \dot{n} = 0.5 \times 3.19 \times 10^{-3} = 1.59 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/s}$$

Convert to mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \Delta \dot{n} \times M_{H_2S} = 1.59 \times 10^{-3} \times 34$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 0.054 \; \text{g/s} = 194 \; \text{g/h} \approx 4.66 \; \text{kg/day}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between a bio-scrubber, a biotrickling filter, and a biofilter. For each, give one specific application where it is the most suitable technology.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the following in a bio-scrubber's design and operation: (i) Henry's Law Constant of the target pollutant, (ii) Liquid-to-Gas (L/G) ratio, (iii) Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT/θc), (iv) Dissolved Oxygen (DO) concentration. State one design consequence of deviating from the optimum for each.

---

##### **Model Solution to B(a):**

| Aspect | Bio-scrubber | Biotrickling Filter | Biofilter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biodegradation location** | Separate suspended-growth regeneration tank | Attached-growth biofilm on packing in same column | Attached-growth on organic medium (compost) in same bed |
| **Liquid flow** | Continuously recirculated between two units | Continuously trickled over the bed | None (moisture maintained by humidification of inlet air) |
| **pH control** | Easy (in the tank) | Easy (in trickling liquid) | Difficult (medium-buffered) |
| **Best application** | WWTP digester off-gas (high H₂S, low pH issue) | VOC-laden air from chemical/pharma industry (hydrophobic compounds) | Municipal composting facility off-gas (low concentration, slowly varying) |

---

##### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**(i) Henry's Law Constant (H):**
- **Role:** Determines the equilibrium partitioning of the pollutant between gas and liquid phases. It dictates the **maximum achievable liquid concentration** for a given gas concentration: $C^*_L = y \cdot P / H$.
- **Design consequence of deviation:** If the target pollutant has a **high H (low water solubility, e.g., toluene)**, very little transfers into the water, drastically reducing removal efficiency. For such hydrophobic VOCs, a biotrickling filter is preferred.

**(ii) Liquid-to-Gas (L/G) Ratio:**
- **Role:** Determines the **driving force for absorption** and the rate of pollutant transfer per unit gas volume. Higher L/G → higher $C_L$ gradient → better absorption, but also higher pumping cost.
- **Design consequence of deviation:** Too low L/G → incomplete absorption, low removal efficiency. Too high L/G → unnecessary pumping energy, possible flooding of the packed column, and washout of biomass from the regeneration tank.

**(iii) Mean Cell Residence Time (θc):**
- **Role:** Determines the **average age of microbial cells** in the regeneration tank. Slow-growing autotrophs (e.g., nitrifiers, *Nitrosomonas*) require $\theta_c \geq 10$ days to be retained; otherwise they wash out.
- **Design consequence of deviation:** If $\theta_c$ is too low, the slow-growing but essential microorganisms are washed out of the system, leading to **complete loss of biodegradation capacity** and process failure.

**(iv) Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Concentration:**
- **Role:** Terminal electron acceptor for aerobic respiration; required for the oxidation of the absorbed pollutant (e.g., H₂S → SO₄²⁻ requires 2 mol O₂ per mol H₂S).
- **Design consequence of deviation:** If DO < 1 mg/L, the reactor becomes oxygen-limited, pollutant accumulates, and anaerobic conditions produce foul intermediates (e.g., H₂S from SO₄²⁻ reduction). Excessively high DO (> 6 mg/L) wastes aeration energy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on Bio-Scrubber Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "bio-scrubber" with "biofilter":** A bio-scrubber has **two separate units**; a biofilter has a single packed bed. If you draw them the same in the exam, you will lose at least 3 marks on a 7-mark sketch question.
> 2. **Forgetting the bioreactor's clarifier/sludge recycle line:** Many students draw the regeneration tank without showing the **biomass separation and recycle**. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Using the wrong Henry's Law form:** Mixing up the dimensional and dimensionless H leads to a factor of $RT$ error in numerical problems — a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer:** Always state units explicitly (e.g., "V_R = 15.3 L", not "V_R = 15.3"). A unitless answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Failing to mention microbial names:** Examiners expect at least 2–3 specific microbial genera in descriptive answers (*Thiobacillus*, *Pseudomonas*, *Nitrosomonas*).
> 6. **Not stating the operating pH and DO range:** These are "must-mention" parameters for any bio-scrubber descriptive question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section as your final 5-minute revision before the exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Bio-scrubber = **Absorption Column + Biological Regeneration Tank** (two separate units, connected by a recirculating liquid loop).
- ✅ **Working principle:** Counter-current absorption followed by **aerobic microbial oxidation** of the dissolved pollutant.
- ✅ **Dominant equation:** Henry's Law: $C^*_L = y \cdot P / H$ controls absorption; **Monod kinetics** control biodegradation in the tank.
- ✅ **Best at removing:** **H₂S, NH₃, mercaptans, amines, water-soluble VOCs** — especially when acidic end-products are produced.
- ✅ **Key operating parameters:** DO ≥ 2 mg/L, pH 6.5 – 8.5, T = 20 – 40 °C, L/G = 0.5 – 5 L/m³, $\tau_{liq}$ ≥ 15 min, $\theta_c$ ≥ 10 d.
- ✅ **Key microbes:** *Thiobacillus* (sulphur oxidation), *Nitrosomonas* + *Nitrobacter* (nitrification), *Pseudomonas* (VOC degradation).
- ✅ **End-products:** CO₂ + H₂O + SO₄²⁻/NO₃⁻ + excess biological sludge.
- ✅ **Difference from biotrickling filter:** Bio-scrubber has *separate* units; biotrickling filter has *integrated* biofilm on packing with continuous trickling.
- ✅ **Difference from biofilter:** Biofilter uses organic medium (compost/wood) and *no continuous liquid*; bio-scrubber uses *synthetic packing + recirculated water*.
- ✅ **Advantages:** No reagent consumption, low operating cost, ambient T/P operation, no secondary chemical waste.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Large footprint, requires nutrient dosing, sensitive to shock loads, slow biological kinetics.
- ✅ **Applications:** WWTP sludge handling, pulp & paper Kraft process, rendering plants, composting, food processing.
- ✅ **Formulae to memorize:** $N_A = k_L a (C^* - C)$, $\eta = 1 - \exp(-X)$, $\theta_c = VX/(Q_w X_w)$, $EBRT = V/Q$.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite 14-marker:** "Explain with sketch + numerical" — always include a labelled diagram and 3–4 numerical steps with units.

---

> **END OF NOTES — BIO SCRUBBERS (OECHT726, Module 4)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Venturi Scrubber

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Venturi Scrubber is a high-energy wet scrubbing device used for the control of particulate matter and gaseous pollutants from industrial gas streams. It operates on the principle of atomization of scrubbing liquid using the kinetic energy of a high-velocity gas stream passing through a converging–throat–diverging section, where intimate gas-liquid contact causes inertial impaction and capture of contaminants.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a Venturi scrubber like a garden hose nozzle connected to a vacuum cleaner. When you squeeze the hose (converging section), water shoots out faster (high velocity). If we spray dirty air through this narrow throat along with water droplets, the fast-moving air smashes dust particles into water droplets — just like throwing dirt at a fast-spinning wet roller. The dust sticks to the water and gets washed out.

- **Standard Operating Velocity in Throat:** **40 – 150 m/s**
- **Pressure Drop Range:** **50 – 250 cm of H₂O (≈ 5 – 25 kPa)**
- **Collection Efficiency:** **70% – 99%+** for fine particulates (0.5 – 5 µm)
- **Liquid-to-Gas Ratio (L/G):** **0.5 – 5 L/m³**

---

## 1.2 Biofilter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Biofilter is an air pollution control device that uses a moist, biologically active medium (such as compost, peat, wood chips, or synthetic media) to biologically degrade odorous compounds and Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) from contaminated air streams into harmless end-products like CO₂, water, and biomass through microbial metabolism (bio-oxidation).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a giant earth-filled bed through which smelly air is slowly pumped. Microorganisms living in the moist soil "eat" the smell molecules (like H₂S, toluene, benzene) and convert them into carbon dioxide and water — exactly like how a forest soil naturally absorbs odors. A biofilter mimics nature's composting action in an engineered bed.

- **Typical Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT):** **15 – 60 seconds**
- **Microbial Operating Temperature:** **20 – 45 °C (mesophilic range)**
- **Removal Efficiency:** **90% – 99%** for biodegradable VOCs
- **pH Range of Biofilter Media:** **6.5 – 8.0**

---

## 1.3 Sources, Types and Control of Indoor Air Pollutants

> [!NOTE]
> **Indoor Air Pollution Definition:** Indoor air pollutants refer to any chemical, biological, or physical contaminant present inside enclosed spaces (homes, offices, schools) at concentrations that may harm health, comfort, or work efficiency.

**Sources of Indoor Pollutants:**
1. **Combustion sources** — gas stoves, woodstoves, tobacco smoke (CO, NO₂, PM, formaldehyde).
2. **Building materials** — asbestos, pressed wood, paints (VOCs, formaldehyde, radon).
3. **Office equipment** — copiers, printers (ozone, toner dust).
4. **HVAC systems** — microbial growth, dust accumulation.
5. **Cleaning products & biological sources** — mold, pet dander, pollen.

**Control Hierarchy:**
- **Source Control:** Eliminate or substitute pollutant-emitting materials.
- **Ventilation Control:** Increase fresh air supply.
- **Air Cleaning:** Use filters, scrubbers, or photocatalytic oxidation.

---

## 1.4 Sick Building Syndrome (SBS) — Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Sick Building Syndrome refers to a set of non-specific symptoms reported by occupants of a building that are linked to time spent inside the building but cannot be identified with a specific cause or illness. It is distinct from **Building-Related Illness (BRI)**, which has identifiable symptoms and diagnosable causes.

**Types of SBS:**
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Type I – Mucous Membrane Irritation** | Eye, nose, and throat irritation |
| **Type II – Neurological Symptoms** | Headaches, fatigue, dizziness |
| **Type III – Respiratory Effects** | Cough, wheezing, shortness of breath |
| **Type IV – Dermal Symptoms** | Skin rashes, dryness, itching |

---

## 1.5 HVAC System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) refers to the integrated technology used to provide indoor environmental comfort through thermal control, fresh air circulation, humidity regulation, and air filtration in buildings.

**Intuition:** Think of HVAC as the "lungs" of a building — it breathes in fresh air, filters it, conditions its temperature and humidity, and exhales clean, comfortable air into occupied zones.

**Key HVAC Parameters:**
- **Air Changes per Hour (ACH):** **4 – 8 ACH** for offices (ASHRAE Standard 62.1)
- **Outdoor Air Supply:** **8.5 L/s per person** minimum
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** **30% – 60%**
- **Filtration Standard:** **MERV 8 to MERV 13**

---

## 1.6 Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Issues & Impacts

> [!NOTE]
> **IAQ Definition:** IAQ refers to the air quality inside and around buildings, especially as it relates to the health and comfort of building occupants. It is determined by the concentration of pollutants, temperature, humidity, and ventilation effectiveness.

**Major IAQ Issues:**
- Insufficient ventilation (low ACH)
- Microbial contamination (Legionella, mold)
- Chemical off-gassing (formaldehyde, benzene)
- Particulate accumulation (PM2.5, PM10)
- CO₂ buildup from human respiration (> 1000 ppm causes drowsiness)

**Occupant Health Impacts:**
- Reduced cognitive performance (up to **50% reduction** at CO₂ > 1000 ppm — Harvard T.H. Chan study).
- Increased sick leave days.
- Allergic and asthmatic reactions.
- "Stuffy room" syndrome and fatigue.

---

## 1.7 Developing an IAQ Profile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IAQ Profile Definition:** A systematic characterization of all indoor air quality parameters in a building, including pollutant sources, concentrations, occupant symptoms, ventilation rates, and HVAC operating conditions — used as a baseline for diagnosis and management.

**Steps to Develop an IAQ Profile:**
1. Building walkthrough inspection.
2. Occupant survey (symptom mapping).
3. HVAC system audit (filter condition, coil cleanliness).
4. Baseline pollutant measurements (CO₂, CO, VOCs, PM).
5. Ventilation rate assessment.
6. Comparison against ASHRAE / NAAQS standards.

---

## 1.8 Diagnosing IAQ Problems

**Diagnostic Toolkit:**
- **Real-time monitors:** CO₂, CO, TVOC, PM sensors.
- **IAQ probes:** Multi-gas analyzers.
- **Microbial sampling:** Air samplers, swab tests.
- **Tracer gas test (SF₆):** Quantifies ventilation effectiveness.

**Diagnostic Logic Tree:**
1. Are pollutant levels elevated? → Yes/No
2. Is ventilation rate below ASHRAE minimum? → Yes/No
3. Are occupants reporting symptoms? → Yes/No
4. Correlate symptom clusters with pollutant hotspots.

---

## 1.9 IAQ Control, Quantification & Measurement

**Control Strategies:**
- **Ventilation improvement** (demand-controlled ventilation — DCV).
- **Source elimination** (low-VOC materials, smoking ban).
- **Air cleaning** (HEPA filtration, activated carbon, UVGI).
- **HVAC maintenance** (coil cleaning, filter replacement).

**Quantification Parameters:**
| Parameter | Unit | Standard Limit |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | ppm | < 1000 |
| CO | ppm | < 9 (8-h avg) |
| PM2.5 | µg/m³ | < 35 (24-h) |
| TVOC | ppb | < 500 |
| Formaldehyde | ppb | < 100 |
| Radon | Bq/m³ | < 200 (WHO) |
| ACH | per hour | 4–8 |
